fields.note: Driver-match ranking priority (lower numeric value = better match). Example live value: 0xfb2006. Forensic signal: flag unexpectedly-low rank — detects drive-by driver-swap where a forced lower-rank driver was installed. Added per repnz ETW manifest.

fields.name: MatchingDeviceId
fields.note: The device-ID string the INF matched against — may be more generic than DeviceInstanceId (e.g. USB\VID_0781&PID_5567 without the instance suffix). Distinguishes exact-match driver binding vs fallback/generic binding. Added per repnz ETW manifest.

fields.name: OutrankedDrivers
fields.note: List of drivers that were considered but superseded by the selected one. Forensic signal: anomalous OutrankedDrivers population can indicate manual driver selection or installer-package injection. Added per repnz ETW manifest.

fields.name: FirmwareDate
fields.note: Optional — populated only for firmware-update-capable devices via FIRMWARE_ID. Absent on simple storage / HID devices. (Win11-era behavior unverified against a newer manifest — 17134-era confirmed.)

fields.name: FirmwareVersion
fields.note: Optional — firmware version string when the device exposes a Firmware ID. (Win11-era behavior unverified against a newer manifest.)

fields.name: FirmwareRevision
fields.note: Optional — firmware revision identifier. Correlates with IOCTL_STORAGE_QUERY_PROPERTY → StorageDeviceIdProperty on the same device. (Win11-era behavior unverified against a newer manifest.)

fields.name: DeviceUpdated
fields.note: True when the device's driver was UPDATED during this configuration (vs first-time configuration). Critical for distinguishing FIRST-CONNECT events from driver-update reconfigurations — first connect is more precisely 'first 400 with DeviceUpdated=false' than 'first 400 for a given DeviceInstanceId' alone. Added per repnz ETW manifest.

fields.name: Status
fields.note: Configuration completion status (NTSTATUS). 0x0 = success; non-zero = kernel-level configuration error. Replaces the prior DeviceStatus / ProblemStatus entries that were incorrectly conflated from event 410. Added per repnz ETW manifest.

fields.name: ParentDeviceInstanceId
fields.note: Parent device in the PnP tree (hub or controller). For USBSTOR devices, ParentDeviceInstanceId resolves to the USB\ROOT_HUB / hub instance above — essential for reconstructing the physical port path.

fields.name: TimeCreated
fields.note: Kernel-timestamped at configuration. First 400 WITH DeviceUpdated=false for a given DeviceInstanceId = first-ever device configuration on this host (the DeviceUpdated flag disambiguates first-connect from driver-update reconfiguration — refined 2026-04-23 per repnz ETW manifest).

nodes.name: KernelPnP-410
nodes.title-description: Device started

fields.name: DeviceInstanceId
fields.note: PnP device instance path — same canonical identifier as 400, USBSTOR, setupapi.dev.log, Security-6416.

fields.name: ServiceName
fields.note: Function driver that handled IRP_MN_START_DEVICE (e.g. USBSTOR, disk). This is the driver that brought the device to the Started state — joins to Services registry for the ImagePath.

fields.name: DriverName
fields.note: INF binding used for the successful start. Matches the DriverName in the preceding KernelPnP-400.

fields.name: Problem
fields.note: 0 on successful start. CM_PROB_FAILED_START (10) on IRP failure — the device enumerated and configured but couldn't start. Classic 'Code 10' in Device Manager.

fields.name: ProblemStatus
fields.note: NTSTATUS of the failing START IRP. 0x00000000 on success; driver-specific otherwise.

fields.name: ParentDeviceInstanceId
fields.note: Parent device in the PnP tree. For USBSTOR devices, the USB hub the device is attached through.

fields.name: TimeCreated
fields.note: Written when the function driver returns from START. 400 without a following 410 for the same DeviceInstanceId = device enumerated but never came online (bad driver match, wrong bus, failed firmware).

nodes.name: NetworkProfile-10000
nodes.title-description: Network connection

fields.name: Name
fields.note: network profile name — SSID for WiFi, 'Network N' for Ethernet, domain name for domain profiles

fields.name: Category
fields.note: 0=Public, 1=Private, 2=Domain — firewall scope driver

nodes.name: PartitionDiagnostic-1006
nodes.display-title: winevtx - "Partition-Diagnostic — disk and partition table capture"
nodes.title-description: Partition-Diagnostic — disk and partition table capture

fields.name: time-created
fields.note: kernel-timestamped at the moment of disk enumeration
fields.update-rule: set-at-event-emission

fields.name: capacity
fields.note: device capacity in bytes — distinguishing factor between physically similar devices

fields.name: parent-id
fields.note: parent device instance path; resolves back to USBSTOR instance hierarchy

fields.name: registry-id
fields.note: maps directly to the USBSTOR <instance-id> for this device

fields.name: adapter-id
fields.note: storage adapter GUID; identifies the host controller the device attached through

fields.name: partition-style
fields.note: 0 = MBR, 1 = GPT, 2 = RAW

fields.name: mbr-disk-signature
fields.note: present when partition-style == MBR; 4-byte disk signature matching MountedDevices binding-data MBR-case encoding

fields.name: mbr-raw
fields.encoding: raw first 512 bytes of the device's Master Boot Record (hex-encoded in the evtx). Disk signature at offset 0x1b8 (4 bytes LE), Master Partition Table follows.
fields.note: Full MBR dump — allows independent recomputation of disk signature and partition layout even if the signature field is stripped.

fields.name: vbr0-raw
fields.encoding: raw Volume Boot Record of device's 1st partition (hex-encoded). Filesystem-specific VSN offset: NTFS 0x48, FAT32 0x43, exFAT 0x64 (all 4 bytes LE). Identify FS via jump-instruction + OEM-ID bytes at offset 0.
fields.note: Vasilaras / Dragonas / Katsoulis 2021 (DFIR Review, doi 10.21428/b0ac9c28.de3816b0): Vbr0 carries the VSN of the device's FIRST partition AT CONNECTION TIME. Persists even after user reformats — provides historical VSN attribution.

fields.name: vbr1-raw
fields.encoding: raw VBR of device's 2nd partition (same offset rules as Vbr0)
fields.note: Second VSN if device has 2+ partitions. Only present on multi-partition MBR devices.

fields.name: vbr2-raw
fields.encoding: raw VBR of device's 3rd partition (same offset rules as Vbr0)
fields.note: Third VSN. Vbr3+ fields are NOT populated even on 4+ partition devices — the event schema is capped at three VBRs.

fields.name: gpt-disk-id
fields.note: present when partition-style == GPT; disk-level GUID

fields.name: gpt-partition-ids
fields.note: GPT partition GUIDs; for GPT-formatted removables, one of these matches the VolumeGUID used by MountPoints2 and the 16-byte tail of MountedDevices' Format-3 binding-data (after the 'DMIO:ID:' prefix)

fields.name: vbr0
fields.note: hash of the first 512 bytes of the VBR — can distinguish two devices that share a disk signature (rare but possible on cloned USBs)

fields.name: user-sid
fields.note: user context at mount time — this is the single biggest reason this event is more forensically powerful than USBSTOR alone

nodes.name: PowerShell-4104
nodes.title-description: PowerShell Script Block Logging

fields.name: script-block-text
fields.note: FULL PowerShell script block source — decoded even if the original invocation was base64-encoded

fields.name: path
fields.note: source file path when available (script file); blank for ad-hoc commands

fields.name: message-number
fields.note: large script blocks split across multiple 4104 events; reassemble by ScriptBlockId

nodes.name: Security-1102
nodes.title-description: The audit log was cleared

fields.name: subject-user-sid
fields.note: SID of the account that cleared the Security log — almost always an admin

nodes.name: Security-4624
nodes.display-title: winevtx - "An account was successfully logged on"
nodes.title-description: An account was successfully logged on

fields.name: subject-user-sid
fields.note: typically SYSTEM (S-1-5-18) for interactive logons; represents the authority that requested the logon

fields.name: subject-user-name
fields.note: account name of the subject — usually the machine account ending in $ or SYSTEM

fields.name: subject-domain-name
fields.note: domain / workgroup / computer name of the subject

fields.name: subject-logon-id
fields.note: logon ID of the session that REPORTED the logon (usually 0x3e7 for SYSTEM); distinct from TargetLogonId which is the session being created

fields.name: target-user-sid
fields.note: the account being logged on — the forensic target

fields.name: target-logon-id
fields.note: THE LUID — unique session identifier assigned by LSASS at logon. Appears as SubjectLogonId on every in-session 4688/4663/4657/4672/5140, and as TargetLogonId on the matching 4634/4647 logoff. The single most important session-scope join key in Security.evtx.

fields.name: logon-type
fields.note: Per MS Learn documentation, 13 values: 0=System, 2=interactive, 3=network, 4=batch, 5=service, 7=unlock, 8=networkCleartext, 9=newCredentials, 10=remoteInteractive(RDP), 11=cachedInteractive, 12=cachedRemoteInteractive (not widely observed in practice — documented Win8+), 13=cachedUnlock. Types 0, 12, and 13 are rarely-surfaced in day-to-day analysis but worth recognizing for edge-case triage.

fields.name: logon-process-name
fields.note: e.g., 'User32', 'Advapi', 'NtLmSsp', 'Kerberos'

fields.name: authentication-package
fields.note: NTLM / Kerberos / Negotiate — the auth protocol used

fields.name: logon-guid
fields.note: Kerberos correlation key — non-zero only for Kerberos logons. Ties 4624 to related 4769 (TGS) / 4648 (explicit creds) / 4964 (special group) events for the same logon. All zeros (00000000-...) when NTLM or local. Caveat per UWS: LogonGuid↔4769 correlation is frequently unreliable in practice — LogonGuid may be all-zero on 4624 even when Kerberos was used, and 4769 events may not always carry a matching GUID. Fall back to correlating via TargetUserName + workstation + time-proximity when GUID-based correlation fails.

fields.name: lm-package-name
fields.note: NTLM version: 'NTLM V1' / 'NTLM V2' / 'LM' (deprecated). Populated only when AuthenticationPackageName == NTLM. '-' for Kerberos. NTLM V1 in a modern environment is a strong anomaly — hunting signal for downgrade attacks.

fields.name: key-length
fields.note: NTLM session key length (128 bits typical). Always 0 for Kerberos — don't interpret as weak crypto.

fields.name: transmitted-services
fields.note: Kerberos S4U (constrained/protocol-transition) delegation chain. '-' when no delegation. Populated entries indicate the logon traversed one or more delegation hops — critical signal for analyzing Kerberoasting / S4U2Self / S4U2Proxy abuse.

fields.name: impersonation-level
fields.note: Anonymous / Identification / Impersonation / Delegation. Delegation on a non-network logon is anomalous — examine the 4648 that spawned the token. (Added in v1 / Win8+.)

fields.name: restricted-admin-mode
fields.note: Populated only on LogonType 10 (RemoteInteractive / RDP). 'Yes' means RDP used Restricted Admin mode (no credential materialization on target) — a hunting signal for either admin defense hygiene OR RDP-RestrictedAdmin pass-the-hash technique. Added v2 / Win10+.

fields.name: target-outbound-user-name
fields.note: 'Network Account Name' — populated on LogonType 9 (NewCredentials = runas /netonly). Shows the alternate credential set the spawned process will use for outbound network auth. v2 / Win10+.

fields.name: target-outbound-domain-name
fields.note: domain for target-outbound-user-name. v2 / Win10+.

fields.name: virtual-account
fields.note: 'Yes' indicates the logon is for a Managed Service Account (MSA/gMSA) or virtual service account. v2 / Win10+.

fields.name: target-linked-logon-id
fields.note: LUID of the paired logon session for UAC split-token scenarios (standard + elevated). Same interactive user produces two 4624s — their TargetLogonIds reference each other via TargetLinkedLogonId. v2 / Win10+.

fields.name: workstation-name
fields.note: NetBIOS name of the host the logon came from; blank for local interactive

fields.name: source-ip
fields.note: source IP for network/RDP logons; '-' for local

fields.name: process-id
fields.note: PID of the process that requested the logon. Cross-check with 4688.NewProcessId for the logon-requesting process.

fields.name: process-name
fields.note: process that initiated the logon (lsass.exe, winlogon.exe, svchost.exe, etc.)

fields.name: elevated-token
fields.note: whether the session is running with full admin token — UAC elevation signal

fields.name: remote-credential-guard
fields.note: Windows Server 2025 added a Remote Credential Guard field — 'Yes' indicates the logon leveraged RCG to keep credentials on the originating host (no token materialization at the target). Normally '-' on pre-2025. Presence of 'Yes' on a sensitive target is a defense-posture signal; absence where policy required it is an investigation signal.

nodes.name: Security-4625
nodes.display-title: winevtx - "An account failed to log on"
nodes.title-description: An account failed to log on

fields.name: subject-user-sid
fields.note: SID reporting the failure — typically SYSTEM (S-1-5-18) for interactive / network logons, or the account of the process attempting auth for service logons

fields.name: subject-user-name
fields.note: usually the machine account ($) or SYSTEM

fields.name: subject-logon-id
fields.note: logon ID of the REPORTING session (0x3e7 for SYSTEM)

fields.name: target-user-sid
fields.note: 'S-1-0-0' (null SID) when the account doesn't exist — distinguishes typo from valid-user-wrong-password

fields.name: failure-reason
fields.note: %%2304=unknown user/bad password, %%2307=account-locked, %%2310=password-expired, %%2311=logon-type-not-allowed, etc.

fields.name: status
fields.note: NTSTATUS — 0xC000006D=generic bad-user-or-pass, 0xC0000064=user-not-found, 0xC000006A=wrong-password-for-valid-user, 0xC0000234=locked-out

fields.name: sub-status
fields.note: more specific than Status; often the actionable field

fields.name: logon-type
fields.note: same values as 4624

fields.name: logon-process-name
fields.note: e.g., 'User32', 'Advapi', 'NtLmSsp', 'Kerberos', 'Seclogo'

fields.name: authentication-package
fields.note: NTLM / Kerberos / Negotiate. Same spray/brute-force typically hits NTLM in one bucket and Kerberos in another — useful split.

fields.name: transmitted-services
fields.note: Kerberos S4U transit list. '-' when no delegation.

fields.name: lm-package-name
fields.note: NTLM version: 'NTLM V1' / 'NTLM V2' / 'LM'. '-' unless AuthenticationPackageName == NTLM. NTLM V1 failures in a modern environment = likely downgrade / legacy-protocol probing.

fields.name: key-length
fields.note: NTLM session key length. 0 for Kerberos or Negotiate-Kerberos.

fields.name: process-id
fields.note: PID of the process attempting the logon on behalf of the target user

fields.name: process-name
fields.note: the process that attempted the logon on behalf of the user

nodes.name: Security-4634
nodes.title-description: An account was logged off

fields.name: target-logon-id
fields.note: same LogonId value that appeared in the corresponding 4624 logon event — use as session pairing key

fields.name: logon-type
fields.note: logon type of the session being ended; matches the type from the paired 4624

nodes.name: Security-4647
nodes.title-description: User initiated logoff

fields.name: TargetLogonId
fields.note: matches the LUID assigned in the session-opening 4624 — closes the session window for any 4688/4663/etc. sharing this LUID

nodes.name: Security-4648
nodes.display-title: winevtx - "A logon was attempted using explicit credentials"
nodes.title-description: A logon was attempted using explicit credentials

fields.name: subject-user-sid
fields.note: the user account that initiated the runas/explicit-credential action

fields.name: subject-logon-id
fields.note: logon ID of the CALLER's session (the session that supplied alternate credentials)

fields.name: logon-guid
fields.note: Subject-side Kerberos correlation GUID. Often all-zero. When non-zero, joins 4648 to the caller's 4624 / 4769 for Kerberos lateral-movement reconstruction.

fields.name: target-user-name
fields.note: the account whose credentials were supplied for the operation

fields.name: target-logon-guid
fields.note: Target-side Kerberos correlation GUID. On a successful 4648, pairs with the LogonGuid on the remote 4624 that completes the chain — the fingerprint that confirms 'this 4648 on host-A produced that 4624 on host-B.'

fields.name: target-server-name
fields.note: server/service the credentials were used against — 'localhost' for local runas

fields.name: process-id
fields.note: PID of the process that issued the explicit-credential call (consent.exe, runas.exe, mstsc.exe, ...). Joins to Sysmon-1 / Security-4688 for full process context.

fields.name: process-name
fields.note: the process that called the explicit-credential API — e.g., consent.exe (UAC), runas.exe, mstsc.exe

fields.name: ip-port
fields.note: source port. 0 for local / interactive callers; non-zero for remote-origin explicit-credential calls (hunt signal).

nodes.name: Security-4656
nodes.display-title: winevtx - "A handle to an object was requested"
aliases: Handle to an object was requested
nodes.title-description: A handle to an object was requested

fields.name: RestrictedSidCount
fields.note: number of restricted SIDs in the subject's token; non-zero indicates the caller is using a write-restricted / sandbox token — forensic signal for sandboxed code paths

fields.name: ObjectServer
fields.note: kernel subsystem — 'Security' for file/registry, 'SC Manager' for services, etc. Usually 'Security'.

fields.name: ObjectType
fields.note: 'File' | 'Key' (registry) | 'Process' | 'Token' | 'Thread' | 'Mutant' | 'SAM' | ...

fields.name: ObjectName
fields.note: Full object path — filesystem absolute path for Files (including \Device\HarddiskVolumeN\... form for raw-device handles) or full registry key path (\REGISTRY\MACHINE\SOFTWARE\...) for Keys. NT-object-namespace form, not drive-letter.

fields.name: HandleId
fields.note: The handle value the kernel just assigned. This HandleId threads all subsequent 4663/4657 events on this open; the matching 4658 will close it.

fields.name: TransactionId
fields.note: KTM transaction ID if the handle was opened as part of a transacted operation; typically all-zero GUID.

fields.name: AccessList
fields.note: Requested access rights — symbolic list like '%%1537 %%4416 %%4417' (each %%N resolves to a named right: ReadData, WriteData, Delete, ReadAttributes, ReadEA, WriteEA, WriteDAC, etc.). Richer than 4663's AccessMask because 4656 captures the FULL set requested at open, even if subsequent 4663s only report the per-operation subset.

fields.name: AccessReason
fields.note: Per-right reason string explaining why each requested right was granted or denied (e.g., 'ReadData: Granted by ACL'). Populated only when 'Audit Handle Manipulation' subcategory is enabled alongside 'Audit Object Access'. Extremely verbose but authoritative for access-decision forensics.

fields.name: AccessMask
fields.note: Bitmask form of AccessList — 0x1=ReadData, 0x2=WriteData, 0x4=AppendData, 0x80=WriteDAC, 0x100=ReadAttributes, 0x10000=Delete, etc. AccessList and AccessMask encode the same data.

fields.name: PrivilegeList
fields.note: Privileges used during the access check (SeSecurityPrivilege, SeTakeOwnershipPrivilege, etc.). Non-empty PrivilegeList with SeSecurity / SeTakeOwnership = admin doing explicit ACL-bypass access.

fields.name: ResourceAttributes
fields.note: Dynamic Access Control claims attached to the resource. Typically absent outside domain environments with DAC configured.

nodes.name: Security-4657
nodes.title-description: A registry value was modified

fields.name: ObjectName
fields.note: Full NT-namespace registry key path — `\REGISTRY\MACHINE\SOFTWARE\...` or `\REGISTRY\USER\<SID>\...`. Strip the `\REGISTRY\MACHINE\` prefix to get the HKLM\... form; replace `\REGISTRY\USER\<SID>` with HKU\<SID>.

fields.name: ObjectValueName
fields.note: Name of the specific REGISTRY VALUE modified (or empty for default-value writes). 4657 is UNIQUE among audit events in carrying this — 4663 only reports the KEY, not the value within.

fields.name: HandleId
fields.note: Ties this write to the preceding 4656 that opened the key handle. ProcessId + HandleId + same-session yields the full context: which process, which session, what key was opened with what rights, what value was written.

fields.name: OperationType
fields.note:
Resolves to one of:
  %%1904 = New registry value created
  %%1905 = Existing registry value modified
  %%1906 = Registry value deleted
The %%N tokens resolve via winevt.dll localization. Parsers should normalize to 'New' / 'Modify' / 'Delete'.

fields.name: OldValueType
fields.note: REG_NONE (0), REG_SZ (1), REG_EXPAND_SZ (2), REG_BINARY (3), REG_DWORD (4), REG_MULTI_SZ (7), REG_QWORD (11), etc. `%%1872` = REG_SZ (Vista+ localization token form). Absent/irrelevant for OperationType=New.

fields.name: OldValue
fields.note: Prior value of the field being modified. PRESERVED in the event even though the registry itself has been overwritten — this is the PRIMARY forensic advantage of 4657 over registry-state analysis: historical value recovery without hive transaction logs.

fields.name: NewValueType
fields.note: Registry type of the new value. Type change (OldValueType != NewValueType) is an anomaly worth investigating — unusual legitimate reason to change a value's type.

fields.name: NewValue
fields.note: Post-modification value. For REG_BINARY and large REG_SZ, the event truncates with EventData-level limits — full payload may be in the transaction log or hive snapshot rather than the event.

nodes.name: Security-4658
nodes.display-title: winevtx - "The handle to an object was closed"
aliases: Handle to an object was closed
nodes.title-description: The handle to an object was closed

fields.name: ObjectServer
fields.note: kernel subsystem — mirrors the value from the matching 4656 ('Security' for file/registry, 'SC Manager' for services, etc.)

fields.name: HandleId
fields.note: The handle being released. After this event, this HandleId value is DEAD — the kernel may reuse it for a future 4656 in the same process, so any subsequent event carrying this HandleId in the same ProcessId is a NEW handle lifecycle, not a continuation.

nodes.name: Security-4663
nodes.display-title: winevtx - "An attempt was made to access an object"
nodes.title-description: An attempt was made to access an object

fields.name: ObjectType
fields.note: 'File' | 'Key' | 'Process' | 'Token' | ...

fields.name: ObjectName
fields.note: filesystem or registry path of the accessed object; absolute path for files

fields.name: ProcessId
fields.note: PID of the process performing the access. Joins backward to the 4688 with NewProcessId==this value to identify when/how this process was created, its parent, its command line, and its SubjectLogonId.

fields.name: HandleId
fields.note: Kernel handle identifier. Joins to the 4656 that opened this handle (same HandleId + ProcessId) and the 4658 that will close it. Same ObjectName accessed via two different HandleIds = two distinct open-access-close lifecycles on the same logical object.

fields.name: AccessList
fields.note: Symbolic list of rights ACTUALLY USED (%%N tokens — %%4416 ReadData, %%4417 WriteData, %%1537 DELETE, etc.). Distinct from 4656's AccessList which is rights REQUESTED at open. Difference between the two indicates 'what was asked' vs 'what was exercised'.

fields.name: AccessMask
fields.note: hex bitmask of requested access rights — 0x1 = ReadData, 0x2 = WriteData, 0x4 = AppendData, 0x100 = ReadAttributes, 0x80 = WriteDAC, etc.

fields.name: ObjectServer
fields.note: kernel subsystem handling the object — 'Security' for file/registry, 'SC Manager' for service-related, etc. Usually 'Security'.

fields.name: ResourceAttributes
fields.note: Dynamic Access Control claims attached to the resource, if configured. Usually absent outside domain environments with DAC.

nodes.name: Security-4672
nodes.display-title: winevtx - "Special privileges assigned to new logon"
nodes.title-description: Special privileges assigned to new logon

fields.name: SubjectUserSid
fields.note: SID being granted special privileges

fields.name: SubjectUserName
fields.note: account name being elevated

fields.name: SubjectLogonId
fields.note: ties this privilege assignment to a specific 4624 logon session; critical pivot

fields.name: PrivilegeList
fields.note: Windows audits 13 sensitive privileges at logon per MS Learn. Corpus enumerates all 13 for reference (not a completeness claim about whether they fire every session): SeAssignPrimaryTokenPrivilege, SeAuditPrivilege, SeBackupPrivilege, SeCreateTokenPrivilege, SeDebugPrivilege, SeEnableDelegationPrivilege, SeImpersonatePrivilege, SeLoadDriverPrivilege, SeRestorePrivilege, SeSecurityPrivilege, SeSystemEnvironmentPrivilege, SeTakeOwnershipPrivilege, SeTcbPrivilege. Forensically most-interesting for detection: SeDebugPrivilege (process memory access), SeTcbPrivilege (act-as-OS), SeCreateTokenPrivilege (token forgery — rare even on admin), SeEnableDelegationPrivilege (Kerberos delegation — often a hunting signal when appearing on service accounts that shouldn't have it).

nodes.name: Security-4688
nodes.display-title: winevtx - "A new process has been created"
nodes.title-description: A new process has been created

fields.name: subject-user-sid
fields.note: the account under which the new process was created

fields.name: subject-domain-name
fields.note: domain / workgroup / computer name of the subject

fields.name: subject-logon-id
fields.note: Session identifier — correlates with 4624 TargetLogonId for session context. Every process this session spawns carries this LUID as its SubjectLogonId, allowing backward chain 4688→4624 for full user-attribution.

fields.name: new-process-id
fields.note: PID assigned to the newly-created process. Appears in subsequent 4663/4657/4658 events as ProcessId — the primary join key for reconstructing what this process did after creation.

fields.name: token-elevation-type
fields.note: %%1936=default, %%1937=elevated, %%1938=limited — UAC split-token indicator

fields.name: mandatory-label
fields.note: integrity level — S-1-16-4096=low, 8192=medium, 12288=high, 16384=system

fields.name: parent-process-id
fields.note: PID of the process that spawned this one. Walk parent-child recursively by joining each parent-process-id to a preceding 4688's new-process-id to reconstruct the full process tree.

fields.name: parent-process-name
fields.note: added in Win10 — earlier versions only have parent PID, requiring cross-reference

fields.name: command-line
fields.note: ONLY populated if 'Include command line in process creation events' policy is enabled — often absent by default

fields.name: target-user-sid
fields.location: EventData\TargetUserSid (when process created in different user context)
fields.note: populated for runas / CreateProcessAsUser scenarios where the creator and target identity differ. v2 / Win10+.

fields.name: target-user-name
fields.note: target account name when the process is spawned in a different user context. v2 / Win10+.

fields.name: target-domain-name
fields.note: domain of the target account. v2 / Win10+.

fields.name: target-logon-id
fields.note: LUID of the session the NEW process runs in (as opposed to SubjectLogonId which is the session that CREATED the process). Differs from SubjectLogonId only when runas / CreateProcessAsUser shifts identity. v2 / Win10+.

nodes.name: Security-4697
nodes.display-title: winevtx - "A service was installed in the system"
nodes.title-description: A service was installed in the system

fields.name: SubjectLogonId
fields.note: logon ID of the installing session — joins to the originating 4624

fields.name: ServiceFileName
fields.note: the ImagePath configured for the new service — raw string, often includes arguments

fields.name: ServiceType
fields.note: 0x10 = user-mode-own-process, 0x20 = share-process, 0x110 = interactive (rare outside legacy)

fields.name: ServiceStartType
fields.note: 2 = Automatic (start at boot), 3 = Manual, 4 = Disabled

fields.name: ServiceAccount
fields.note: account the service runs as — LocalSystem, NetworkService, LocalService, or explicit

nodes.name: Security-4698
nodes.title-description: A scheduled task was created

fields.name: TaskContent
fields.note: FULL XML definition of the task at creation time — every Action, Trigger, Principal, Settings element. Priceless for post-facto analysis of tasks that have since been modified or deleted. Pair with TaskScheduler-106 (no audit policy requirement) for always-on coverage.

fields.name: ClientProcessStartKey
fields.note: Client-process start key — Win10 1903+. Opaque per-process identifier used by the task-scheduler service; pair with ClientProcessId for full invoker attribution.

fields.name: ClientProcessId
fields.note: PID of the process that RPC-called schtasks. Win10 1903+. Hunt-gold field — reveals the actual invoker when schtasks is abused remotely.

fields.name: ParentProcessId
fields.note: Parent PID of the invoking client process. Win10 1903+. Joins to 4688 to reconstruct the parent-child chain behind task creation.

fields.name: RpcCallClientLocality
fields.note: RPC call locality (0 = unknown, 1 = local-same-host, 2 = remote). Non-local values indicate cross-host task creation via RPC — strong lateral-movement signal. Win10 1903+.

fields.name: FQDN
fields.note: Fully-qualified domain name of the client host creating the task. Populated for remote (non-local) task creation. Win10 1903+.

nodes.name: Security-4699
nodes.title-description: A scheduled task was deleted

fields.name: subject-user-sid
fields.note: SID of the user that deleted the task. For cleanup-trail investigations this identifies the removing actor.

fields.name: subject-logon-id
fields.note: Logon session that made the change. Joins back to Security-4624.

fields.name: task-name
fields.note: Full task path (e.g., \Microsoft\Windows\CustomTask\<task>). Joins to Security-4698 of the SAME TaskName in the earlier lifecycle event pair.

fields.name: task-content
fields.note: Snapshot of the task XML at deletion time. Preserves the Actions / Triggers / Principal that would otherwise be lost with the task itself — especially important when 4698 rolled.

fields.name: event-time
fields.note: Delete timestamp. Pair with preceding 4698 (task create) for lifecycle window.

nodes.name: Security-4704
nodes.title-description: A user right was assigned

fields.name: target-user-sid
fields.note: Account receiving the new right. Attacker-useful grants: SeDebugPrivilege (debug processes — credential dump), SeTakeOwnershipPrivilege (overwrite ACLs), SeBackupPrivilege (bypass NTFS ACLs — read any file), SeLoadDriverPrivilege (load unsigned driver).

fields.name: privilege-list
fields.note: Privileges granted. Alert on grants of SeDebug, SeBackup, SeTakeOwnership, SeLoadDriver, SeTrustedCredManAccess, SeImpersonatePrivilege to non-admin accounts. The privilege name is the definitive field — cross-reference against organizational allow-list.

nodes.name: Security-4719
nodes.title-description: System audit policy was changed

fields.name: subcategory-guid
fields.note: GUID of the audit subcategory being changed. Well-known values: {0CCE922B} = Process Creation, {0CCE9211} = Logon, {0CCE9226} = Kerberos Authentication Service, {0CCE9216} = File System. Disabling these = classic pre-attack tamper.

fields.name: audit-change
fields.note: String describing the change. '%%8452 %%8448' = Success audit added + Failure audit added. '%%8449' = Success removed (audit disabled). Flip from enabled → disabled is the attacker pattern.

fields.name: subject-user-sid
fields.note: Account that changed the policy. SYSTEM = automatic policy refresh from GPO. User SID = direct tamper.

nodes.name: Security-4720
nodes.title-description: A user account was created

fields.name: subject-user-sid
fields.note: the account that CREATED the new user (admin/SYSTEM)

fields.name: subject-logon-id
fields.note: session in which the creation was performed — joins to the 4624 that opened the admin session

fields.name: privilege-list
fields.note: privileges exercised during the user-creation call (often empty or "-"; admin subjects typically don't need named privileges for account management)

fields.name: target-user-sid
fields.note: SID of the NEW user account

fields.name: display-name
fields.note: AD displayName attribute. '-' or '<value not set>' for locally-created accounts.

fields.name: user-principal-name
fields.note: UPN form (user@domain.tld). Populated for domain accounts; blank for local.

fields.name: home-directory
fields.note: UNC path to user's home directory. Attacker-created accounts frequently leave this at default '-' / '<value not set>'.

fields.name: home-path
fields.note: drive letter mapped to the home directory (homeDrive attribute)

fields.name: script-path
fields.note: logon-script path. Non-empty on a local-account creation is unusual — local accounts don't typically run logon scripts. Hunt signal.

fields.name: profile-path
fields.note: roaming-profile path if set

fields.name: user-workstations
fields.note: comma-separated list of workstations the account is allowed to log on from. Legitimate admin creations often leave this unrestricted.

fields.name: password-last-set
fields.note: when the password was last set. '%%1794' = never (no password yet). Populated with a concrete timestamp on account creation if the caller set a password.

fields.name: account-expires
fields.note: account-expiration timestamp. '%%1794' = never expires. Non-%%1794 values indicate deliberate time-bounded access.

fields.name: primary-group-id
fields.note: RID of the primary group. 513 = Domain Users / Users; 512 = Domain Admins; 515 = Domain Computers. Non-513 at creation = deliberate privileged setup.

fields.name: allowed-to-delegate-to
fields.note: SPN list for constrained delegation. Non-empty on a user-account creation is rare outside administrative setup — flag for delegation-abuse review.

fields.name: old-uac-value
fields.note: always 0x0 on 4720 (the account didn't exist before)

fields.name: new-uac-value
fields.note: SAM USER_ACCOUNT flags at creation. NOTE — this is the SAM [MS-SAMR] encoding, NOT the AD userAccountControl schema; the two differ (bit values don't map 1:1). Common: 0x15 = Normal/PasswordNotReq/AccountDisabled bundle at initial create.

fields.name: user-account-control
fields.note: decoded UAC flag set — %%-coded (e.g., '%%2080 %%2082' = account-disabled + password-never-expires). Look up %%<N> codes in the event-log message table (winevt.dll).

fields.name: user-parameters
fields.note: RAS / dial-in settings. Rarely populated on modern systems.

fields.name: sid-history
fields.note: prior SIDs from cross-domain migration. Populated only on migrated accounts; non-empty at local account creation = SID-history injection attempt (classic domain-persistence technique).

fields.name: logon-hours
fields.note: %%1793 = All (unrestricted). Restricted hours are a legitimate admin configuration but also a signal of deliberate access-windowing.

nodes.name: Security-4724
nodes.display-title: winevtx - "An attempt was made to reset an account's password"
nodes.title-description: An attempt was made to reset an account's password

fields.name: target-user-sid
fields.note: Account whose password was reset. Classic attacker pattern: reset a dormant admin account's password to 'take it over' without knowing the original password.

fields.name: target-username
fields.note: SAM account name of the target.

fields.name: subject-user-sid
fields.note: Account that performed the reset. For attacker-initiated reset this is the compromised admin account.

fields.name: subject-logon-id
fields.note: Session LUID of the acting admin.

nodes.name: Security-4725
nodes.title-description: A user account was disabled

fields.name: target-user-sid
fields.note: Account that was disabled. Pair with 4722 (enabled) for lifecycle. Attacker post-ops: disable legitimate admin accounts to force reactivation via their chosen channel.

nodes.name: Security-4728
nodes.display-title: winevtx - "A member was added to a security-enabled global group"
nodes.title-description: A member was added to a security-enabled global group
nodes.integrity-mechanism: DC EVTX + replicated via AD to other DCs

fields.name: member-sid
fields.note: SID of the account added. NTDS-dit lookup resolves to current account attributes + cross-reference against Security-4738 for any simultaneous attribute changes.

fields.name: member-dn
fields.note: Full AD DN of the added member. For users: 'CN=...,OU=...,DC=...'. For other groups (nested group-in-group adds): same format for the group DN.

fields.name: target-group-sid
fields.note: SID of the GLOBAL group the member was added to. High-privilege global SIDs to alert on: S-1-5-21-<domain>-512 (Domain Admins), -516 (Domain Controllers), -519 (Enterprise Admins), -518 (Schema Admins).

fields.name: target-group-name
fields.note: Group sAMAccountName. 'Domain Admins' / 'Enterprise Admins' / application-specific privileged groups.

fields.name: subject-user-sid
fields.note: Account that made the add. Privileged-group-add actor tracking.

fields.name: subject-logon-id
fields.note: Session LUID of the acting admin. Threads to Security-4624 for full session context.

fields.name: event-time
fields.note: Group-add moment. For forensic timeline + insider / mover investigations.

nodes.name: Security-4732
nodes.display-title: winevtx - "A member was added to a security-enabled local group"
nodes.title-description: A member was added to a security-enabled local group

fields.name: member-sid
fields.note: SID of the account added to the group. Joins to NTDS-dit / SAM for account context.

fields.name: member-name
fields.note: Distinguished name or sAMAccountName of the added member. For domain groups, DN format; for local groups, account name.

fields.name: target-group-sid
fields.note: SID of the group. Well-known high-privilege SIDs to alert on: S-1-5-32-544 (Administrators), S-1-5-32-551 (Backup Operators), S-1-5-32-548 (Account Operators), S-1-5-32-549 (Server Operators), S-1-5-32-578 (Hyper-V Administrators).

fields.name: target-group-name
fields.note: Group name. 'Administrators' / 'Backup Operators' / custom-named groups.

fields.name: subject-user-sid
fields.note: Account that performed the group-add. For mover / privilege-abuse investigations this is the person who added the member.

fields.name: subject-logon-id
fields.note: Session LUID of the acting admin. Threads to Security-4624 for context.

fields.name: event-time
fields.note: Group-add moment. For mover-pattern analysis, compare against documented transfer-date.

nodes.name: Security-4738
nodes.title-description: A user account was changed
nodes.integrity-mechanism: EVTX-level; replicated across DCs via AD replication for domain events

fields.name: target-user-sid
fields.note: SID of the account that was changed. Joins to NTDS-dit / SAM for account-context and to subsequent auth events.

fields.name: target-username
fields.note: SAM account name + domain. Format: DOMAIN\user. Cross-reference against Security-4624 / 4776 post-change.

fields.name: subject-user-sid
fields.note: Account that MADE the change. For auditing: attacker-initiated changes (password reset, UAC flags, SID history addition) reveal who performed the modification.

fields.name: subject-logon-id
fields.note: Session LUID of the acting user — threads to Security-4624 for context.

fields.name: changed-attributes
fields.location: EventData — AttributeName / OldValue / NewValue AND UserAccountControl bit deltas
fields.note: Set of attribute changes applied in this event. Key fields: PasswordLastSet (password reset), UserAccountControl (enable/disable, password-never-expires, don't-require-preauth — preauth-off = AS-REP roasting exposure), PrimaryGroupId, SidHistory, UserParameters. Multiple changes in one event = bulk edit (admin tooling or attacker script).

fields.name: user-account-control
fields.note: Encoding caveat (2026-04-23): OldUacValue/NewUacValue carry MS-SAMR USER_ACCOUNT codes (MS-SAMR §2.2.1.12), NOT AD userAccountControl schema values. Bit layouts differ — e.g., DONT_REQ_PREAUTH = 0x00010000 (SAM) vs 0x00400000 (AD); SMARTCARD_REQUIRED = 0x00001000 (SAM) vs 0x00040000 (AD); TRUSTED_FOR_DELEGATION = 0x00002000 (SAM) vs 0x00080000 (AD). See ms-samr-user-account-codes for the authoritative bitmask and ms-kb-useraccountcontrol for AD-side cross-reference. Cited bit values below reflect legacy annotations and MUST be re-verified against MS-SAMR before SIEM rule-building. Attacker-useful changes (conceptual, encoding-independent): enable disabled account, clear DONT_EXPIRE_PASSWORD, set DONT_REQ_PREAUTH (AS-REP-roast exposure), set TRUSTED_FOR_DELEGATION (Kerberos unconstrained delegation).

fields.name: event-time
fields.note: Change-apply time. For Mover investigations: bracket 4738 events for the Mover's account vs the date-of-transfer to distinguish legitimate transfer-related changes from unexpected later modifications.
fields.clock: system (DC for domain; host for local)

nodes.name: Security-4741
nodes.title-description: A computer account was created

nodes.name: Security-4742
nodes.title-description: A computer account was changed

fields.name: user-account-control
fields.note: Encoding caveat (2026-04-23): OldUacValue/NewUacValue carry MS-SAMR USER_ACCOUNT codes (MS-SAMR §2.2.1.12), NOT AD userAccountControl schema values. Bit layouts differ — e.g., TRUSTED_FOR_DELEGATION = 0x00002000 (SAM) vs 0x80000 (AD). See ms-samr-user-account-codes for the authoritative bitmask and ms-kb-useraccountcontrol for AD-side cross-reference. Cited values below reflect legacy AD-derived annotations and MUST be re-verified against MS-SAMR before SIEM rule-building. Attacker-high-signal: TRUSTED_FOR_DELEGATION / TRUSTED_TO_AUTH_FOR_DELEGATION set on a computer account = unconstrained or constrained delegation grant (Kerberos privilege escalation path).

nodes.name: Security-4743
nodes.title-description: A computer account was deleted

nodes.name: Security-4776
nodes.display-title: winevtx - "The computer attempted to validate the credentials for an account"
nodes.title-description: The computer attempted to validate the credentials for an account

fields.name: workstation-name
fields.note: NetBIOS name of the workstation attempting authentication — the CLIENT. Crucial for pass-the-hash detection: a workstation name that does NOT exist in the domain is a tell that an attacker forged the name. Also the same-workstation-many-accounts pattern reveals credential spraying.

fields.name: target-username
fields.note: Account being validated. Multiple 4776 events for the same account from the same workstation in close time = normal. Many DIFFERENT accounts from ONE workstation = spray / enumeration. One account from MANY workstations = credential reuse / lateral movement.

fields.name: status
fields.note: 0x0 = validation succeeded. Non-zero = failed. Common failure codes: 0xC0000064 (no such user), 0xC000006A (wrong password), 0xC0000234 (account locked), 0xC0000072 (account disabled), 0xC0000234 (locked-out). Pattern of 0xC000006A across many accounts from same workstation = password-spray.

fields.name: error-code
fields.location: EventData → Status (duplicate of status on this event)
fields.note: Same as Status. NTLM validation does not report sub-status — unlike 4625 which has both Status and SubStatus. Use Status alone for classification.

fields.name: event-time
fields.note: Validation moment. On a DC, 4776 timestamp precedes the 4624 / 4625 on the target server — joining them gives the full path (validator → target) of the NTLM auth.
fields.clock: system (DC or local host acting as validator)

nodes.name: Security-4781
nodes.display-title: winevtx - "The name of an account was changed"
nodes.title-description: The name of an account was changed

fields.name: target-user-sid
fields.note: SID of the renamed account — PERSISTENT across the rename (SID doesn't change). Cross-reference with both old and new SAM name.

fields.name: new-name
fields.note: Attacker pattern: rename an existing account to masquerade as a different user. The SID stays — so SID-based audit trails still trace the original account.

nodes.name: Security-4782
nodes.display-title: winevtx - "The password hash of an account was accessed (ADMT password migration)"
nodes.title-description: The password hash of an account was accessed (ADMT password migration)

fields.name: target-user-sid
fields.note: Account whose password hash was extracted by ADMT. In legitimate use: source-domain account whose credential material is being migrated to the target forest.

fields.name: target-username
fields.note: SAM account name of the target. Anomalous if target is a privileged account (domain admin, krbtgt) outside a documented migration plan.

fields.name: subject-user-sid
fields.note: Account that invoked the ADMT PasswordMigrationFilter. For legitimate use: the administrator running ADMT. Any 4782 with a non-admin, non-migration-service SubjectUserSid is suspicious but should be triaged as unauthorized ADMT / misuse-of-migration-tooling rather than DCSync.

fields.name: subject-logon-id
fields.note: Session LUID of the acting account on the DC.

fields.name: event-time
fields.note: Timestamp of the ADMT password-migration API call.

nodes.name: Security-5038
nodes.display-title: winevtx - "Code integrity determined that the image hash of a file is not valid"
nodes.title-description: Code integrity determined that the image hash of a file is not valid

fields.name: image-path
fields.note: Path of the image whose hash didn't match. Attacker tamper with signed system binaries (e.g., overwriting a System32 DLL with a patched version) surfaces here. Cross-reference against Amcache SHA-1 to see WHICH hash was observed.

nodes.name: Security-5140
nodes.display-title: winevtx - "A network share object was accessed"
nodes.title-description: A network share object was accessed

fields.name: SubjectLogonId
fields.note: join key back to the 4624 session

fields.name: ObjectType
fields.note: typically 'File' for share access

fields.name: IpAddress
fields.note: source IP of the connecting host

fields.name: ShareName
fields.note: UNC-style share identifier (\\\\*\\IPC$, \\\\*\\C$, \\\\*\\<custom>)

fields.name: ShareLocalPath
fields.note: local filesystem path backing the share (C:\, C:\shares\..., etc.)

nodes.name: Security-5156
nodes.display-title: winevtx - "Windows Filtering Platform has permitted a connection"
nodes.title-description: Windows Filtering Platform has permitted a connection

fields.name: process-id
fields.note: PID of the process whose connection was permitted. Joins to Security-4688 NewProcessId / Sysmon-1 ProcessId for process attribution. Key join-field for the authentication-to-network-effect chain.

fields.name: application
fields.note: Kernel-format path of the executable initiating / receiving the connection. Requires drive-letter translation (via GetFinalPathNameByHandle or mapping against MountedDevices). Prefetch / Amcache lookup by filename provides execution-evidence context.

fields.name: source-address
fields.note: Source IP of the connection. For outbound (direction=0) from this host, equals this host's IP. For inbound (direction=1), equals the remote peer's IP.

fields.name: source-port
fields.note: Source port. Ephemeral for outbound client sockets; service port for inbound.

fields.name: destination-address
fields.note: Destination IP. For outbound = remote peer; for inbound = this host.

fields.name: destination-port
fields.note: Destination port.

fields.name: protocol
fields.note: Transport protocol. Combined with ports identifies the service being accessed (80=HTTP, 443=HTTPS, 445=SMB, 3389=RDP, 88=Kerberos).

fields.name: direction
fields.note: Connection direction relative to this host. Attacker connections to C2 = outbound; attacker inbound pivot = inbound.

fields.name: layer-name
fields.note: Which WFP layer filtered the connection. Most common: 'Application Layer Enforcement IPv4 Send/Recv' (%%14611/14610). Differs for SMB / named pipes / Kerberos / other sub-layers.

fields.name: subject-logon-id
fields.note: Logon session the connecting process belongs to. Joins back to Security-4624 TargetLogonId — completes the user→process→connection chain.

fields.name: event-time
fields.note: Event record time. Per-connection precision — high-volume data supporting timeline reconstruction of every network connection.

nodes.name: Security-5379
nodes.title-description: Credential Manager credentials were read

fields.name: target-name
fields.note: Credential target identifier — 'TERMSRV/<host>' for saved RDP, 'git:https://<host>' for Git credentials, 'LegacyGeneric:target=<service>' for saved Outlook. Reveals WHICH credential was read.

fields.name: type
fields.note: Credential type: 1=Generic, 2=Domain, 3=Generic-Certificate, 4=Domain-Certificate, 5=Domain-Visible-Password.

fields.name: subject-user-sid
fields.note: Account whose Credential Manager was read (is the process running under this user's context).

nodes.name: Security-5381
nodes.title-description: Vault credentials were enumerated

fields.name: vault-id
fields.note: GUID of the vault whose credentials were read. Well-known: {4BF4C442-9B8A-41A0-B380-DD4A704DDB28} = Web Credentials, {77BC582B-F0A6-4E15-4E80-61736B6F3B29} = Windows Credentials. Attacker reading Web Credentials vault = browser-saved-HTTP-auth exfil.

nodes.name: Security-6416
nodes.display-title: winevtx - "A new external device was recognized by the System"
nodes.title-description: A new external device was recognized by the System

fields.name: SubjectUserSid
fields.note: always SYSTEM / S-1-5-18 — PnP runs in kernel context; real attribution comes from correlating timestamp with user-context events

fields.name: SubjectUserName
fields.note: always the machine account — computer name with trailing $ — reflecting that PnP runs as SYSTEM not an interactive user

fields.name: SubjectDomainName
fields.note: domain / workgroup name; in local contexts equals computer name

fields.name: SubjectLogonId
fields.note: always 0x3e7 (SYSTEM logon) — same caveat

fields.name: DeviceId
fields.note: PnP instance path — casing trap: DeviceId (not DeviceInstanceId as used by Kernel-PnP)

nodes.name: Sysmon-1
nodes.title-description: Process Create
nodes.integrity-mechanism: EVTX checksums; Sysmon driver is signed

fields.name: rule-name
fields.note: Name of the matching config rule (populated when the Sysmon XML config uses `<ProcessCreate name='...'>`). Empty for events matched by default rules. A labeled RuleName makes SIEM grouping / hunting rules dramatically more readable than raw image-path matching.

fields.name: process-guid
fields.note: Sysmon-generated unique identifier; correlates with other Sysmon events for the same process

fields.name: process-id
fields.note: PID assigned to this newly-created process. Joins forward to all subsequent Sysmon events for this process via ProcessGuid (more durable) or PID (faster, reuse-risky).

fields.name: original-file-name
fields.note: mismatches with image's filename indicate renamed binaries — classic masquerade tell

fields.name: command-line
fields.note: always captured (unlike 4688 which requires extra GPO)

fields.name: user
fields.note: friendly user — the SID must be resolved via SAM/ProfileList

fields.name: logon-id
fields.note: correlates with Security 4624 TargetLogonId — joins Sysmon-1 to the Security.evtx session window

fields.name: integrity-level
fields.note: Low / Medium / High / System

fields.name: hashes
fields.note: which algorithms are included depends on Sysmon config (HashAlgorithms directive)

fields.name: parent-process-id
fields.note: PID of the process that spawned this one. Recursively join each parent-process-id to a prior Sysmon-1's process-id to reconstruct the process tree.

nodes.name: Sysmon-10
nodes.title-description: ProcessAccess

fields.name: RuleName
fields.note: Matching config rule name. Event 10 is very noisy — named rules are the only sane way to run SIEM alerting on it.

fields.name: SourceThreadId
fields.note: TID of the thread in the source process that called OpenProcess. Useful for correlating with Sysmon-8 NewThreadId (the thread created post-access) during inject-then-run sequences.

fields.name: TargetProcessId
fields.note: PID of the process being OpenProcess'd. Join to Sysmon-1.process-id for the target's image+parent+command-line lineage.

fields.name: TargetImage
fields.note: typically lsass.exe in credential-access cases

fields.name: GrantedAccess
fields.note: hex bitmask of PROCESS_* access rights granted via OpenProcess. Key values — 0x1010 and 0x1410 are the PROCESS_VM_READ + PROCESS_QUERY_INFORMATION patterns used by credential-dump tools

fields.name: CallTrace
fields.note: module+offset stack of the OpenProcess call; CRITICAL for attribution because the calling module often identifies the tool

fields.name: SourceUser
fields.note: User context of the process calling OpenProcess (Sysmon 13+).

fields.name: TargetUser
fields.note: User context of the target process (Sysmon 13+). TargetUser != SourceUser is frequently a privilege-boundary crossing signal.

nodes.name: Sysmon-11
nodes.title-description: FileCreate

fields.name: rule-name
fields.note: Matching config rule name. FileCreate is high-volume; named rules drive scoped alerts (drop-to-temp, executable-in-user-dir, etc.).

fields.name: image
fields.note: the process that created the file

fields.name: creation-utc-time
fields.note: NTFS $SI.created timestamp for the new file — same as kernel-timestamped creation

nodes.name: Sysmon-12
nodes.display-title: winevtx - "Registry object added or deleted (CreateKey / DeleteKey)"
nodes.title-description: Registry object added or deleted (CreateKey / DeleteKey)

nodes.name: Sysmon-13
nodes.title-description: RegistryEvent (Value Set)

fields.name: rule-name
fields.note: Matching config rule name. Persistence-oriented rules typically carry named identifiers (e.g. 'RunKey', 'Service-ImagePath') so SIEM queries can alert by mechanism.

fields.name: target-object
fields.note: full registry path being modified — 'HKLM\...\Run\<name>' etc.

fields.name: details
fields.note: value data written; may be truncated for large REG_BINARY

nodes.name: Sysmon-14
nodes.title-description: Registry key or value renamed

fields.name: new-name
fields.note: Destination name after rename.

nodes.name: Sysmon-15
nodes.display-title: winevtx - "FileCreateStreamHash — Alternate Data Stream created with hashed content"
nodes.title-description: FileCreateStreamHash — Alternate Data Stream created with hashed content

nodes.name: Sysmon-17
nodes.title-description: Named pipe created (PipeEvent Created)

fields.name: pipe-name
fields.note: Pipe name. Cobalt Strike default pipes (msagent_*, postex_*, status_*, mypipe-*) are famous IOC patterns. PsExec pipes (PSEXESVC). Named-pipe impersonation tools. Attacker C2 / lateral-movement fingerprint.

nodes.name: Sysmon-18
nodes.title-description: Named pipe connected (client → server)

fields.name: image
fields.note: Process connecting TO the pipe. Pairs with Sysmon-17 (creation) to capture the full pipe-client-server handshake — detecting both the framework's server side and the lateral-movement client side.

nodes.name: Sysmon-2
nodes.title-description: File creation time changed (timestomping indicator)

fields.name: previous-creation-time
fields.note: Creation time BEFORE the set. Delta vs CreationUtcTime reveals direction of timestomp (backdate).

nodes.name: Sysmon-22
nodes.title-description: DnsQuery

fields.name: rule-name
fields.note: Matching config rule name. DNS-hunt rules frequently use named rules for DGA pattern, exfil-over-DNS, or suspicious-TLD detection.

fields.name: process-id
fields.note: PID of the process that issued the DNS query. Join to Sysmon-1.process-id for the resolver caller's image+command-line.

fields.name: image
fields.note: process that made the DNS query — unique visibility Sysmon provides that the OS resolver doesn't

fields.name: query-status
fields.note: 0 = success; non-zero codes include NXDOMAIN (3), SERVFAIL, etc.

fields.name: query-results
fields.note: per-record results — parse for individual IPs/CNAMEs

nodes.name: Sysmon-23
nodes.display-title: winevtx - "File delete archived (FileDelete logged — preserves deleted content)"
nodes.title-description: File delete archived (FileDelete logged — preserves deleted content)

fields.name: archived
fields.note: When true, Sysmon preserved the deleted file's bytes in its archive directory (configurable). Analyst can recover the deleted file from Sysmon's cache even after true deletion.

nodes.name: Sysmon-25
nodes.display-title: winevtx - "Process tampering (image hollowing / process herpaderping / doppelgänging detected)"
nodes.title-description: Process tampering (image hollowing / process herpaderping / doppelgänging detected)

fields.name: tamper-type
fields.encoding: 'Image is replaced' / 'Image is replaced by other process'
fields.note: Distinguishes self-hollowing from remote-hollowing. Image replacement = the PE on disk doesn't match the in-memory image = classic injection / hollowing.

nodes.name: Sysmon-3
nodes.title-description: Network Connection

fields.name: rule-name
fields.note: Matching config rule name (empty if not named). Hunt queries frequently filter by RuleName to get targeted alerting.

fields.name: process-guid
fields.note: Sysmon-assigned — joins to Sysmon-1.process-guid for the full initiating-process lineage.

fields.name: initiated
fields.note: true = outbound from this host, false = inbound

fields.name: source-is-ipv6
fields.note: whether source-ip is IPv6. Pair with destination-is-ipv6; mixed-stack behavior can surface IPv6-preferring malware on dual-stack hosts.

fields.name: source-hostname
fields.note: reverse-PTR lookup of source-ip. Frequently blank; don't over-interpret.

fields.name: source-port-name
fields.note: IANA service-name lookup for source-port (e.g. 'http', 'https', 'ssh'). Empty for ephemeral ports above 49152.

fields.name: destination-hostname
fields.note: reverse-PTR lookup of destination-ip — NOT the name the process resolved. For the actually-queried name, pair with Sysmon-22 (DNS) by matching process-guid + nearby timestamp.

fields.name: destination-port-name
fields.note: IANA service-name for destination-port.

nodes.name: Sysmon-5
nodes.title-description: Process terminated

fields.name: process-guid
fields.note: Sysmon-assigned unique process instance ID — joins reliably to Sysmon-1 (create) across PID reuse.

nodes.name: Sysmon-6
nodes.display-title: winevtx - "Driver loaded (kernel-mode driver load; signing + hash + path for rootkit detection)"
nodes.title-description: Driver loaded (kernel-mode driver load; signing + hash + path for rootkit detection)

fields.name: image-loaded
fields.note: Full path of the loaded driver. Microsoft drivers live under %SystemRoot%\System32\drivers\. Third-party drivers in vendor-specific paths (typically Program Files or dedicated driver directories). Attacker-loaded drivers in unusual paths (%TEMP%, user-writable locations) = candidate rootkit. BYOVD (Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver) attacks load legitimately-signed BUT KNOWN-VULNERABLE drivers to abuse their kernel-mode capabilities — check loaded drivers against Microsoft's known-vulnerable-driver list.

fields.name: hashes
fields.encoding: MD5=... SHA256=... IMPHASH=... (concatenated per Sysmon HashAlgorithms config)
fields.note: Cryptographic hashes of the driver binary. Cross-reference against VirusTotal / MSRC / internal threat intel for attribution. Microsoft's LOLDrivers project maintains a public list of known-vulnerable drivers by hash — Sysmon-6 hashes matching LOLDrivers = BYOVD indicator.

fields.name: signed
fields.note: true = driver has a valid Authenticode signature. false = unsigned. Modern Windows (10 / 11) refuses to load unsigned kernel drivers by default unless testsigning is on. Unsigned driver loaded = BCD-testsigning-on OR kernel-signing bypass exploit. Pair with BCD-Store registry for context.

fields.name: signature-name
fields.note: Publisher name from the signing certificate (e.g., 'Microsoft Windows Publisher', 'NVIDIA Corporation'). Attacker drivers signed with: stolen legitimate certs (signer = legitimate vendor but hash + filename don't match vendor catalog), purpose-attacker-obtained code-signing certs (signer = unknown entity), self-signed on testsigning hosts.

fields.name: signature-status
fields.note: 'Valid' / 'Expired' / 'UntrustedRoot' / 'Unknown'. Non-'Valid' status on a loaded driver = integrity issue — either signing cert problems OR attacker bypass.

fields.name: process-id
fields.note: PID of the process that triggered the driver load (typically the Service Control Manager or System process). For user-initiated driver loads (e.g., via sc.exe or net start), PID identifies the initiator — joins to Security-4688 / Sysmon-1 process-creation record.

fields.name: utc-time
fields.note: Driver load time. Pair with System-7045 (service install) when the driver is loaded via a newly-created service — T1543.003 kernel-driver-service persistence combo.

nodes.name: Sysmon-7
nodes.title-description: Image loaded

fields.name: rule-name
fields.note: Matching config rule name. Because Event 7 is extremely high-volume, well-tuned configs heavily rely on named rules for filter clarity.

fields.name: process-id
fields.note: PID of the process into which the module was loaded.

fields.name: image
fields.note: the process into which the module was loaded

fields.name: image-loaded
fields.note: the DLL/EXE module that was loaded — this is the forensic target

fields.name: original-file-name
fields.note: PE OriginalFilename resource. Mismatch with ImageLoaded's filename = renamed binary — canonical DLL-side-load indicator (legitimate DLL name at a wrong path with a different embedded OriginalFileName).

fields.name: hashes
fields.note: hash of the LOADED module (not the process)

fields.name: signed
fields.note: whether the loaded module carries a valid Authenticode signature

fields.name: signature
fields.note: signer name when Signed=true

nodes.name: Sysmon-8
nodes.title-description: CreateRemoteThread detected

fields.name: RuleName
fields.note: Matching config rule name. Event 8 benefits from named rules because discriminating benign sources (AV, debuggers) from suspicious ones is rule-driven.

fields.name: SourceProcessGuid
fields.note: Sysmon-assigned process GUID; joins with Sysmon-1 ProcessGuid field

fields.name: SourceImage
fields.note: process creating the remote thread

fields.name: TargetImage
fields.note: process receiving the remote thread — commonly lsass.exe, explorer.exe, or a legitimate binary being hollowed

fields.name: StartAddress
fields.note: virtual address where the new thread begins execution — if outside the target's loaded modules, strong injection signal

fields.name: StartModule
fields.note: module owning StartAddress (blank if into allocated heap — SUSPICIOUS)

fields.name: StartFunction
fields.note: exported symbol at StartAddress (blank if into raw allocated memory — SUSPICIOUS)

fields.name: SourceUser
fields.note: User context of the source process (Sysmon 13+). Split source/target identities expose cross-user injection attempts.

fields.name: TargetUser
fields.note: User context of the target process (Sysmon 13+). TargetUser != SourceUser is often a strong signal (e.g., a user-mode process injecting into a SYSTEM service).

nodes.name: System-104
nodes.display-title: winevtx - "The System log file was cleared"
nodes.title-description: The System log file was cleared
nodes.integrity-mechanism: EVTX-level; channel-wide retention policy applies after 104 as normal

fields.name: event-time
fields.note: The moment the System log was cleared — this is the very first timestamp in the post-clear System.evtx.

fields.name: channel-cleared
fields.note: The channel whose log was cleared — usually 'System' (matching the container) but 104 is emitted to the System log when OTHER non-Security channels are cleared too (Application, Setup, custom admin channels). The Channel field disambiguates which log the clear actually targeted.

fields.name: subject-user-sid
fields.note: SID of the account that cleared the log — requires membership in Administrators / Backup Operators / Event Log Readers group OR equivalent 'Manage auditing and security log' right.

fields.name: subject-logon-id
fields.note: LUID of the session that initiated the clear. Joins to Security-4624 / 4634 around the event to identify how the clearing session was established (interactive / remote / scheduled / etc).

nodes.name: System-1074
nodes.display-title: winevtx - "The process has initiated the restart / shutdown of the computer"
nodes.title-description: The process has initiated the restart / shutdown of the computer

fields.name: initiating-process
fields.note: Full path of the process that issued the shutdown API call. Common values: C:\Windows\System32\shutdown.exe (command-line / GUI shutdown), C:\Windows\System32\winlogon.exe (Start-menu shutdown), C:\Windows\System32\wbem\wmiprvse.exe (WMI Win32_OperatingSystem.Shutdown), powershell.exe (Stop-Computer / Restart-Computer). Attacker-initiated forced reboots typically show shutdown.exe or powershell.exe in this field.

fields.name: reason-text
fields.note: Human-readable reason string. 'No title for this reason could be found' when unspecified; otherwise text like 'Operating System: Service pack (Planned)'.

fields.name: reason-code
fields.note: Hex shutdown reason code (Major.Minor pair). Useful for distinguishing planned (major 0x8) vs. user-initiated (major 0x0) vs. application-initiated (major 0x4) shutdowns.

fields.name: shutdown-type
fields.note: 'shutdown' | 'restart' | 'power off' — distinguishes restart from full power-off. Forced reboots by attackers often use 'restart' (shutdown /r) to bring the host back up quickly.

fields.name: initiating-user
fields.note: User account under which the shutdown was initiated. Cross-reference against Security-4624 / 4634 around the event time to verify that user's session was active. A 1074 with a SYSTEM user and a scripted initiating-process suggests scheduled / service / remote-triggered shutdown rather than interactive.

fields.name: computer-name
fields.note: Host that recorded the event. Useful in multi-host investigations.

fields.name: event-time
fields.note: Moment the shutdown was INITIATED — not when power actually cut. Delta to the next-boot System-6005 / System-41 bounds the shutdown-and-boot duration.

nodes.name: System-41
nodes.display-title: winevtx - "The system rebooted without cleanly shutting down first"
nodes.title-description: The system rebooted without cleanly shutting down first

fields.name: bugcheck-code
fields.note: Nonzero = BSOD crash occurred. Zero = power loss / forced hard-off with no crash. Common crash codes: 0x9F (DRIVER_POWER_STATE_FAILURE), 0xEF (CRITICAL_PROCESS_DIED), 0xA (IRQL_NOT_LESS_OR_EQUAL). Attacker-forced reboots (Stop-Computer -Force, holding the power button, PSU kill) typically produce 0x0.

fields.name: bugcheck-parameters
fields.note: BSOD-specific parameters. Useful only when BugcheckCode is nonzero. Zero in all four is the normal dirty-power-off pattern.

fields.name: sleep-in-progress
fields.note: True if the system was transitioning to sleep when the shutdown happened. False is the more-common attacker-forced pattern.

fields.name: power-button-timestamp
fields.note: Exact moment the power button was registered (when the firmware/kernel captured it). Nonzero = user held the power button. Zero = not a power-button event (crash / PSU loss / remote forced reboot / VM host kill). For attacker-forced reboot via `shutdown /r /f /t 0` or `Stop-Computer -Force`, this field is usually zero — the OS issued the shutdown before the kernel could record a button press.

fields.name: computer-name
fields.note: Host that recorded the event. Useful in multi-host investigations where evtx bundles land together and need sorting.

fields.name: event-time
fields.note: Boot time of the session that NOTICED the dirty shutdown — i.e., the first boot after the crash / power loss. Subtract from the last surviving event in the PREVIOUS boot to bound when the unexpected shutdown occurred.

nodes.name: System-7036
nodes.display-title: winevtx - "A service entered the running / stopped state"
nodes.title-description: A service entered the running / stopped state

fields.name: StateText
fields.note: localized string — 'running' | 'stopped' | ...

nodes.name: System-7045
nodes.display-title: winevtx - "A service was installed in the system"
nodes.title-description: A service was installed in the system

fields.name: ImagePath
fields.note: raw binary-plus-args as configured; may include service-host host PID or DLL path for svchost-style services

fields.name: StartType
fields.note: auto-start | demand-start | system | boot | disabled

fields.name: AccountName
fields.note: service account (LocalSystem, NetworkService, etc., or explicit SID/name)

nodes.name: TaskScheduler-106
nodes.title-description: Task registered

fields.name: UserContext
fields.note: account that registered the task (domain\user or SID)

nodes.name: TaskScheduler-140
nodes.title-description: Task updated

nodes.name: TaskScheduler-141
nodes.title-description: Task deleted

nodes.name: TaskScheduler-200
nodes.title-description: Action started

fields.name: ActionName
fields.note: full path of the action binary — the ImagePath TaskScheduler will launch

fields.name: TaskInstanceId
fields.note: GUID per launch — joins 200 (started) with 201 (completed)

nodes.name: TaskScheduler-201
nodes.title-description: Action completed

fields.name: TaskInstanceId
fields.note: GUID matching the paired 200 event

fields.name: ResultCode
fields.note: process exit code (hex). 0 = success; non-zero values reveal task failure — especially useful when attacker cleanup destroys the action binary after one run

nodes.name: TS-LSM-21
nodes.display-title: winevtx - "Remote Desktop Services — session logon succeeded"
nodes.title-description: Remote Desktop Services — session logon succeeded

fields.name: user
fields.note: 'DOMAIN\username' or machine\username

fields.name: source-ip
fields.note: 'LOCAL' for console logons, IP for RDP

nodes.name: exFAT-Boot
nodes.location-path: sector 0 of an exFAT partition (main boot region); sectors 12-23 hold the backup boot region
nodes.integrity-mechanism: boot-region checksum — sectors 0-10 hashed into sector 11, validated at mount time by Windows exFAT driver

fields.name: jump-instruction
fields.note: differs from FAT32's EB 58 90 — useful filesystem-identifier hint

fields.name: oem-id
fields.location: offset 0x03 (8 bytes ASCII) — 'EXFAT   ' (5 chars + 3 spaces), required by spec
fields.note: unlike FAT32, exFAT spec MANDATES this exact OEM-ID string. Any variation indicates non-spec formatter or tamper.

fields.name: zero-region
fields.location: offset 0x0B to 0x3F (53 bytes) — required to be zero per spec
fields.note: distinguishes exFAT from FAT32: FAT32 has BPB fields in this range; exFAT explicitly zeros it and puts the BPB at offset 0x40+

fields.name: partition-offset
fields.location: offset 0x40 (uint64 LE) — LBA of this partition in the whole-disk view

fields.name: volume-length
fields.location: offset 0x48 (uint64 LE) — partition size in sectors

fields.name: fat-offset
fields.location: offset 0x50 (uint32 LE) — sector offset of FAT (relative to partition)

fields.name: fat-length
fields.location: offset 0x54 (uint32 LE) — FAT size in sectors

fields.name: cluster-heap-offset
fields.location: offset 0x58 (uint32 LE) — sector offset of cluster heap

fields.name: first-cluster-of-root
fields.location: offset 0x60 (uint32 LE) — cluster address of root directory

fields.name: volume-serial-number
fields.location: offset 0x64 (uint32 LE) — filesystem-level serial, generated at format time
fields.note: THE VSN. Offset differs from FAT32 (0x43) and NTFS (0x48). Same shell-artifact representation: 4-byte LE integer, often shown XXXX-XXXX in Explorer.

fields.name: filesystem-revision
fields.location: offset 0x68 (uint16 LE) — high byte major, low byte minor (e.g., 01 00 = revision 1.0)
fields.note: currently always 01 00; future revisions may change BPB layout

fields.name: volume-flags
fields.location: offset 0x6A (uint16 LE) — bitfield: bit0 ActiveFat, bit1 VolumeDirty, bit2 MediaFailure, bit3 ClearToZero
fields.note: VolumeDirty bit = clean-unmount flag; analog to NTFS $Volume dirty bit. Set when volume is mounted RW, cleared on clean unmount. Always-set value suggests frequent unsafe removal.

fields.name: number-of-fats
fields.location: offset 0x6E (uint8) — typically 1 on exFAT (can be 2 for TexFAT, rare)

fields.name: percent-in-use
fields.location: offset 0x70 (uint8) — volume fullness, 0-100 or 0xFF if unknown

fields.name: boot-code
fields.location: offset 0x78 to 0x1FD — 390 bytes of real-mode bootstrap

fields.name: boot-checksum
fields.location: sector 11 of main boot region (end-of-boot-region) — stored repeatedly across the sector as uint32 values
fields.encoding: 32-bit rolling checksum of sectors 0-10 per exFAT spec §3.4; VolumeFlags and PercentInUse excluded from the computation
fields.note: INTEGRITY MECHANISM. After hex-editing the boot sector (including VSN), this checksum must be recomputed or the volume will fail mount on Windows. Tamper-detection signal unavailable on FAT32.

nodes.name: FAT32-Boot
nodes.location-path: sector 0 of a FAT32 partition (raw-sector read — no named file)
nodes.integrity-mechanism: none (no checksum on FAT32 boot sector)

fields.name: jump-instruction
fields.note: non-code-fetch filesystem-identifier hint; combined with OEM-ID distinguishes FAT32 from exFAT

fields.name: oem-id
fields.location: offset 0x03 (8 bytes ASCII) — 'MSWIN4.1' (NT-series default), 'MSDOS5.0', or formatter-specific string

fields.name: reserved-sectors
fields.location: offset 0x0E (uint16 LE) — includes boot sector + FSInfo + backup

fields.name: total-sectors
fields.location: offset 0x20 (uint32 LE) — FAT32 uses the 32-bit field; earlier 16-bit field at 0x13 is zero

fields.name: sectors-per-fat
fields.location: offset 0x24 (uint32 LE) — FAT32-specific; larger-than-FAT16 field width

fields.name: root-cluster
fields.location: offset 0x2C (uint32 LE) — cluster address of root directory (typically 2)

fields.name: fsinfo-sector
fields.location: offset 0x30 (uint16 LE) — relative sector number of FSInfo block

fields.name: backup-boot-sector
fields.location: offset 0x32 (uint16 LE) — relative sector of boot sector replica (typically 6)

fields.name: volume-serial-number
fields.location: offset 0x43 (uint32 LE) — filesystem-level serial, generated by format.exe
fields.note: THE VSN. Shell artifacts (LNK, ShellBags, jump lists) record this 4-byte value at access time. Regenerated on every format.

fields.name: volume-label-boot
fields.note: shell-visible label; in FAT32 there are TWO volume labels — one here in the boot sector, and one in the root directory as a special entry with VOLUME_ID attribute. `format.exe /V:` updates both; in-OS rename typically updates only the directory-entry copy — boot-sector label becomes stale.

fields.name: filesystem-type
fields.note: not required by spec — some formatters leave blank; don't rely on this string alone for FS identification

fields.name: boot-code
fields.note: on non-bootable data volumes, usually a 'This is not a bootable disk' message; on bootable FAT32 volumes, Windows bootstrap

fields.name: boot-signature
fields.note: present on both FAT32 and exFAT; absence strongly suggests the sector is not a valid VBR

fields.name: entry-volume-birth-droid
fields.note: DLT volume identifier at the target's creation — partially decodable as FilesystemVolumeSerial

fields.name: entry-file-birth-droid
fields.note: DLT file identifier at creation; pair with machine-id for cross-host attribution

fields.name: entry-machine-id
fields.location: DestList per-entry — NetBIOS machine name field

fields.name: entry-mft-entry-ref
fields.location: DestList per-entry — MFT segment reference (6-byte entry + 2-byte sequence)

fields.name: entry-modified-time
fields.note: target file's modification time at the moment of jump-list write

fields.name: entry-last-access-time
fields.location: DestList per-entry — last time the user selected this entry in Explorer
fields.note: the 'last opened via jump list' signal — distinct from the target's own MAC times

fields.name: entry-pin-status
fields.note: 0 = auto-tracked, -1 (0xFFFFFFFF) = pinned by user

fields.name: entry-stream-id
fields.location: DestList per-entry — Entry ID linking to an embedded LNK stream
fields.encoding: hex string matching an OLE substream name

fields.name: embedded-lnk-stream
fields.note: full Shell Link structure per entry — shell-item list with volume-GUID / volume-label / MFT refs

fields.name: header-version
fields.note: 2 on most observed files

fields.name: category-count
fields.note: number of named pin-categories (e.g., 'Tasks', 'Recent', 'Pinned')

fields.name: category-name
fields.note: category labels seen in the taskbar jump-list UI

fields.name: entry-separator-magic
fields.encoding: fixed byte sequence (AB FB BF BA ...) separating items
fields.note: the magic separator is how parsers know where one LNK blob ends and the next begins

fields.name: embedded-lnk-blob
fields.location: raw LNK-format blob between magic separators
fields.note: full Shell Link per entry — MAC times, target path, tracker-block, volume info all present

fields.name: footer-magic
fields.encoding: AB FB BF BA + reserved bytes
fields.note: distinguishes 'clean end of file' from 'truncated/corrupted' — carvers use this

fields.name: appid
fields.location: value name in MuiCache / AutomaticDestinations filename / TaskbarLayout

fields.name: friendly-name
fields.note: human-readable app name rendered by Windows — resolves otherwise-opaque hex AppIDs

fields.name: entry-number
fields.note: monotonically increasing per-AppID; corresponds to the embedded-LNK stream name (hex of entry-number)

fields.name: pin-status
fields.note: -1 (0xFFFFFFFF) if pinned; 0 if not. Pinned = user-deliberate retention

fields.name: entry-string
fields.note: the user-visible display label for the jump-list entry — filename, URL, or custom string

fields.name: lnk-blob
fields.note: full ShellLNK structure — every field of the LNK substrate applies: target path, volume IDs, TrackerDataBlock, etc.

nodes.name: JumpList-PinnedItem
nodes.location-path: AutomaticDestinations-ms OR CustomDestinations-ms → entries with PinStatus ≠ 0

fields.name: pinned-target
fields.location: embedded LNK within the pinned entry

fields.name: pin-timestamp
fields.location: DestList LastAccessTime for the pinned entry
fields.note: LastAccessTime on a pinned entry freezes at last OPEN, not at pin time — pin date itself is not directly recorded

fields.name: arguments
