fields.note: arguments — key phishing indicator. A downloaded .lnk whose target is a LOLBin (powershell.exe, mshta.exe, cmd.exe) with a long argument string is a near-certain malicious-LNK

fields.name: icon-path
fields.note: attackers mismatch icon vs target (PDF icon + powershell.exe target) to deceive users

fields.name: zone-identifier
fields.location: Zone.Identifier:$DATA stream on the .lnk file itself
fields.note: MotW stamped by the browser at download time; ZoneId=3 = Internet

fields.name: volume-serial
fields.note: inherited from the embedded ShellLNK structure — attributes the LNK to a specific volume VSN at the moment of link creation

fields.name: volume-serial
fields.note: inherited from the embedded ShellLNK structure — attributes the LNK to a specific volume VSN at the moment of link creation

nodes.name: NetworkShare-LNK
nodes.location-path: any *.lnk whose LinkInfo.LocalBasePath is empty and NetworkPath is populated

fields.name: unc-target
fields.note: \\server\share\path — UNC target. Survives target-server disappearance.

fields.name: tracker-machine-id
fields.note: MachineID of the HOST that CREATED the LNK, not the target server. Reveals cross-host LNK movement.

fields.name: droid-volume-id
fields.note: NTFS ObjectID of the target's parent volume — joins with $ObjId artifact if the target server is in scope

fields.name: volume-serial
fields.note: inherited from the embedded ShellLNK structure — attributes the LNK to a specific volume VSN at the moment of link creation

fields.name: target-path
fields.note: document path opened through Office apps

fields.name: volume-serial
fields.note: inherited from the embedded ShellLNK structure — attributes the LNK to a specific volume VSN at the moment of link creation

fields.name: target-path
fields.note: full path to the file the user opened

fields.name: tracker-machine-id
fields.note: NetBIOS name of the host that created the LNK — survives copy, shows cross-host provenance

fields.name: lnk-file-mac
fields.update-rule: LNK file is created on first target open, rewritten on subsequent opens (MAC reset)

fields.name: target-file-mac
fields.location: LNK header FileAttributes + Target's $SI captured in LNK
fields.note: snapshot of target's MAC times at the moment the LNK was created/rewritten

fields.name: link-flags
fields.note: declares which optional sections follow (LinkInfo, Strings, ExtraData, etc.) — parser must honor these bits

fields.name: target-creation-time
fields.note: frozen snapshot of target's CreationTime — not updated on LNK re-access
fields.clock: system (target-file $SI at time of LNK creation)

fields.name: target-access-time
fields.note: frozen snapshot of target's AccessTime — not updated on LNK re-access

fields.name: target-write-time
fields.note: frozen snapshot of target's WriteTime — not updated on LNK re-access

fields.name: target-file-size
fields.note: low 32 bits of target size at LNK creation; truncates for >4 GB files

fields.name: link-target-idlist
fields.location: optional — follows header when HasLinkTargetIDList bit set
fields.note: full shell-namespace chain to target; embeds volume-GUID when target is on a removable volume; embeds MFT entry+sequence when target is a file or folder on NTFS (from shell-item extension blocks)

fields.name: local-base-path
fields.note: canonical filesystem path to target

fields.name: drive-type
fields.note: DRIVE_UNKNOWN|DRIVE_NO_ROOT_DIR|DRIVE_REMOVABLE|DRIVE_FIXED|DRIVE_REMOTE|DRIVE_CDROM|DRIVE_RAMDISK

fields.name: fs-volume-serial
fields.note: 32-bit FS serial; regenerated on reformat — see FilesystemVolumeSerial concept

fields.name: network-share-name
fields.note: present when target was on a network share (\\server\share\...)

fields.name: name-string
fields.note: human-readable description; often the target's display name

fields.name: relative-path
fields.note: relative path from LNK location to target — survives target-path changes that absolute path doesn't

fields.name: command-line-arguments
fields.note: arguments that would be passed to the target executable; attackers stage LNK-based lures here

fields.name: tracker-machine-id
fields.note: NetBIOS name of the host whose DLT service stamped this LNK — travels with the file

fields.name: tracker-droid
fields.note: DLT object identifier — second GUID is the volume identifier, first is the object identifier

fields.name: tracker-droid-birth
fields.note: original DLT identifier at creation; differs from tracker-droid if the object was copied/moved through DLT-aware hops

fields.name: target-path
fields.note: executable (or script) launched at logon

fields.name: arguments
fields.note: command-line arguments — key for PowerShell/VBS downloaders that launch via wscript.exe or powershell.exe in Startup-LNK

fields.name: icon-path
fields.note: attackers sometimes mismatch icon vs target to deceive users scanning Startup folder

fields.name: tracker-machine-id
fields.note: if non-local, indicates the LNK was created on another host and copied in (supply-chain or lateral drop)

fields.name: volume-serial
fields.note: inherited from the embedded ShellLNK structure — attributes the LNK to a specific volume VSN at the moment of link creation

fields.name: stream-name
fields.note: user-visible form: filename:streamname. Zone.Identifier is the best-known; arbitrary names allowed.

fields.name: stream-content
fields.note: up to resident-size threshold stored in MFT; larger streams get non-resident cluster runs

fields.name: cluster-state
fields.note: volume_size_bytes / cluster_size / 8 bytes long; parses to a per-cluster allocation map

nodes.name: Boot
nodes.location-path: <root>\$Boot (MFT entry 7; occupies first cluster of volume)
nodes.exit-attribution-sentence: The $Boot metadata file contains the volume signature, the BIOS parameter block, and the boot loader (Metz, 2021).

fields.name: bytes-used
fields.note: total bytes owned by this SID on this volume

nodes.name: Extended-Attributes
nodes.title-description: NTFS Extended Attributes ($EA / $EA_INFORMATION) — named key/value pairs attached to a file

fields.name: ea-name
fields.note: Name of the EA entry. Well-known Windows EAs: $KERNEL.PURGE.ESBCACHE, $TXF_DATA (Transactional NTFS), WSL-related ($LXATTRB). Attacker EAs use custom names — any unfamiliar EA name on a user-writable file = investigate.

fields.name: ea-value
fields.note: The key/value data. Limited to ~64KB per entry but attackers can split payload across multiple EAs on one file. Reading requires NTFS-aware parser (standard file APIs expose EAs only via specialized APIs — most analyst tools miss them unless explicitly requested).

fields.name: ea-size
fields.note: Size of the value. Total EA data per file tracked in $EA_INFORMATION attribute (UnpackedEaSize + PackedEaSize fields). Large or anomalous EA size on a benign-looking file = suspicion.

fields.name: ea-information
fields.note: Aggregate header attribute summarizing EA state on the file. Required for certain OS/2 compatibility behaviors; exists only when file has EAs. Presence = file has EAs.

fields.name: index-entry
fields.note: each allocated entry is a directory listing — filename, MFT reference, $SI and $FN timestamps, file size, attribute flags

fields.name: referenced-mft
fields.note: MFT reference of the file/subfolder this entry points at

fields.name: $SI-timestamps
fields.note: snapshot of the referenced file's $SI times AT THE MOMENT the index was written

fields.name: $FN-timestamps
fields.note: snapshot of $FN times at the moment of the last rename/creation

fields.name: slack-entry
fields.note: THE forensic payoff — deleted filenames + their timestamps remain intact in slack until the node is rebalanced by B-tree operations

nodes.name: LogFile-T-Stream
nodes.location-path: <volume>\$LogFile:$T (NTFS system file stream, MFT entry 2 alternate data stream)
nodes.title-description: NTFS $LogFile:$T companion stream — metadata for the NTFS transaction log

fields.name: log-metadata-blob
fields.note: Binary blob carrying NTFS log-state metadata including restart-area references and LSN bookmarks. Format is not publicly documented by Microsoft; community parsers handle it. Directly inspectable in hex but meaningful only in context with the main $LogFile stream.

fields.name: log-stream-coupling
fields.location: implicit — $T is coupled to the parent $LogFile by its alternate-data-stream nature
fields.note: The $T stream cannot be acquired independently — it lives on MFT entry 2 as an ADS of $LogFile. Correct acquisition captures both streams together. Accidentally acquiring only the default $LogFile data stream misses $T.

fields.name: stream-size
fields.note: Stream size is small relative to the main $LogFile (which is typically 64 MB default). $T is a few KB to tens of KB.

fields.name: restart-area
fields.note: checkpoint metadata — last-flushed LSN, active transaction list

fields.name: log-record
fields.note: per-transaction redo+undo pair recording a single NTFS metadata change (MFT entry update, directory-index insert, attribute list update, etc.)

fields.name: redo-op
fields.note: SetNewAttributeSizes / CreateAttribute / DeleteAttribute / SetBitsInNonresidentBitMap / OpenNonresidentAttribute / AddIndexEntryRoot / DeleteIndexEntryAllocation / ...

fields.name: lsn
fields.note: monotonically-increasing log sequence number; provides strict ordering of filesystem operations

fields.name: target-file-reference
fields.note: the $MFT record this transaction acted upon

fields.name: transaction-scope
fields.note: groups related log records; partial transactions on a dirty volume are the recovery target

fields.name: sequence-number
fields.location: FILE record header — incremented on record reuse

fields.name: logfile-sequence-number
fields.note: Points to the last $LogFile transaction affecting this record. Enables the Triforce correlation pattern (Cowen HECFBlog) — MFT record LSN links directly into the $LogFile transaction chain, which in turn cross-references $UsnJrnl RecordNumbers for that transaction. Gives full transaction-level change history for any file.

fields.name: hard-link-count
fields.note: number of directory entries referencing this record. >1 means hard-linked file (common for system DLLs and some installer-managed files); 0 on deleted records after unlink.

fields.name: base-record-reference
fields.note: Zero if this IS a base record. Non-zero if this is an extension record whose base lives elsewhere — set when a file's attributes overflow a single 1024-byte MFT record and spill into extension records linked via $ATTRIBUTE_LIST. When parsing, always check this before interpreting a record standalone.

fields.name: filename-namespace
fields.note: 0 = POSIX (case-sensitive, any-char), 1 = Win32 (case-insensitive Unicode), 2 = DOS (8.3 short name), 3 = Win32+DOS (combined). Files with a Win32 long filename and a DOS 8.3 alias have TWO $FILE_NAME attributes — one with namespace=1, one with namespace=2. Forensic consequence: parsers that stop after the first $FILE_NAME miss the alias; investigations looking for obfuscated short-name references need to enumerate ALL $FILE_NAME attributes per record.

fields.name: record-header-signature
fields.encoding: 'FILE' FOURCC (deleted records may show 'BAAD' if corrupted)

fields.name: in-use-flag
fields.note: 0x01 = record in use, 0x02 = directory; absence of 0x01 = deleted

fields.name: si-accessed
fields.note: atime updates may be disabled via NtfsDisableLastAccessUpdate registry — default varies by Windows version

fields.name: si-mft-changed
fields.note: MFT-record metadata change time — updates on any $MFT attribute change, even without file content change

fields.name: fn-created
fields.note: harder to forge than $SI timestamps — requires kernel-level access

fields.name: parent-mft-reference
fields.note: references the PARENT DIRECTORY's MFT record — walk these to reconstruct full path

fields.name: is-resident-data
fields.note: small files (<~700 bytes) have $DATA resident within the MFT record itself; larger files reference external clusters

fields.name: file-attributes
fields.note: READONLY, HIDDEN, SYSTEM, DIRECTORY, ARCHIVE, etc. (standard DOS attributes)

fields.name: object-id
fields.note: assigned to a file when a shell operation (like creating a LNK to it) requests an ObjectID. Persists for the file's lifetime on this volume.

fields.name: birth-object-id
fields.note: original ObjectID at the moment the ObjectID was first assigned — survives volume-change / file-move if DLT is enabled

fields.name: reparse-index-entry
fields.note: per-reparse-point entry — reparse tag + MFT entry reference + data pointer

fields.name: reparse-tag
fields.note: identifies reparse-point type — IO_REPARSE_TAG_SYMLINK (0xA000000C), IO_REPARSE_TAG_MOUNT_POINT (0xA0000003), IO_REPARSE_TAG_DEDUP (0x80000013), IO_REPARSE_TAG_CLOUD (0x9000001A for OneDrive placeholders), IO_REPARSE_TAG_WOF (0x80000017 for Windows Overlay / compressed files)

fields.name: target-path
fields.location: the reparse point's REPARSE_DATA_BUFFER in the referenced file
fields.note: actual symlink/junction target, dedup reference, or cloud placeholder path

nodes.name: Secure-SDS
nodes.exit-attribution-sentence: The $Secure metadata file contains the security descriptors used for access control (Metz, 2021).
nodes.title-description: NTFS $Secure:$SDS stream — the per-file security descriptor (ACL) store on an NTFS volume

fields.name: security-descriptor-blob
fields.note: Each record: header (hash + SecurityId + offset + size) + SELF_RELATIVE_SECURITY_DESCRIPTOR blob (Owner SID + Group SID + DACL + SACL). Blob can be parsed to recover per-file ACL. Since $SDS holds UNIQUE SDs, many files typically share one record (common ACLs for user-profile subtrees, Program Files, etc.).

fields.name: security-id
fields.note: Per-MFT-entry index into $SDS. MFT entry's SecurityId joined to $SDS record produces the file's ACL. When a file's ACL is changed, Windows may allocate a new SecurityId + append new $SDS record — the OLD SD may remain in $SDS until the volume reuses the space. DFIR recovery: compare MFT entry's current SecurityId to historical values (if archived) OR scan $SDS for SIDs matching incident-relevant accounts.

fields.name: sd-hash
fields.note: Used for deduplication — Windows hashes each new SD and consults $SDH index to reuse an existing SDS record when hash matches. Not typically a DFIR pivot directly but confirms a given SD is shared across multiple files.

fields.name: orphaned-sd
fields.location: $SDS records with no corresponding SecurityId in the live MFT
fields.note: SDs that no file currently references — but still in $SDS. These are ACL history: SDs that were applied to files that have been deleted OR had their ACL changed. Scanning orphan SDs recovers deleted-file-ACLs (including the attacker user that owned them) that no longer exist in the live filesystem.

fields.name: owner-sid
fields.location: inside SD blob — Owner SID field
fields.note: SID of the file owner at SD-write time. Recovering historical owner SIDs from $SDS lets analysts identify which users created / owned specific files (including deleted ones). An attacker SID owning files in an unexpected directory = evidence of their prior activity.

fields.name: dacl-aces
fields.location: inside SD blob — DACL → per-ACE Trustee SID + access mask
fields.note: Access Control Entries granting / denying access. Historical ACL reconstruction from $SDS reveals e.g., 'Everyone Full Control' ACLs that an attacker applied to stage a file-share attack, even if those ACLs have since been normalized.

fields.name: file-volume-stream
fields.location: NTFS volume $Secure file — accessed via raw NTFS read, not standard file API
fields.note: Access requires NTFS-level file reader (dd / FTK Imager / libtsk). Standard Windows APIs can't expose this stream directly.

nodes.name: UsnJrnl-Max-Stream
nodes.title-description: NTFS $UsnJrnl:$Max stream — Update Sequence Number journal header / sizing metadata

fields.name: maximum-size
fields.note: Maximum journal size in bytes before Windows starts dropping the oldest records. Default varies by volume size but typically 32 MB (small volumes) to 128 MB (larger) to 1 GB (very large / server). Attacker-shrinking the journal (via fsutil usn createjournal with very small MaximumSize) forces rapid rollover and evidence loss without attracting the noise of a full journal delete.

fields.name: allocation-delta
fields.note: Increment added when the journal grows or rotates. Typically 8 MB. Controls granularity of rollover.

fields.name: usn-journal-id
fields.note: Creation timestamp of the current journal instance. Changes ONLY when the journal is recreated (fsutil usn deletejournal followed by creation, or filesystem reformat). If UsnJournalID is significantly newer than the volume's $MFT creation time, the journal was reset post-volume-creation — possibly attacker action to wipe USN evidence.

fields.name: first-usn
fields.note: FirstUsn = the lowest USN still retrievable in $J (rollover boundary). NextUsn = the next USN to be written. Gap = records lost to rollover. Journal-size + activity-rate + FirstUsn gives a rough retention window for the journal.

fields.name: max-stream-size
fields.note: $Max is itself very small (~32 bytes for the header struct). The size is fixed; growth only occurs via header-structure changes across Windows versions.

nodes.name: UsnJrnl
nodes.integrity-mechanism: USN counter monotonicity — gaps reveal edits

fields.name: usn
fields.note: monotonically increasing — serves as record identifier + chronological ordering

fields.name: parent-file-reference
fields.note: parent directory at time of event — useful for path reconstruction of deleted files

fields.name: timestamp
fields.note: when the change event occurred — kernel-timestamped

fields.name: reason-flags
fields.note: bitfield: DATA_OVERWRITE, DATA_EXTEND, DATA_TRUNCATION, NAMED_DATA_OVERWRITE, FILE_CREATE, FILE_DELETE, EA_CHANGE, SECURITY_CHANGE, RENAME_OLD_NAME, RENAME_NEW_NAME, INDEXABLE_CHANGE, BASIC_INFO_CHANGE, HARD_LINK_CHANGE, COMPRESSION_CHANGE, ENCRYPTION_CHANGE, OBJECT_ID_CHANGE, REPARSE_POINT_CHANGE, STREAM_CHANGE, CLOSE

fields.name: source-info
fields.note: bits indicating WHO caused the event (OS-initiated, replication, etc.) — mostly 0 for user activity

fields.name: filename
fields.note: filename at the moment of the event — for rename events paired with OLD_NAME/NEW_NAME reason flags

fields.name: zone-id
fields.note: 0=Local, 1=Intranet, 2=Trusted, 3=Internet, 4=Untrusted — browsers and mail clients tag downloads from Zone 3

fields.name: host-url
fields.note: the URL of the downloaded resource itself

fields.name: last-writer-package
fields.note: the app that wrote the file — e.g., 'Microsoft.MicrosoftEdge_8wekyb3d8bbwe'

fields.name: executable-name
fields.note: short form — the full path is in the filename-strings section; pair them for canonical ExecutablePath

fields.name: path-hash
fields.note: displayed as 8 hex chars in the filename; hashing algorithm varies by Windows version (XP=classical, Win7+=SSDT-style)

fields.name: last-run-time
fields.location: file-information struct — first element of run-times array
fields.update-rule: updated on each execution

fields.name: volume-device-path
fields.note: typically '\DEVICE\HARDDISKVOLUME<N>' — resolves via MountedDevices to drive letter / volume-GUID

fields.name: file-reference-list
fields.note: on Win8+, each referenced file carries its MFT entry+sequence — direct pivot to the $MFT at the time the prefetch was written

fields.name: loaded-filenames
fields.note: every file (DLL, config, data) the OS saw loaded during the first 10 seconds of execution — useful for DLL-sideload detection

nodes.name: Outlook-OST
nodes.exit-attribution-sentence: Adversaries may target user email on local systems to collect sensitive information (MITRE ATT&CK, n.d.).

fields.name: deleted-items-folder
fields.location: Deleted Items NBT node — often retains messages the user believed deleted

nodes.name: Outlook-PST
nodes.exit-attribution-sentence: Adversaries may target user email on local systems to collect sensitive information (MITRE ATT&CK, n.d.).

fields.name: internet-headers
fields.note: full RFC-5322 headers — contains Received chain, source IPs, SPF/DKIM results

fields.name: sent-time
fields.note: sender's clock — UNRELIABLE for chronology; use Received: header timestamps instead

fields.name: folder-path
fields.note: e.g., 'Inbox', 'Sent Items', 'Deleted Items', user-created subfolders

fields.name: attachment-content
fields.note: recoverable as original file; may be hash-matched against ExecutableHash concept if PE

nodes.name: Recycle-Bin-INFO2
nodes.title-description: Legacy Recycle Bin INFO2 index (Windows 95 / 98 / Me / XP) — deleted-file metadata predating $I/$R format

fields.name: original-path
fields.note: Full original path of the deleted file (C:\Users\...\document.doc). Recovers the pre-delete filename AND the pre-delete directory. For cold-case investigations this is the direct evidence of what the user had in a location before deletion.

fields.name: record-index
fields.location: per-record at offset 4 — numeric record index
fields.note: Integer N that maps to the companion 'DCn' file in the same directory holding the actual deleted bytes. INFO2 record N ↔ DCn file — identical to the $I/$R relationship in modern format. Pair the INFO2 metadata to the DCn content for full recovery.

fields.name: deletion-time
fields.note: When the file was sent to the Recycle Bin (not original creation). The single-most-important pivot for incident timeline on legacy systems.

fields.name: original-size
fields.note: File size at deletion time. Joins to the DCn file size as consistency check.

fields.name: drive-byte
fields.note: Drive letter index (0=A, 1=B, 2=C...). Preserved even if the partition later changed drive-letter assignment — historical state.

fields.name: format-version
fields.note: 0x01 for Vista/7/8/8.1, 0x02 for Win10+

fields.name: deletion-time
fields.update-rule: set at the moment the file was moved to Recycle Bin

fields.name: original-path
fields.location: $I header offset 0x18 (v1 = fixed 520 bytes, v2 = path-length prefix + variable)
fields.note: full filesystem path the file occupied before deletion

fields.name: deleting-user-sid
fields.note: the SID of the account that deleted the file — cryptographically bound via folder ownership

nodes.name: Active-Setup
nodes.title-description: Active Setup — run-once-per-user command invoked by userinit.exe on first logon after stub change

fields.name: stub-path
fields.note: Command line executed by userinit.exe at logon trigger. Stock Microsoft Active Setup components reference signed Microsoft binaries under %SystemRoot% (ie4uinit.exe, themeui.exe). A StubPath pointing to rundll32.exe with an attacker DLL argument, or to a non-Microsoft binary, or to a script-interpreter + inline command = textbook attacker plant.

fields.name: component-name
fields.note: Human-readable component name. Stock components have descriptive names ('Internet Explorer Components', 'Windows Media Player', 'Themes Setup'). Attacker components sometimes have blank or generic Default values — worth checking against a baseline.

fields.name: version
fields.note: Dotted version string ('1,0,0,0' for legacy format or '1,0' newer). userinit compares HKLM.Version > HKCU.Version to decide whether StubPath fires. Incrementing this value in HKLM (attacker technique) forces the StubPath to re-fire for every user — including those who previously completed the component.

fields.name: is-installed
fields.note: 1 = active (process StubPath); 0 = inactive (skip). Stock components typically have IsInstalled=1. Setting IsInstalled=0 is a way to suppress execution without deleting the registration.

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.note: LastWrite on a component's subkey reflects registration or version-bump time. A fresh LastWrite not corresponding to a Windows feature install or OS update = drive-by plant.

fields.name: hkcu-version-marker
fields.note: Per-user 'last executed' stamp. Compare against HKLM.Version — if HKLM is newer (or HKCU entry missing), StubPath fires at next logon. For forensics, the ABSENCE of this value under a given user's NTUSER.DAT means that user has NOT yet received the component's StubPath execution.

nodes.name: AeDebug
nodes.title-description: Application Error Debugger (AeDebug) registry — auto-invoked debugger on process crash or WER escalation
nodes.integrity-mechanism: none; WER does not validate that the Debugger path is signed

fields.name: debugger-command
fields.note: Command line executed when WER triggers. Standard format contains %ld placeholders for PID and event handle. Legitimate Visual Studio entry: '"C:\WINDOWS\system32\vsjitdebugger.exe" -p %ld -e %ld -j 0x%p'. Attacker-authored entries replace the binary path with a dropper or injector — the crash of ANY application triggers attacker-code execution in the crashing process's security context.

fields.name: auto-flag
fields.note: 0 = prompt user before launching debugger; 1 = launch automatically without prompt. Auto=1 combined with an attacker-Debugger value = silent on-crash trigger. Auto=0 only fires on user interaction with the WER prompt, reducing attack surface but still viable.

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.note: LastWrite updates when Debugger / Auto values are written. Baseline a clean Windows install (no dev tooling) → this key should be empty or absent; any populated AeDebug on such a host = investigate.

fields.name: sibling-image-file-execution-options
fields.location: Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Image File Execution Options\<process>.exe\Debugger value
fields.note: Per-process debugger hijack — different mechanism, same concept. ImageFileExecutionOptions\<process.exe>\Debugger causes Windows to launch the specified debugger in place of the named process. Classic AccessibilityFeature / sticky-keys persistence (utilman.exe, sethc.exe). Sibling artifact worth checking alongside AeDebug.

fields.name: program-id
fields.note: installer-assigned program ID (hash of name+version+publisher)

fields.name: Name
fields.note: installed program display name (matches Add/Remove Programs)

fields.name: RootDirPath
fields.note: install location of the application

fields.name: InstallDateArpLastModified
fields.note: last Add/Remove-Programs touch — uninstall/repair tracking

fields.name: Source
fields.note: MsiInstaller | Win32-installer | Store | AddRemoveProgramsRegistry | Unknown

fields.name: Type
fields.note: Application / Patch / MsuPatch / LiveTile / AppX

fields.name: RegistryKeyPath
fields.note: Add/Remove Programs registry key backing this entry

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.update-rule: on install, uninstall, or major update

fields.name: file-id
fields.note: leading four zeros indicate SHA1 algorithm; the remaining 40 chars are the PE content SHA1

fields.name: name
fields.note: filename component

fields.name: original-file-name
fields.note: PE-declared original name — mismatches with `Name` are suspicious (renamed binaries)

fields.name: link-date
fields.note: from PE header — when the executable was compiled by its author, NOT when it ran here
fields.clock: PE compile time (external — NOT this system's clock)

fields.name: is-os-component
fields.note: useful baseline — filters out OS binaries during triage

fields.name: usn
fields.note: USN Journal number at time of Amcache entry creation

fields.name: binary-type
fields.note: 'pe32_i386' / 'pe32plus_amd64' / 'pe_arm64' etc.

fields.name: long-path-hash
fields.note: hash of the full path — distinguishes same-hash executables at different paths

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.update-rule: CRITICAL (Lagny/ANSSI 2019): on Win10 1709+ InventoryApplicationFile layout, subkey LastWriteTime = last run of compattelrunner.exe (Microsoft Compatibility Appraiser scheduled task), NOT last execution of the binary. The legacy Root\File\{VolumeGUID}\<FileID> layout (pre-October 2017 Amcache DLLs) used LastWriteTime to approximate first-execution — those semantics do not apply to the current layout.

fields.name: shortcut-id
fields.note: hash of the shortcut's resolved target path

fields.name: ShortcutPath
fields.note: full filesystem path of the .lnk file itself

fields.name: ShortcutTargetPath
fields.note: resolved target of the shortcut — the executable the shortcut points at

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.update-rule: when the shortcut was inventoried (creation or modification observed)

fields.name: container-id
fields.note: the same ContainerID that appears in InventoryDevicePnp, MountedDevices, MountPoints2, and WPD

fields.name: FriendlyName
fields.note: user-visible device name (e.g., 'Kingston DataTraveler', 'AirPods Pro', 'HP Color LaserJet')

fields.name: PrimaryCategory
fields.note: category taxonomy (Computer.Desktop / PrintFax.Printer / Communications.Bluetooth / ... )

fields.name: IsPaired
fields.note: Bluetooth/WiFi-direct paired devices get IsPaired=1; paired-but-absent devices still have the record

fields.name: DiscoveryMethod
fields.note: USB | Bluetooth | WSD | SSDP | PnPX

fields.name: Class
fields.note: DiskDrive | USB | HIDClass | PrintQueue | Net | Bluetooth | ...

fields.name: ClassGuid
fields.note: device setup class GUID

fields.name: Model
fields.note: human-readable device model name

fields.name: ContainerId
fields.note: cross-device pivot — same physical device gets the same ContainerId across its logical interfaces

fields.name: Parent
fields.note: parent device instance path — enables PnP tree reconstruction

fields.name: Service
fields.note: driver service name (usbstor, disk, volume, nlaapi, ...)

fields.name: InstallDate
fields.note: most-recent driver install or device-configure timestamp

fields.name: DriverName
fields.note: full path to the driver file (typically .sys)

fields.name: DriverId
fields.note: Microsoft-computed identifier for the driver

fields.name: DriverCompany
fields.note: signing publisher

fields.name: Service
fields.note: if the driver registers a service, its service-name — cross-references HKLM\SYSTEM\...\Services\<name>

fields.name: WdfVersion
fields.note: Windows Driver Framework version (null for pure-kernel drivers)

fields.name: Inf
fields.note: driver install INF file reference

fields.name: Signed
fields.note: 1 = driver signature verified; 0 = unsigned (test-signing / PatchGuard bypass setup)

fields.name: DriverLastWriteTime
fields.note: most-recent filesystem modification time of the driver binary

fields.name: DriverTimeStamp
fields.note: PE compile timestamp from the driver's header

fields.name: package-id
fields.note: INF package identifier (typically oem<N>.inf_<hash>)

fields.name: Inf
fields.note: INF file path (often %WINDIR%\INF\oem<N>.inf) — names the driver-install manifest that caused this driver package to be registered

fields.name: Class
fields.note: driver class (Display / Net / HIDClass / DiskDrive / USB / SCSIAdapter / ...)

fields.name: Date
fields.note: driver-package signing/build date as declared in the INF

fields.name: SubmissionId
fields.note: WHQL submission ID (if Microsoft-certified)

fields.name: Hwids
fields.note: hardware IDs the driver claims to support — useful for mapping driver to device

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.update-rule: driver-package install or reconfig

fields.name: dll-value
fields.location: REG_SZ values under AppCertDlls key; value name is arbitrary, data is a DLL path
fields.note: Every DLL listed here is loaded into ANY process that calls CreateProcess / CreateProcessAsUser / WinExec. Broadest-reach DLL-injection persistence in Windows.

nodes.name: AppInit-DLLs
nodes.integrity-mechanism: RequireSignedAppInit_DLLs setting (post-Win8) is a GUARDRAIL — but can be disabled by tampering

fields.name: appinit-dlls
fields.note: space-separated list of DLLs; EVERY user32-loading process loads them

fields.name: load-appinit-dlls
fields.note: 0 = disabled, 1 = enabled; must be 1 for AppInit_DLLs to actually load

fields.name: require-signed-appinit-dlls
fields.note: if 1, only Microsoft-signed DLLs load; default on Win8+ under Secure Boot

fields.name: default-path
fields.note: full path of the executable; registered app launcher. Start → Run or Win+R resolves exe name via App Paths.

fields.name: Path
fields.note: PATH prepended during launch — lets a registered app override process PATH

nodes.name: Audit-Policy
nodes.exit-attribution-sentence: Adversaries may disable Windows event logging to limit data that can be leveraged for detections and audits (MITRE ATT&CK, n.d.).

fields.name: audit-policy-blob
fields.encoding: packed per-subcategory flags — 53 subcategories on modern Windows, each with Success/Failure bits
fields.note: decoded gives the complete audit policy: which Security.evtx event categories are ON, OFF, or mixed

fields.name: AutoAdminLogon
fields.note: '1' = autologon enabled, '0' or missing = disabled

fields.name: DefaultUserName
fields.note: Account name used for auto-logon. Not a SID — joins to SAM.username or ProfileList.profile-image-path for SID resolution.

fields.name: DefaultDomainName
fields.note: Domain scope of the auto-logon account. For local accounts this is the machine NetBIOS name; for domain accounts, the AD DNS or NetBIOS domain.

fields.name: DefaultPassword
fields.location: DefaultPassword value (legacy plaintext) OR LSA-Secrets DefaultPassword (Vista+)
fields.note: Historically plaintext in HKLM\SOFTWARE\...\Winlogon\DefaultPassword. Modern configurations store it in LSA-Secrets — but legacy writes and some 3rd-party installers still use the plaintext slot.

fields.name: AutoLogonCount
fields.note: one-shot autologon enabled with count; decrements each logon

fields.name: user-scope-sid
fields.note: BAM's per-user scope is encoded directly in the key hierarchy — no ProfileList lookup needed

fields.name: executable-path
fields.note: the value name IS the full executable path — BAM uses the path as its key

fields.name: last-execution-time
fields.note: the canonical per-user per-exe last-run timestamp on Win10 1709+
fields.update-rule: set by the BAM kernel service on each execution observed

fields.name: value-trailer-bytes
fields.location: value data bytes 8–23 (exactly 24 bytes total per Suhanov/dfir.ru — bam.sys rejects anything else)
fields.note: record-format sentinel; the full record is exactly 24 bytes FILETIME + 16B trailer. Deviation from this layout is a tamper indicator.

fields.name: bam-key-last-write
fields.update-rule: updated when any value under <SID> is written — approximates 'most recent BAM activity for this user'

nodes.name: BCD-Store
nodes.exit-attribution-sentence: Adversaries may use bootkits to persist on systems (MITRE ATT&CK, n.d.).
nodes.title-description: Boot Configuration Data (BCD) store — registry-hive file controlling Windows bootmgr / winload paths
nodes.integrity-mechanism: hive-level checksum; no per-element signing. Secure Boot enforces signed boot images; BCD config itself is not signed.

fields.name: boot-entry-osdevice
fields.note: Device identifier of the partition the OS loader reads Windows from. Attacker modification of OSDevice on the active boot entry = redirect to a different partition (another-OS boot, rescue-environment boot). Usually a secondary tamper signal since direct OSDevice changes are visible in bcdedit output.

fields.name: boot-entry-path
fields.note: Path on the OSDevice to the OS loader (\Windows\system32\winload.efi or \Windows\system32\winload.exe). Redirecting this to an attacker binary = the single most privileged persistence on Windows because winload.efi runs before the OS itself (kernel-mode bootkit territory).

fields.name: bootmgr-path
fields.note: Path to bootmgfw.efi / bootmgr.exe itself. Change = attacker replaced the boot manager (full-chain bootkit).

fields.name: secure-boot-disable
fields.location: bcdedit /set testsigning on | disable-integrity-checks | loadoptions DISABLE_INTEGRITY_CHECKS
fields.note: testsigning=Yes / nointegritychecks=Yes / disable-vbs on the active boot entry = security mitigations disabled. Any of these on a production host without an explicit kernel-driver-development use case = deliberate bypass of driver-signing enforcement — usually a precursor to loading an unsigned (attacker-signed or self-signed) kernel driver.

fields.name: safeboot-alternateshell
fields.note: alternateshell=Yes puts safe-mode boot into 'safeboot with alternate shell' — cmd.exe in place of explorer.exe. Attacker persistence that uses safe-mode-with-alt-shell can control the system during safe-mode-based recovery sessions. Rare signal but high-value when present.

fields.name: recovery-sequence
fields.note: GUID pointing at the recovery environment BCD entry. Attacker-redirected recovery sequence points the Windows-Recovery entry at attacker tooling — triggered when the user holds Shift while clicking Restart or when automatic repair kicks in.

fields.name: bcd-file-mtime
fields.note: NTFS mtime of the BCD hive file. BCD is rarely modified in steady-state operation (OS install, feature upgrade, driver-install that changes boot, manual bcdedit). Any mtime change outside those events is forensically material.

nodes.name: BitLocker-FVE
nodes.exit-attribution-sentence: BitLocker is a Windows security feature that provides encryption for entire volumes, addressing the threats of data theft or exposure from lost, stolen, or inappropriately decommissioned devices (Microsoft, 2022).
nodes.title-description: BitLocker Full Volume Encryption registry metadata (recovery password protectors, key protector state)
nodes.integrity-mechanism: FVE metadata on the volume itself has HMAC; registry entries are unsigned

fields.name: encryption-method
fields.note: Cipher selection. 1 = AES128 (CBC); 2 = AES256 (CBC); 3 = AES128 XTS (modern default for OS/fixed/removable); 4 = AES256 XTS. The XTS variants are preferred on modern Windows. Enterprise policy may force a specific value — mismatched runtime state vs policy = misconfiguration.

fields.name: key-protector-guids
fields.location: CurrentControlSet\Services\FVE\ per-volume subkeys — KeyProtector GUIDs list
fields.note: GUIDs of key protectors applied to each BitLocker-protected volume. Key protector TYPES include: TPM only, TPM+PIN, TPM+Startup Key, Startup Key only, Password (non-OS volumes), Recovery Password (numerical 48-digit), Recovery Key (external .bek), AD DS recovery, Azure AD recovery, Auto-unlock. Each protector GUID in registry corresponds to a specific unlock method. Critical for investigations: 'Recovery Password' protector presence indicates a 48-digit numerical recovery key was generated and should be backed up somewhere (print, USB, AD, Azure AD).

fields.name: recovery-info-to-ad
fields.note: 1 = BitLocker recovery info was configured to be backed up to Active Directory (or Azure AD on newer hosts). Enterprise standard. If enabled, the recovery password is in AD's ms-FVE-RecoveryInformation attribute on the computer object — DFIR can recover it domain-side. If disabled on an enterprise machine, this is a policy anomaly worth flagging.

fields.name: disable-encryption-event
fields.note: Event IDs 745 (encryption started), 773 (decryption started), 24589 (recovery key backup succeeded), 24770 (key protector added), 24771 (key protector removed). Timeline anchors for BitLocker lifecycle events. NOT a registry field but the canonical cross-reference EVTX channel for interpreting FVE registry state.

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.note: LastWrite on FVE subkeys updates when encryption state or key-protector configuration changes. Pair with BitLocker EVTX events for a full lifecycle timeline.

fields.name: tpm-owner-info
fields.note: TPM ownership state. BitLocker's TPM-bound protectors require TPM ownership — TPM registry gives TPM-clear and TPM-ownership-change events useful for interpreting BitLocker key-protector rotations.

nodes.name: COM-HijackKeys
nodes.title-description: COM CLSID InprocServer32 / InprocHandler32 / LocalServer32 hijack registry

fields.name: clsid
fields.note: The COM class identifier being registered or hijacked. Well-known abused CLSIDs include {AB8902B4-09CA-4bb6-B78D-A8F59079A8D5} (COMCAT category manager), {42aedc87-2188-41fd-b9a3-0c966feabec1} (Shell.Explorer delegation), and IE's browser helper object CLSIDs.

fields.name: inproc-server32
fields.note: Path to the in-process DLL implementing the COM object. Loaded into whatever process instantiates the CLSID. Hijack = replace with attacker DLL; every caller of CoCreateInstance(<CLSID>) loads the attacker code.

fields.name: inproc-handler32
fields.note: DLL that provides lightweight local handling of a remote COM object. Less common than InprocServer32 but equally weaponizable; same load semantics when the CLSID is instantiated locally.

fields.name: local-server32
fields.note: Full executable path launched as its own process to host the COM object (out-of-process server). Attacker gets a new process spawning whenever the CLSID is activated; process name can be anything, persistence trigger is COM activation rather than boot/logon.

fields.name: threading-model
fields.note: 'Apartment' / 'Free' / 'Both' / 'Neutral'. Usually doesn't change across hijack; presence of a non-default ThreadingModel on a CLSID whose DLL path looks suspicious is a secondary signal.

fields.name: key-last-write-inproc
fields.note: Per-CLSID LastWrite under the InprocServer32 subkey. Post-install creation/modification of a CLSID registration is a higher-suspicion signal than the broad Classes\CLSID tree (which is touched continuously by legitimate software).

nodes.name: CommandProcessor-AutoRun
nodes.title-description: Command Processor AutoRun (HKLM / HKCU) — command-line executed on every cmd.exe start

fields.name: autorun-command
fields.note: Full command line run at every cmd.exe start. Any command / script / binary invocation here is effectively invoked once per cmd.exe launch. Attackers commonly set this to a PowerShell one-liner ('powershell -w hidden -enc ...') or a path to a dropper .bat.

fields.name: hive-scope
fields.note: HKLM AutoRun fires for every user's cmd.exe; HKCU fires only for the current user but can be set without admin. HKCU is more stealthy for an unprivileged attacker who wants persistence in their own session.

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.note: LastWrite on the Command Processor key moves when AutoRun is added / modified. Pair with Security-4688 / Sysmon-1 to catch the write process.

fields.name: disabled-flag
fields.note: Sibling values under Command Processor control other cmd.exe behaviors. Not themselves persistence but edits to this key (other than standard defaults) signal the key has been deliberately touched — worth baseline-comparing the whole key rather than only AutoRun.

fields.name: ComputerName
fields.note: current NetBIOS computer name; also mirrored under ActiveComputerName

fields.name: ActiveComputerName
fields.note: runtime value (may differ from persistent ComputerName after rename-pending)

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.update-rule: touched only on hostname change

nodes.name: Credential-Guard-State
nodes.title-description: Credential Guard / VBS / HVCI registry state — virtualization-based protection of LSA secrets
nodes.integrity-mechanism: UEFI-lock — registry-only tamper insufficient when LsaCfgFlags was set with UEFI lock

fields.name: lsa-cfg-flags
fields.note: Credential Guard enforcement flag. 0 = off; 1 = Enabled with UEFI lock; 2 = Enabled without UEFI lock. Microsoft baseline: 1 or 2 on all Windows 10/11 Enterprise systems. Attacker setting 0 + reboot + UEFI-clear-LsaCfgFlags-variable (if previously UEFI-locked) fully disables CG — then LSASS becomes dumpable.

fields.name: enable-virtualization-based-security
fields.note: Master switch for VBS (requires VBS for Credential Guard and HVCI). 1 = enabled; 0 = disabled. Setting 0 disables the foundation of CG + HVCI + other VBS features. Enterprise baseline: 1.

fields.name: hvci-value
fields.note: Hypervisor-Enforced Code Integrity — Memory Integrity feature in Windows Security UI. 1 = enabled (kernel-mode code-integrity enforced in hypervisor partition — blocks unsigned kernel drivers). 0 = disabled. Attacker disable prerequisite for loading unsigned kernel rootkit.

fields.name: required-security-properties
fields.note: Bitmask: 1 = Secure Boot required; 2 = Secure Boot + DMA protection required. Tightening value = stricter posture.

fields.name: secure-boot-state
fields.note: 1 = Secure Boot enabled; 0 = disabled. Secure Boot is foundational — without it, CG and HVCI are weaker because the boot chain isn't verified. Attacker-disabled Secure Boot preceded by UEFI-shell access = most invasive pre-OS tamper.

fields.name: running-security-services
fields.note: Runtime-observed state: which VBS services are ACTUALLY running. 1 = Credential Guard running; 2 = HVCI running. Differs from the configured-to-run state — this reports what the hypervisor actually enforced at boot. Useful for catching configuration-vs-runtime drift (policy enables CG but hardware lacks support → it's configured but not running).

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.note: LastWrite on DeviceGuard or Lsa subkeys = policy-change time. Cross-reference with reboot events — CG enable/disable takes effect on next boot. A LastWrite followed by reboot = tamper cycle candidate.

fields.name: uefi-lsa-cfg-variable
fields.location: UEFI variable LsaCfgFlags (outside registry — UEFI firmware NVRAM)
fields.note: When LsaCfgFlags = 1 was configured, Windows sets a corresponding UEFI variable that re-asserts the setting on boot even if the registry is cleared. To truly disable UEFI-locked CG, the attacker must boot to UEFI shell and clear the variable — high-noise operation.

nodes.name: CredentialProviders
nodes.title-description: Credential Providers and Credential Provider Filters (Vista+ logon-UI plugin DLLs)

fields.name: credential-provider-clsids
fields.note: Each subkey GUID identifies a registered Credential Provider. Default providers on Win10: Picture Password, PIN, Password, Smart Card, Windows Hello Face/Fingerprint. Attacker-registered GUID = custom provider that can intercept or supply credentials during logon.

fields.name: credential-provider-dll
fields.note: The DLL actually loaded for the CLSID. The GUID → DLL resolution goes via the COM class registration under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Classes\CLSID. A Credential Provider CLSID pointing to a DLL outside %SystemRoot%\System32 is high-suspicion.

fields.name: credential-provider-name
fields.note: Human-readable label displayed in the logon UI (e.g., 'PasswordProvider', 'PicturePasswordLogonProvider'). Attackers sometimes register with names that mimic legitimate providers.

fields.name: credential-provider-filter-clsids
fields.note: Credential Provider Filters are a SEPARATE hook that can HIDE or ENABLE specific providers at logon time. An attacker registering a filter can suppress legitimate providers (forcing users to use the attacker's) or cloak their own provider from the UI.

fields.name: credential-provider-filter-dll
fields.location: Credential Provider Filters\<CLSID>\(Default) — resolved via HKLM\SOFTWARE\Classes\CLSID
fields.note: DLL implementing the filter. Same resolution path as providers; same suspicion profile for non-system32 paths.

nodes.name: Credentials-cached
nodes.exit-attribution-sentence: Adversaries may attempt to access cached domain credentials used to allow authentication to occur in the event a domain controller is unavailable (MITRE ATT&CK, n.d.).

fields.name: cached-user-sid
fields.note: SID of the domain user whose credentials were cached

fields.name: cached-email-identity
fields.location: cache entry — UPN or email-form logon name
fields.note: UPN form (user@domain.com) for modern domain accounts — email-form identity

fields.name: mscache-hash
fields.note: MSCachev1 (pre-Vista) or MSCachev2 (Vista+); NOT reversible — offline cracking via hashcat mode 1100 (v1) or 2100 (v2)

fields.name: last-written
fields.location: cache entry or Cache subkey LastWrite
fields.note: approximates last-successful-domain-logon for this account

fields.name: secret-name
fields.note: e.g., "$MACHINE.ACC" (machine account), "DefaultPassword" (autologon), service-name for service credentials

fields.name: executable-path
fields.location: values named by full EXE path under each SID subkey

fields.name: last-executed-time
fields.update-rule: on each tracked foreground execution under Connected-Standby

nodes.name: Defender-ASR-Rules
nodes.title-description: Microsoft Defender Attack Surface Reduction (ASR) rule enforcement state + Network Protection
nodes.integrity-mechanism: Tamper Protection on Win10/11 Enterprise (when enabled via Intune / Defender portal) blocks ASR registry changes

fields.name: asr-rule-state
fields.note: Enforcement mode for a specific ASR rule: '0' = Disabled (not enforcing), '1' = Block (enforce and block), '2' = Audit (log but don't block), '6' = Warn (user prompt). Microsoft recommends Block mode for all ASR rules on hardened endpoints. Attacker turning '1' → '0' silently disables a specific rule — targeted defense-evasion. Commonly-targeted: LSASS credential-theft rule (9e6c4e1f-7d60-472f-ba1a-a39ef669e4b2), Office-macro-creates-child-process rule (D4F940AB-401B-4EFC-AADC-AD5F3C50688A), untrusted-USB rule (b2b3f03d-6a65-4f7b-a9c7-1c7ef74a9ba4).

fields.name: asr-rule-guid
fields.note: Microsoft-documented rule GUID. Each GUID maps to a specific behavior. Microsoft's ASR rules page lists all current GUIDs with their rule descriptions. DFIR should baseline a host's full rule-GUID enumeration against Microsoft's published list to catch: (1) expected rule entirely absent, (2) expected rule in Audit/Disabled mode when Block is baseline, (3) unexpected/fake rule entries.

fields.name: asr-enabled-master
fields.note: Master enable flag for ASR rule enforcement. 1 = enforcement active; 0 = ASR rules registry honored but NO enforcement. Attacker setting this to 0 effectively disables all ASR regardless of per-rule state. Sneaky — per-rule inspection still shows Block mode, but the master flag is off.

fields.name: network-protection-state
fields.location: Windows Defender Exploit Guard\Network Protection\EnableNetworkProtection value
fields.note: 0 = disabled; 1 = enabled (Block); 2 = Audit. Blocks outbound connections to Microsoft-reputation-bad domains/IPs. Attacker setting 0 disables Network Protection — attacker C2 traffic unimpeded by Defender network reputation. Microsoft baseline: 1.

fields.name: controlled-folder-state
fields.location: Windows Defender Exploit Guard\Controlled Folder Access\EnableControlledFolderAccess value
fields.note: 0 = disabled; 1 = enabled (Block mode — protected folders cannot be written except by allow-listed apps); 2 = Audit; 3 = Block disk modifications only; 4 = Audit disk modifications only. Anti-ransomware feature. Attacker setting 0 before ransomware encryption = removes the protection.

fields.name: controlled-folder-allowlist
fields.location: Windows Defender Exploit Guard\Controlled Folder Access\AllowedApplications (REG_MULTI_SZ of app paths)
fields.note: Applications explicitly allowed to write to protected folders. Attacker-added entry = attacker binary allow-listed to bypass Controlled Folder Access.

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.note: LastWrite on ASR / Network Protection / CFA keys updates on state changes. Cross-reference with Security-4688 / Sysmon-1 for reg.exe / PowerShell / WMI-Set-MpPreference process-creation events.

nodes.name: Defender-Exclusions
nodes.title-description: Microsoft Defender scan exclusion registry (Paths / Processes / Extensions / IpAddresses)
nodes.integrity-mechanism: Tamper Protection (Win10 1903+; Intune or Defender UI enabled) blocks non-TrustedInstaller writes

fields.name: excluded-paths
fields.note: Value NAME is the full path or glob pattern excluded (e.g., 'C:\Users\*\AppData\Local\Temp\'). Value DATA is 0x00000000. Every excluded path bypasses real-time + scheduled scanning. Attacker-added exclusions nearly always precede payload drop.

fields.name: excluded-processes
fields.note: Value NAME is a process executable name (e.g., 'mimikatz.exe'). Defender will not scan file activity initiated by that process. Classic evasion: exclude the attacker's dropper's process name.

fields.name: excluded-extensions
fields.note: Value NAME is a file extension (e.g., '.enc', '.dat'). Files with that extension aren't scanned. Attacker drops payload with the excluded extension, then renames at execution time.

fields.name: excluded-ip-addresses
fields.note: Value NAME is an IP address whose outbound/inbound traffic Defender Network Inspection skips. Newer key; exclusions here are unusual even in legitimate deployments.

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.location: Exclusions key metadata (per subkey: Paths, Processes, Extensions, IpAddresses)
fields.note: LastWrite on any Exclusions subkey matching the suspected attack window = tamper indicator.

fields.name: disable-real-time-monitoring
fields.note: 1 = real-time protection disabled. Complementary tamper indicator — often set alongside exclusion additions. Blocked by Tamper Protection when enabled.

fields.name: disable-anti-spyware
fields.note: 1 = Defender fully disabled (pre-TP). Modern Windows 10/11 ignores this unless Tamper Protection is off.

nodes.name: DNS-NRPT
nodes.title-description: Name Resolution Policy Table (NRPT) registry — per-namespace DNS redirection rules

fields.name: namespace
fields.note: DNS namespace the rule applies to. Formats: '.corp.example.com' (suffix match), 'exact.example.com' (exact), '*.example.com' (wildcard), 'example.com' (exact without dot). Attacker-authored rule targeting a specific sensitive domain = redirect that domain's queries to attacker infrastructure while leaving other DNS normal.

fields.name: generic-dns-servers
fields.note: DNS server(s) to route the matching namespace's queries to. Multiple servers allowed. An attacker IP here + sensitive namespace in Name = queries for sensitive domains go to attacker server → attacker sees internal DNS patterns + can serve malicious A records.

fields.name: doh-template
fields.note: For rules using DNS-over-HTTPS: the DoH template URL (e.g., https://attacker.example/dns-query). Attacker-configured DoH target bypasses enterprise DNS telemetry AND enterprise TLS inspection (DoH goes over HTTPS to a single server — looks like normal HTTPS traffic).

fields.name: dnssec-validation
fields.note: 1 = require DNSSEC validation for this namespace; 0 = don't require. Attacker disabling DNSSEC validation for a specific namespace = allows spoofed responses to be accepted.

fields.name: config-options
fields.note: Bitmask of rule options — encryption requirement, lookahead, custom resolver behavior. See Microsoft DnsClient CSP docs for flag meanings.

fields.name: rule-version
fields.note: Internal version counter. Advances on rule edit.

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.note: LastWrite on DnsPolicyConfig or per-rule subkey advances when a rule is added / modified / removed. Correlate with Security-4688 for the reg.exe / PowerShell Set-DnsClientNrptRule invocation.

fields.name: ttl-remaining
fields.note: seconds remaining before cache expiry; 0 = just-resolved or permanently cached

fields.name: cache-observed-timestamp
fields.encoding: examiner-set; not preserved in cache itself
fields.note: DNS cache entries don't carry the query timestamp directly; examiner must record when they captured the state

fields.name: volume-identifier-string
fields.location: subkey name — long composite string embedding DeviceSerial + VolumeLabel + FilesystemVolumeSerial
fields.note:
Canonical format (Vista–11):
  _??_USBSTOR#Disk&Ven_<Vendor>&Prod_<Product>&Rev_<Revision>#<USBSTOR-Instance-ID>#{53f56307-b6bf-11d0-94f2-00a0c91efb8b}<VolumeLabel>_<VolumeSerialDecimal>
Four extractable identifiers in ONE subkey name:
  (1) USBSTOR instance id — the hardware serial (or OS-synthesized &0 suffix)
  (2) Class GUID {53f56307-...} — DiskDrive class, constant
  (3) VolumeLabel — shell-visible label at evaluation time
  (4) VolumeSerial — DECIMAL uint32 (NOT hex); convert to hex to join other artifacts
Parser: split on '#' then on final two '_' (VolumeLabel may contain embedded _).
The safest parse is to anchor on the trailing digits (VolumeSerial) and the class-GUID.

fields.name: volume-label
fields.location: subkey name — segment between final class-GUID and final '_<decimal>'
fields.note: Duplicated link to VolumeLabel from the composite string, broken out for parser clarity. Shell-visible volume label at ReadyBoost evaluation time.

fields.name: volume-serial-number
fields.location: final numeric segment of the subkey name (after the last '_')
fields.encoding: decimal (NOT hex) — EMDMgmt stores the FS VSN as a decimal integer (uint32). Other artifacts (LNK, Prefetch, Partition/Diagnostic Vbr0) store it hex. Convert for cross-artifact join: dec→hex, zero-pad to 8 chars, format XXXX-XXXX.
fields.note:
Common parser pitfall. Example: EMDMgmt value `3472651852` converts to
hex `0xCF08D94C` → conventional display `CF08-D94C`. That hex form is
what appears in LNK `DriveSerialNumber`, Prefetch volumes section, and
Partition/Diagnostic-1006 Vbr0 offset 0x48/0x43/0x64.

fields.name: usbstor-serial
fields.location: subkey name — segment after `USBSTOR#Disk&Ven_...&Prod_...&Rev_...#` and before the next `#{classGUID}`
fields.note: Broken out for parser clarity. Matches the <InstanceID> in USBSTOR keys byte-for-byte. If 2nd char is '&' the serial is OS-synthesized (not device-firmware reported) — same rule as USBSTOR.

fields.name: cache-status
fields.note:
ReadyBoost eligibility/configuration status for this volume.
Common observed values (not all publicly documented):
  0 = not evaluated / pending
  1 = eligible + enabled (ReadyBoost cache active)
  2 = eligible but not configured
  3 = ineligible
Presence of this value = Windows ran the ReadyBoost test on this device.

fields.name: cache-size-bytes
fields.note: Active ReadyBoost cache size in bytes. Non-zero only when cache-status = 1. Forensically secondary — indicates cache configuration.

fields.name: device-status
fields.note: Per-device ReadyBoost eligibility result. Distinct from cache-status — one is device eligibility, the other is cache state.

fields.name: read-speed-kbs
fields.encoding: uint32 — read throughput in KiB/s observed during ReadyBoost performance test
fields.note: Kernel-measured device read performance. Forensically circumstantial but can distinguish flash classes (USB 2.0 ~20-40 MB/s, USB 3.0 ~80-200 MB/s).

fields.name: write-speed-kbs
fields.note: Kernel-measured device write throughput. Together with read-speed, fingerprints the device performance profile.

fields.name: physical-device-size
fields.note:
Byte-accurate device capacity. Cross-references Partition/Diagnostic-1006
EventData\Capacity for the same device. Two devices with matching USB
vendor/product/revision but different PhysicalDeviceSize values are
genuinely different hardware.

fields.name: failure-reason
fields.location: subkey value `FailureReason` (present when device failed ReadyBoost test)
fields.note: Reason code for ReadyBoost ineligibility — e.g., too small, too slow, on system drive. Presence at all = Windows evaluated the device. Absence could mean pass OR never-evaluated.

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.note:
Subkey last-write time corresponds to when ReadyBoost last updated
this device's entry — typically first evaluation on initial plug-in,
re-evaluation if device characteristics changed, or cache reconfiguration.
NOT a per-connection timestamp like USBSTOR's FIRST/LAST INSTALL.
fields.update-rule: set-on-any-value-change-within-key

nodes.name: ETW-Autologger
nodes.title-description: ETW Autologger registry — persistent Event Tracing for Windows trace sessions (EDR / Defender / attacker-blinding)
nodes.integrity-mechanism: none — no signing of Autologger session / provider registrations

fields.name: session-start
fields.note: 1 = session starts on boot (default for active sessions). 0 = session disabled at boot. Attacker setting this to 0 on an EDR-critical session silently stops that session from recording telemetry — the security product may still RUN but its ETW data source is empty. Classic blinding technique (T1562.002).

fields.name: session-guid
fields.note: Session's GUID. Joins with runtime ETW session enumeration (logman query -ets) for live-state comparison.

fields.name: provider-subkeys
fields.location: Autologger\<session>\{provider-guid}\ subkey — one per enabled provider
fields.note: Each subkey under a session represents an ETW provider enabled for that session. Key name is the provider's GUID. Deleting a provider subkey UNREGISTERS the provider from the session — the provider's events no longer reach this session's consumer. For EDR-blinding, attackers don't disable the whole session (which is noisy) — they remove specific provider subkeys from the session.

fields.name: provider-enabled
fields.note: 1 = provider active in session; 0 = provider present but disabled. More subtle than full deletion; the provider subkey exists (so a casual registry inspection sees it) but is dormant. Attacker blinding vector for providers they want to silently suppress.

fields.name: provider-matchanykeyword
fields.note: Bitmask of provider keyword groups this session subscribes to. Setting MatchAnyKeyword=0 effectively disables keyword filtering causing the provider to match NO events — even more subtle blinding, as the provider appears 'enabled' but nothing passes the keyword filter.

fields.name: known-critical-sessions
fields.note: EventLog-System / EventLog-Application / EventLog-Security — Windows Event Log EVTX ingestion sessions (disabling breaks EVTX!). DefenderAuditLogger / DefenderApiLogger / Microsoft-Antimalware-* — Defender telemetry. DiagLog / WdiContextLog — diagnostic tracing. Third-party: EDR sessions installed by the EDR vendor under names like 'CrowdStrike-Sensor-*', 'CylanceOptics-*', 'CbEventLog-*'. Inventory against expected vendor set.

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.note: LastWrite on a session's subkey = configuration change time. Disabling a session (Start=0 write) or unregistering a provider (subkey delete) moves this timestamp. Correlate with incident timeline to pinpoint tamper moment.

fields.name: AppSwitched-entry
fields.note: increments when user clicks the taskbar icon of an app already in background (focus-change count)

fields.name: AppLaunch-entry
fields.note: increments when the user clicks the taskbar icon to launch the app (cold-start count)

fields.name: ShowJumpView-entry
fields.note: increments when the user opens the jump list of the app (right-click)

fields.name: TrayButtonClicked-entry
fields.location: TrayButtonClicked → values named by system-tray applet identifier

fields.name: AppBadgeUpdated-entry
fields.note: notification-badge update events per app

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.update-rule: touched on each counter increment (any AppID under the subkey)

nodes.name: FirewallRules
nodes.exit-attribution-sentence: Adversaries may disable or modify system firewalls in order to bypass controls limiting network usage (MITRE ATT&CK, n.d.).

fields.name: rule-name
fields.location: REG_SZ VALUE NAME under FirewallRules key
fields.note: value names are rule IDs — either friendly names or {GUIDs}

fields.name: rule-definition
fields.note: attacker-created rules commonly set Action=Allow + Dir=Out + RA=* to whitelist outbound C2 traffic

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.note: subkey-level; records only the most-recent rule-list mutation

fields.name: Debugger
fields.note: executable launched in place of the original when the named binary is invoked — classic hijack primitive

fields.name: GlobalFlag
fields.note: when set to 0x200 (FLG_MONITOR_SILENT_PROCESS_EXIT) combined with SilentProcessExit key elsewhere, triggers external program launch on process exit

fields.name: MitigationOptions
fields.note: per-process EMET/Defender exploit-guard overrides; attacker use is to DISABLE protections for a target binary

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.update-rule: on any IFEO write for that binary

nodes.name: Installer-Products-SourceList
nodes.title-description: MSI Installer Products SourceList — UNC / network path each installed MSI came from

fields.name: source-path
fields.note: UNC path or local path of the MSI source directory. Legitimate: '\\fileserver\software$\Office365\', 'C:\Installers\'. Attacker: '\\attacker-c2\share\', '\\compromised-fileshare\staged\', or a drive letter matching a removable USB. This value persists EVEN AFTER the source share is taken down — preserving the attacker's staging location for investigators.

fields.name: packed-guid
fields.note: The MSI ProductCode, reordered for lexical indexing. Unpack by reversing groups 1 and 2 (8+4+4 chars) and pairwise-swapping group 3 (16 chars). Join to the Uninstall-Keys artifact by computing the ProductCode → matches the Uninstall subkey's DisplayName / ProductCode.

fields.name: package-name
fields.note: Filename of the .msi package as installed (e.g., 'VisualStudioCode.msi', 'attacker-payload.msi'). Combined with source-path reveals the full original install location.

fields.name: product-name
fields.note: Product name as registered by the MSI. Cross-references to the Uninstall-Keys DisplayName for the same ProductCode.

fields.name: last-used-source
fields.note: Index of the most-recently-used source from the Net subkey. Useful when multiple sources are listed — identifies the one actually used for the last installer operation (install / repair / update).

fields.name: media-source
fields.note: Media source (local or CD-ROM / DVD path). Sibling to Net, captures non-network install sources. USB-delivered MSIs often appear here with a drive-letter path matching the USB's mount at install time.

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.note: LastWrite on SourceList or SourceList\Net reflects last-install / last-repair write time. Correlate with Uninstall-Keys InstallDate / key-last-write for the same product to confirm install event timing.

nodes.name: Intune-PolicyManager
nodes.title-description: Intune / MDM PolicyManager registry — cached cloud-pushed policies for managed Windows endpoints
nodes.integrity-mechanism: MDM sync protocol signature (server-side; not verified on local registry)

fields.name: policy-value
fields.note: Cached policy value. Example Areas: Defender (DisableRealtimeMonitoring, ExcludedPaths), BitLocker (EncryptionMethodByDriveType), Wifi (profile blobs), Update (WUfBPolicy overrides), Browser (Edge config), LocalPoliciesSecurityOptions (wide range of security settings). Each Area's policies are CSP-documented. Attacker-authored policies pushing AV-disable or adding Defender exclusions = ransomware tenant-compromise TTP.

fields.name: policy-area
fields.note: CSP Area (policy group). Known set published by Microsoft — Microsoft-intent Areas all prefixed with standard names (Defender, BitLocker, ConnectivityProfiles, Update, Browser, etc.). Unknown / misspelled Area names = fabricated plant.

fields.name: enrollment-id
fields.note: Each MDM enrollment creates a provider-id GUID. Multiple enrollments possible (rare outside test scenarios). The active enrollment's provider subkey holds the authoritative cached policy. Cross-reference to HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Enrollments\<enrollment-id>\ for enrollment metadata (tenant ID, Azure AD device ID, enrollment time).

fields.name: last-policy-refresh
fields.note: When the policy cache was last refreshed from the MDM server. Typical refresh cadence: every 8 hours default, configurable per-tenant. Recent LastWrite on specific Area subkeys = recent policy-push from tenant.

fields.name: admx-ingested
fields.note: ADMX-backed policies ingested into the MDM policy channel (via the Ingest MDM ADMX feature). Covers Microsoft's own ADMX backlog + third-party vendor ADMX files. A vendor\product subkey that doesn't match documented enterprise software deployment = candidate attacker injection.

fields.name: parent-dm-client-state
fields.note: Parent MDM enrollment metadata. Each enrollment has a subkey under Enrollments with TenantID, UPN (user principal name), enrollment type, discovery URL. Start here to identify what tenant the host is enrolled in.

fields.name: app-binary-name
fields.location: embedded in each value's binary structure
fields.note: the executable that opened this dialog

fields.name: last-folder-path
fields.note: the last folder this app's dialog was pointed at — serialized ITEMIDLIST comparable to ShellBags / OpenSavePidlMRU / LNK LinkTargetIDList captures of the same location

nodes.name: LSA-Cached-Logons
nodes.exit-attribution-sentence: Adversaries may attempt to access cached domain credentials used to allow authentication to occur in the event a domain controller is unavailable (MITRE ATT&CK, n.d.).
nodes.title-description: LSA cached domain logons (MSCACHEv2 / DCC2) — hashed copies of the last 10 domain credentials per host
nodes.integrity-mechanism: AES-encryption with machine key; no per-entry signing

fields.name: cached-entry
fields.encoding: NL$KM encrypted blob (DCC2 hash + username + domain + SID + group list, AES-encrypted with machine key)
fields.note: Each NL$1 through NL$<MaxCachedLogons> entry = one cached logon. Blob contents: user name, domain, SID, group memberships, and DCC2 hash of password. Decrypted offline with the machine's NL$KM key (extracted from same hive) → reveals username + domain + DCC2 hash. DCC2 is then fed to hashcat / John for offline cracking.

fields.name: cache-key-material
fields.note: The LSA secret named NL$KM holds the AES key used to encrypt Cache entries. This IS the decryption key — mimikatz lsadump::cache extracts it alongside the cache entries, then decrypts. Backup copy at SECURITY\Policy\Secrets\NL$KM\OldVal when the key has been rotated.

fields.name: cache-entry-count
fields.note: Configured maximum cached logons. Default '10'. Microsoft security baseline for high-security environments recommends '1' or '2' to reduce offline-crack exposure. Value '0' disables caching entirely — a user cannot log on while the DC is unreachable. Enterprise policy value '0' on a domain-joined workstation = explicit anti-MSCACHE posture.

fields.name: dcc2-hash-in-blob
fields.note: The per-user DCC2 hash. Hashcat mode 2100. Slower to crack than NT hash (mode 1000) by ~10000x due to PBKDF2 iteration — but weak passwords (dictionary, common patterns, leaked lists) still fall quickly. Recovery yields the PLAINTEXT domain password — direct credential exposure.

fields.name: username
fields.note: Domain\username of the cached user. Each cache entry identifies a distinct user that logged on — so cache enumeration reveals everyone who's logged into this host within the cache window (typically last 10 users).

fields.name: last-logon-time
fields.note: Last successful cached logon for this user on this host. Gives per-user logon timeline independent of EVTX retention.

fields.name: user-sid
fields.note: Full domain SID of the cached user. Cross-reference with Security-4624 logon events + SAM/AD for consistency check.

nodes.name: LSA-Packages
nodes.title-description: LSA Authentication / Notification / Security Packages (lsass.exe-loaded DLLs)
nodes.integrity-mechanism: RunAsPPL (Win8.1+) blocks unsigned SSP / AP loads once in effect

fields.name: authentication-packages
fields.note: DLLs lsass.exe loads as authentication packages at boot. Defaults: msv1_0, kerberos (implicitly). Any additional entry is a persistence DLL that runs in LSASS with access to every authentication event — ideal for credential interception.

fields.name: notification-packages
fields.note: Password-change notification DLLs lsass.exe loads at boot. Default: scecli, rassfm. Each DLL is called when ANY password changes (incl. plaintext pre-hash) — classic credential-capture persistence (Mimikatz's mimilib.dll uses this vector).

fields.name: security-packages
fields.location: Security Packages value (under \OSConfig or \Lsa depending on Windows version)
fields.note: Security Support Provider (SSP) DLLs lsass.exe loads as SSPI providers. Defaults: kerberos, msv1_0, schannel, wdigest, tspkg, pku2u, negoexts, cloudAP. Additional entries = rogue SSP — attack surface for NTLM/Kerberos interception. SSPs can access cleartext credentials via the SpAcceptCredentials / SpInitLsaModeContext callbacks.

fields.name: lsa-cfg-flags
fields.note: Credential Guard + RunAsPPL flags. 0 = off. 1 = PPL (Credential Guard runs LSASS as Protected Process Light on VBS-capable systems). Setting to 0 after enabling = guardrail tamper.

fields.name: run-as-ppl
fields.note: 1 = lsass.exe runs as Protected Process Light (Win8.1+) — blocks unprotected credential-dump tools and SSP/AP loading except for signed packages. 0 or missing = LSASS is unprotected and the Packages values above can load arbitrary DLLs.

fields.name: run-as-ppl-boot
fields.note: Whether RunAsPPL was in effect at the last boot. Compare against current RunAsPPL — mismatch means PPL was toggled since boot.

nodes.name: LSA-Protection-RunAsPPL
nodes.title-description: LSA Protection RunAsPPL registry flag — PPL protection for LSASS process (credential-theft mitigation)
nodes.integrity-mechanism: UEFI-lock (RunAsPPL=2 variant) — registry-change alone cannot disable when UEFI-locked

fields.name: run-as-ppl
fields.note: 0 = off; 1 = on; 2 = on + UEFI-locked. Microsoft baseline recommends 1 on Win10+ and 2 where UEFI is available. Value 0 or absence on modern Windows = explicit tamper or misconfigured baseline. Takes effect at next boot (not immediate — the PPL attribute is applied at lsass.exe process creation which happens at boot).

fields.name: run-as-ppl-boot
fields.note: Alternate / successor value used on some newer builds. Semantics equivalent to RunAsPPL. When both are present, check which applies on the target Windows build via Microsoft documentation. Attackers who know the name of the value to clear are more sophisticated; cross-check both.

fields.name: audit-level
fields.note: Related setting controlling LSA auditing verbosity. 0 = default; 8 = verbose. Attackers sometimes set to 0 to reduce LSA-related audit events; aggressive defenders bump to 8.

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.note: LastWrite on the Lsa key advances when RunAsPPL or sibling values change. A recent LastWrite on this key without a corresponding IT-baseline-push event = tamper candidate. Cross-reference with System-4800 (RunAsPPL audit event) if LSA-audit is active.

fields.name: uefi-variable-presence
fields.location: UEFI variable 'LsaCfgFlags' (outside registry — UEFI firmware NVRAM)
fields.note: When RunAsPPL=2 was set, a UEFI variable stores the locked state. Even if the attacker clears the registry value, the UEFI-locked setting re-asserts on next boot. To genuinely disable PPL, the attacker must boot to UEFI shell and clear LsaCfgFlags — leaves UEFI-variable-edit evidence. Check UEFI variable state when RunAsPPL=2 is the baseline.

nodes.name: LSA-Secrets
nodes.exit-attribution-sentence: Adversaries with SYSTEM access to a host may attempt to access Local Security Authority (LSA) secrets, which can contain a variety of different credential materials, such as credentials for service accounts (MITRE ATT&CK, n.d.).

fields.name: secret-name
fields.note: well-known names include $MACHINE.ACC, DefaultPassword, NL$KM, L$_SQSA_*, _SC_<service>; arbitrary names may exist per-application

fields.name: CurrVal
fields.note: current encrypted secret value; requires SYSTEM token + DPAPI decryption (Impacket secretsdump, mimikatz lsadump::secrets)

fields.name: OldVal
fields.note: previous secret value; retained for password-rotation fallback — often contains last-rotated machine-account password

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.update-rule: on secret rotation

fields.name: binding-data
fields.encoding:
Three documented formats (libyal/winreg-kb reference):
(1) DEVICE-STRING — variable-length UTF-16LE; format: DeviceID#InstanceID#{interface-class-GUID}. The middle #...# segment IS the USBSTOR serial (direct string-join to USBSTOR\\<Ven&Prod>\\<InstanceID>).
(2) MBR — EXACTLY 12 bytes. Offset 0: 4-byte DiskSignature (little-endian; identical to bytes at MBR sector 0 offset 0x1B8). Offset 4: 8-byte partition-byte-offset (little-endian; Vista+ default 0x00100000 = 1 MiB).
(3) GPT — EXACTLY 24 bytes. Starts with ASCII literal "DMIO:ID:" (8 bytes), followed by 16-byte GPT Unique Partition GUID (NOT the disk GUID).
fields.note: DeviceSerial only extractable from Format (1) device-string. Formats (2) MBR (via DiskSignature) and (3) GPT (via Partition GUID) encode volume identity without a device serial — pivot via DiskSignature → MBR sector or via PartitionGUID → GPT Partition Entry Array on the captured image.

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.note: aggregate — updates on any value change; NOT per-device

fields.name: autoplay-command
fields.note:
Command line Explorer would invoke if this device were re-mounted with
AutoRun enabled. On modern Windows AutoRun is suppressed for most
removable-storage scenarios, BUT the registered command persists from
whenever it was last set. Forensic IOCs:
- Command pointing at a non-standard path (%TEMP%, %APPDATA%, a cmd/.bat
  in an unusual location) = malware that registered AutoRun at mount time
- Command pointing at `autorun.inf`-derived executable = classic
  autorun-malware family (Conficker-era), still encountered on isolated
  networks and older machines

fields.name: namespace-type
fields.note:
MountPoints2 has THREE distinct subkey namespaces with different forensic semantics:
  (1) `{<VolumeGUID>}` — removable/fixed volume mount (the USB-tracking case)
  (2) `CPC\Volume\{<GUID>}` — Computed Path Cache, "volumes this user BROWSED
      TO" but did not necessarily mount. Present when shell traversed the
      volume path (shellbag-adjacent). NOT a mount event.
  (3) `#<server>#<share>` — SMB network share; UNC-path access, not
      removable storage
Always filter by namespace before reasoning about mount events.

fields.name: user-scope-sid
fields.location: derived from NTUSER.DAT owner via ProfileList

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.note: CAVEAT (Carvey / Hedley / libyal): LastWriteTime is NOT reliably first-connection time. Reflects 'some Explorer-shell interaction with this user's mount-point entry' — often the first connect, but can be a later interaction. Entries may be MISSED entirely if the user was not logged in at insertion, and may be attributed to a prior logged-in user after unlocked auto-relogin. Corroborate with USBSTOR Properties\{83da6326-...}\0064/0066 or Partition/Diagnostic 1006 before treating as authoritative.

fields.name: executable-path
fields.note: value name IS the path to an executable the shell has seen

fields.name: app-id
fields.location: value name suffix or separate value
fields.note: some MUICache entries carry AppID-suffixed value names for per-AppID display config

fields.name: friendly-app-name
fields.note: display name the shell caches for this executable

nodes.name: Netsh-Helpers
nodes.title-description: Netsh helper DLLs (HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Netsh) — loaded into netsh.exe on every invocation
nodes.integrity-mechanism: none (the DLL itself may or may not be signed; registration doesn't care)

fields.name: helper-dll-path
