fields.note: DLL loaded into netsh.exe. Legitimate helpers ship from Microsoft: wlancfg, ifmon, p2pnetsh, nshhttp, etc. — all in %SystemRoot%\System32\. A path outside System32 or a name not matching a stock helper = persistence plant.

fields.name: helper-name
fields.note: The value NAME is the helper's context name ('wlancfg' → netsh wlan ...). Stock set on Win10/11 is well-known; Autoruns and AutorunsC enumerate all registered helpers alongside their DLL signing state.

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.note: LastWrite timestamp — moves when a helper is added/removed. Correlate with System-7045 / Security-4697 or software install telemetry.

fields.name: profile-name
fields.note: the SSID for Wi-Fi profiles; a friendly name for Ethernet

fields.name: category
fields.note: 0=public, 1=private, 2=domain

fields.name: default-gateway-mac
fields.note: the gateway MAC — used by Windows to recognize a network; survives even after profile rename

nodes.name: NLA-Cache-Intranet
nodes.title-description: NetworkList NLA Cache Intranet — FQDNs of corporate / intranet networks the host has been on

fields.name: intranet-fqdn
fields.note: Value NAME is the intranet FQDN itself. Value DATA is typically minimal / flag-value. Each entry = one corporate or intranet DNS suffix the host has encountered. For a laptop that roams across multiple enterprise networks (MSP contractor, consultant, multi-tenant environments), this key preserves the full list of intranet domains touched. Critical for lateral-movement investigations — an attacker who pivoted through an intranet leaves its FQDN here.

fields.name: ipsubnet-cache
fields.note: Value NAME is a CIDR-ish IP subnet entry. Covers non-domain (home, hotel, public) networks by subnet rather than FQDN. Pairs with NLA-Signatures-Unmanaged for a complete non-domain connection picture.

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.note: LastWrite on the parent Intranet key advances on any new cache entry. Each value's individual LastWrite is not separately exposed, but the key-level LastWrite serves as 'most-recent cache update time'.

fields.name: companion-nla-event
fields.note: NLA logs network-transition events with event IDs 4001 (connected to network), 4002 (disconnected). Cross-reference with Nla\Cache to correlate individual cache entries to specific connection events when the EVTX channel is enabled.

nodes.name: NLA-Signatures-Unmanaged
nodes.title-description: Network List Manager Signatures\Unmanaged — default-gateway MAC + SSID for every unmanaged network

fields.name: default-gateway-mac
fields.note: MAC address of the default gateway for this unmanaged network. Because gateway MACs are (practically) globally unique and because home routers / AP vendors use predictable OUI prefixes, this MAC identifies a SPECIFIC physical router. Cross-reference against macaddress.io / Wireshark OUI database to attribute the router vendor. For investigations involving rogue APs, this is the single most valuable field — the MAC doesn't change even if the SSID is spoofed.

fields.name: first-network-ssid
fields.note: The network name (SSID for WiFi, or wired-network identifier). An attacker-set SSID is intentionally suggestive ('Verizon-ABCD', 'Starbucks', 'att-wifi') to social-engineer association — investigate patterns of SSIDs that don't match the victim's known networks.

fields.name: description
fields.note: Human-readable description as shown in the Network-and-Sharing-Center UI. Typically matches or extends FirstNetwork with metadata like 'Wi-Fi' or 'Ethernet'.

fields.name: profile-guid
fields.note: Join key to NetworkList\Profiles\<profile-guid>. Profiles hold additional metadata (NameType, Category, ProfileName, DateCreated, DateLastConnected). Always read together with the sibling Profiles subkey for full context.

fields.name: source
fields.note: 6 = DHCP learned; 8 = Manual; other values per Microsoft documentation. Distinguishes auto-acquired networks (DHCP-configured home / public) from manually-configured networks (often VPN or statically-assigned test environments).

fields.name: date-created
fields.note: First time this network was connected from this host. Binary 16-byte format (not FILETIME). Parsed as: (uint16 Year)(uint16 Month)(uint16 DayOfWeek)(uint16 Day)(uint16 Hour)(uint16 Minute)(uint16 Second)(uint16 Millisecond).

fields.name: date-last-connected
fields.note: Most recent connection to this network. Pair with DateCreated for connection-window duration. A network with DateCreated = DateLastConnected = single-use (drove past once); repeated connections indicate a familiar network (home, office, regular venue).

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.note: LastWrite on the signature subkey — typically close to DateLastConnected but independently-sourced. Cross-reference for tamper detection.

fields.name: File-MRU-entry
fields.note:
Each value encodes both the file path AND a FILETIME for the last access.
Format prefix '[F00000000]' is flags; '[T01D7...]' is the last-used FILETIME
in hex; what follows '*' is the path (local or UNC).

fields.name: per-entry-time
fields.location: embedded in value data as '[T<hex-filetime>]' token
fields.note: per-entry timestamp — richer than most MRUs which only give key-level last-write
fields.update-rule: on each file open or save (user-initiated)

fields.name: trust-record
fields.location: Trusted Documents\TrustRecords → values named by full file path
fields.encoding: proprietary TrustRecord blob — 24 bytes header with FILETIME + trust flags
fields.note: marks a document as trusted (macros enabled, content unblocked); strong signal for macro-document execution history

fields.name: recent-files
fields.note: serialized ITEMIDLIST captured from a standard Open/Save file dialog; byte-for-byte comparable (on decoded fields) with the same item appearing in ShellBags, LastVisitedPidlMRU, LNK LinkTargetIDList, and jump list entries

fields.name: ProductName
fields.note: human-readable OS name (e.g. 'Windows 10 Pro'); still reads 'Windows 10' on Win11 due to MS preserving the string

fields.name: CurrentBuild
fields.note: build number — authoritative distinguisher for Win10 vs Win11 (22000+)

fields.name: DisplayVersion
fields.note: marketing version label (21H2, 22H2, 23H2, 24H2...)

fields.name: InstallDate
fields.update-rule: set at OS install; reset by in-place upgrade

fields.name: InstallTime
fields.note: higher-resolution install timestamp (Win10+)

fields.name: RegisteredOwner
fields.note: user-provided at setup; often default or org placeholder

fields.name: EditionID
fields.note: edition token (Professional, Enterprise, Core, CoreN, ...)

nodes.name: Port-Monitors
nodes.title-description: Print Spooler Port Monitors — DLL loaded by SYSTEM-privileged spoolsv.exe on startup
nodes.integrity-mechanism: none; driver DLL is not signature-validated by Print Spooler

fields.name: driver-dll
fields.note: DLL path loaded into spoolsv.exe. Stock Microsoft port monitors reference DLLs in %SystemRoot%\System32\ (tcpmon.dll, localspl.dll, inetppui.dll). A Driver value pointing outside System32 or to an unusual path = candidate hijack. AddMonitor API (documented by Microsoft) is the legitimate installation path — attacker use typically bypasses it and writes registry directly.

fields.name: monitor-name
fields.note: The port monitor name. Stock names ('Standard TCP/IP Port', 'BJ Language Monitor', 'Local Port', 'USB Monitor', 'WSD Port') are well-known. Attacker names are sometimes fanciful ('System Monitor', 'Windows Update Monitor') to look plausible at a glance — cross-reference every entry against the documented in-box set.

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.note: LastWrite on the monitor's subkey reflects registration time. A LastWrite outside printer-driver-install windows = drive-by persistence write. Correlate with System-7045 (service registration — spooler restart after monitor add) and Security-4697 / Security-4688 (AddMonitor API call evidence).

fields.name: port-inventory
fields.note: Some monitors enumerate their configured ports here. Not persistence-critical but useful to understand what endpoints the monitor advertises.

nodes.name: PrintNightmare-PointAndPrint
nodes.title-description: Point and Print registry flags — CVE-2021-34527 (PrintNightmare) exploit-surface enabling state

fields.name: restrict-driver-installation
fields.note: THE PrintNightmare mitigation switch. 1 = driver install requires admin (mitigation ACTIVE). 0 or absent = any user can install drivers (mitigation OFF, exploit surface present). Microsoft post-July-2021 baseline: 1. Attacker-flipped to 0 + exploit PrintNightmare = SYSTEM-level execution from non-admin context.

fields.name: no-warning-no-elevation
fields.note: Legacy pre-patch setting. 1 = suppress UAC / warning dialogs on driver install; 0 = normal warnings. Attackers set 1 alongside RestrictDriverInstallationToAdministrators=0 for silent driver install. Microsoft post-patch baseline: 0 (or absent).

fields.name: update-prompt-settings
fields.note: Legacy pre-patch setting. Controls driver-update prompting. 2 = no prompts for package updates. Attacker-configured value of 2 eliminates another user-interaction barrier. Not directly the CVE but used in chained exploit variants.

fields.name: allow-server-list
fields.note: Allow-listed print servers for Point and Print. When set, clients will only install drivers from listed servers. Empty or absent list + RestrictDriverInstallationToAdministrators=0 = fully-open driver-install attack surface.

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.note: LastWrite on PointAndPrint subkey = policy change time. Attacker registry-flip to open the exploit surface leaves this timestamp. Pair with Security-4688 / Sysmon-1 for the reg.exe / PowerShell process that did the write.

fields.name: spoolsv-state
fields.note: Print Spooler service state. 2 = Automatic (default). 4 = Disabled. Microsoft's post-patch guidance for endpoints that don't need print: DISABLE the Spooler entirely (Start=4). Value of 2 on an endpoint that has no business printing = open PrintNightmare surface IF RestrictDriverInstallationToAdministrators is also 0.

fields.name: user-sid
fields.note: authoritative SID for this local or cached domain account

fields.name: profile-image-path
fields.note: e.g., C:\Users\alice or %SystemDrive%\Users\alice — resolves SID → disk profile location. Corrected 2026-04-23 from REG_SZ to REG_EXPAND_SZ per Psmths forensic-artifacts writeup + live-registry evidence. Parser-impact: values may contain unexpanded environment variables (%SystemDrive%, %SystemRoot%) that REG_SZ parsers will not resolve. Always treat as REG_EXPAND_SZ and call ExpandEnvironmentStrings (or equivalent) before path-comparing.

fields.name: state
fields.note: Profile state bitfield. Canonical values per Precedence Wiki / MS Windows 2000 Resource Kit: 0x001 = PROFILE_MANDATORY, 0x002 = PROFILE_USE_CACHE, 0x004 = PROFILE_NEW_LOCAL, 0x008 = PROFILE_NEW_CENTRAL, 0x010 = PROFILE_UPDATE_CENTRAL, 0x020 = PROFILE_DELETE_CACHE, 0x040 = PROFILE_UPGRADE, 0x080 = PROFILE_GUEST_USER, 0x100 = PROFILE_ADMIN_USER (using admin privileges), 0x200 = DEFAULT_NET_READY, 0x400 = SLOW_LINK, 0x800 = TEMP_ASSIGNED. Corrected 2026-04-23 — prior annotations (0x100=temporary, 0x200=mandatory, 0x400=roaming) were all wrong (temporary is 0x800, mandatory is 0x001, roaming is a SEPARATE ProfilePath/RUP mechanism not encoded in State). Forensically interesting bits: 0x001 (mandatory enforcement), 0x800 (TEMP_ASSIGNED — profile used this session but will not persist), 0x100 (admin-privileged profile load).

fields.name: sid-binary
fields.note: binary encoding of the SID — redundant with subkey name; useful for verification

fields.name: profile-load-time
fields.note: last time this profile was loaded

fields.name: AppPath
fields.note: full path or URI of the launched application

fields.name: LaunchCount
fields.note: per-app launch count for the lifetime of this entry

fields.name: RecentItem-path
fields.note: per-(app, file) record — each file opened by that app gets its own GUID-named subkey with the Path

fields.name: RecentItem-time
fields.update-rule: on file open via that app

fields.name: recent-file-list
fields.note: each value is a shell-item with full path + filename of a recent file of the given extension

fields.name: mru-list-ex
fields.note: most-recent-first order of the numbered values

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.update-rule: updated when a new file of this extension is opened

nodes.name: RegBack-Hives
nodes.location-path: %SystemRoot%\System32\config\RegBack\ (SAM, SECURITY, SOFTWARE, SYSTEM, DEFAULT hives copied here)
nodes.title-description: Registry hive backups (C:\Windows\System32\config\RegBack) — offline-recoverable prior SAM/SOFTWARE/SYSTEM/SECURITY state

fields.name: sam-backup
fields.note: Prior SAM state. Recovers deleted local accounts, changed password hashes (you can see the account existed before current state), and RID values that have been reused. Offline-comparable to live SAM with Registry Explorer.

fields.name: security-backup
fields.note: Prior SECURITY state — holds LSA policy secrets. Includes cached credentials, service-account secrets, machine key. Comparing to live recovers LSA-Secrets entries that an attacker cleared.

fields.name: software-backup
fields.note: Prior SOFTWARE state. Recovers deleted Run keys, removed persistence subkeys (Winlogon, Shell extensions, COM hijacks, Defender exclusions, AppCompatFlags Custom shims), and uninstalled-application traces the attacker cleared.

fields.name: system-backup
fields.note: Prior SYSTEM state. Recovers deleted services, registered drivers, Port-Monitor registrations, Time-Providers, TCP/IP parameters, USB device enumerations, MountedDevices entries — all of which are persistence-adjacent and frequently attacker-targeted for cleanup.

fields.name: default-backup
fields.note: Prior DEFAULT hive state. Less commonly forensically pivotal but covers per-default-user profile settings that new accounts inherit.

fields.name: backup-mtime
fields.location: each RegBack hive file $SI modified time
fields.note: NTFS mtime = when the backup was taken. Typical cadence is ~every 10 days on pre-1803 installs (controlled by the RegIdleBackup scheduled task). Post-1803 systems may have a stale mtime from before the upgrade OR have manually-triggered backups from admin / GPO. Compare backup mtime against incident window: if backup is recent ENOUGH to have captured pre-attack state, it's the golden compare-point.

fields.name: enable-periodic-backup
fields.note: 0 or absent = auto-backup disabled (Win10 1803+ default). 1 = auto-backup enabled (pre-1803 default; must be explicitly set on modern Windows). A recent value of 1 on a post-1803 system indicates someone (admin, GPO, or attacker covering their tracks ironically) re-enabled periodic backup.

nodes.name: Regedit-LastKey
nodes.title-description: Regedit LastKey — the last registry path the user navigated to in Registry Editor (per-user intent evidence)

fields.name: last-key
fields.note: Full registry path of the last key the user had selected in Registry Editor when regedit.exe closed. Format: 'Computer\HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\...full\path'. Investigator red flags: LastKey pointing at a persistence key (Run / RunOnce / Services), a Defender-Exclusions path, an Image File Execution Options subkey, a COM CLSID subkey of interest. 'This user opened regedit and navigated here' is user-intent evidence.

fields.name: favorites-list
fields.note: User-added Favorites (Regedit's Favorites menu). Rare to see a malicious user / attacker add Favorites — but when present, each favorite is a pinned location the user returned to repeatedly. Highly specific to individual user habit.

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.note: LastWrite on the Regedit key updates when LastKey value changes — i.e., on every regedit.exe close. Gives a moment-of-registry-editor-close timestamp. Pair with Security-4688 / Sysmon-1 for the regedit.exe launch event bracketing the session.

fields.name: view-mode
fields.note: Regedit's view preference (expanded/collapsed pane widths, etc.). Not directly forensic but a moving value that indicates the user genuinely used regedit (a fresh View mtime aligns with fresh LastKey). Stale View with fresh LastKey = possible tamper.

nodes.name: Registered-Owner
nodes.title-description: RegisteredOwner / RegisteredOrganization — attribution fields recorded at Windows install

fields.name: registered-owner
fields.note: Name recorded at install. Rarely changes afterward (can be edited manually). For attribution in cases where the installing-user identity matters (seized device of unknown provenance), this value often preserves the original operator name even if the admin / user accounts were later renamed.

fields.name: registered-organization
fields.note: Organization name at install. For enterprise-imaged machines reveals the imaging source (company / vendor name). For OEM-sold machines shows 'Microsoft' / manufacturer defaults. Empty / blank on self-installed personal machines.

fields.name: productid
fields.note: Windows Product ID — install-specific ID derived from the product key. Sibling attribution value set at install time. Joins multiple machines installed from the same product key.

fields.name: install-date
fields.note: Unix epoch timestamp of Windows install. More precise than the Registered* label fields for install-time attribution.

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.note: LastWrite updates on any sibling value change. Many Windows values live under CurrentVersion — the key-level mtime is noisy and not directly meaningful for these specific install-time values.

nodes.name: Registry-Transaction-Logs
nodes.title-description: Registry .LOG1 / .LOG2 transaction logs — unflushed dirty-page recovery (replay to restore deleted keys)

fields.name: dirty-pages
fields.note: Page-aligned writes that modify the primary hive but have not been flushed. Each dirty page records: target hive block, sequence number, and the new page contents. Replay applies them in sequence to reconstruct the latest state.

fields.name: sequence-number
fields.note: Log writes are sequence-numbered for idempotent replay. The replay tool processes log entries in ascending order and stops at the last valid entry. Corruption / tampering often manifests as gaps or out-of-order sequences — the replay tool reports these.

fields.name: primary-hive-divergence
fields.location: comparison between live hive file and replayed hive-plus-logs
fields.note: The whole POINT of this artifact — what lives in the logs that is NOT yet in the hive file. For an attacker who committed a cleanup then immediately imaged the system (or whose cleanup happened right before a crash), the hive file shows CLEAN state but the logs contain the PRE-CLEANUP dirty pages. Replay surfaces those.

fields.name: log-file-mtime
fields.note: NTFS mtime — when the last unflushed write occurred. For the SYSTEM / SOFTWARE hives this is essentially always very recent on a live system. On acquired images, log mtime bracketed with hive mtime tells you how much unflushed state was pending.

fields.name: log-file-size
fields.note: Log files grow to accommodate unflushed writes, then are reset (truncated or zero-filled) on clean flush. A large log file indicates substantial unflushed state — expect meaningful recovery on replay. Tiny log file indicates hive is nearly-flushed (typical steady state on a gracefully-running system).

fields.name: replay-validity
fields.note: The replay tool (rla.exe / Registry Explorer / libhivex) reports whether the log-to-hive replay was 'clean' (all sequence numbers present, all checksums valid) or 'partial' (some entries invalid / tampered). Partial replay still surfaces the valid entries and is still forensically valuable — but note the anomaly.

nodes.name: RootCertificate-Store
nodes.title-description: Trusted Root Certification Authority store — attacker-installed roots enable silent TLS MITM
nodes.integrity-mechanism: Windows AuthRoot update mechanism does NOT remove manually-installed roots — attacker roots persist through Windows Update CTL refreshes. Tamper Protection does not cover this store.

fields.name: certificate-blob
fields.encoding: Microsoft SystemCertificates binary wrapper containing DER-encoded X.509
fields.note: The raw certificate. Binary-format wrapper (SystemCertificate property bag) with the DER-encoded X.509 certificate embedded. Tools: certutil -store ROOT; PowerShell Get-ChildItem Cert:\LocalMachine\Root; or parse the Blob directly via CertParseCertificateContext.

fields.name: cert-subject
fields.note: Subject of the root CA. Well-known legit roots: DigiCert, Let's Encrypt ISRG, VeriSign, Microsoft, GlobalSign, Sectigo, etc. A Subject that is NOT on the Microsoft Trusted Root Program list AND not on the enterprise's documented internal-CA list = investigation target.

fields.name: cert-issuer
fields.note: For a true root CA, Issuer == Subject (self-signed). For intermediates mistakenly placed in ROOT store, Issuer differs from Subject. Presence of an intermediate-looking cert in ROOT store is either misconfiguration or deliberate cert-chain-pinning by an attacker.

fields.name: cert-validity
fields.note: Validity window. Attacker-generated roots often have very long validity (50+ years, 9999-12-31) to outlive detection; excessive far-future NotAfter = signal. Also: a NotBefore matching the suspected intrusion timeline is a direct pivot.

fields.name: cert-serial
fields.note: Serial number is certificate-issuer-scoped. For an attacker-generated root this is an arbitrary value set at cert generation; no lookup against external registries possible, but pairs with Subject / Thumbprint for cert-identity confirmation.

fields.name: cert-thumbprint
fields.note: SHA-1 hash of the DER-encoded certificate. Join key across the whole certificate ecosystem — use this to cross-reference against certutil output, Event Viewer CAPI2 events, Microsoft's Trusted Root list, and any EDR cert-inventory data.

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.note: LastWrite on the subkey = cert-install time. Correlate with Microsoft-Windows-CAPI2/Operational event 4097 (cert installed) when CAPI2 operational logging is enabled. Bonus: CAPI2 channel logs the SID of the installing user.

fields.name: per-user-vs-machine
fields.note: Machine-wide root installs (HKLM) affect every user and require admin; per-user installs (HKCU) affect only that user but do NOT require admin. Low-privilege attackers use HKCU to install rogue roots in their own session silently. Always enumerate BOTH scopes; an unprivileged attacker's cert may only be in HKCU.

fields.name: entry-name
fields.location: value name under Run or RunOnce
fields.note: descriptive label; chosen by whatever registered the autostart — often product name

fields.name: command-line
fields.note: executable path + arguments; may contain environment variables (%ProgramFiles%\...). Microsoft Win32 documentation caps command-line length at 260 characters — attempts to set longer values may truncate or fail silently depending on the writing API.

fields.name: value-name-prefix
fields.location: first character of RunOnce value name (HKLM\RunOnce + HKCU\RunOnce only)
fields.note: RunOnce-specific value-name conventions (per MS Win32 spec). `!` prefix defers value deletion until AFTER successful execution (classic retry-on-failure); `*` prefix forces execution even in Safe Mode (used by setup-repair paths and by some anti-forensic-evasion tools). Presence of `*` on an unfamiliar entry is a strong persistence-investigation signal.

fields.name: user-scope-sid
fields.note: only for HKCU; HKLM variant is machine-scope with no user binding

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.update-rule: updated on value add / modify / delete

fields.name: command-letter
fields.note: each lettered value holds one command entered at Win+R followed by '\1' (literal) as list terminator

fields.name: command-text
fields.note: raw command string as typed (cmd.exe /c powershell ..., \\\\server\\share, http://..., etc.)

fields.name: MRUList
fields.note: string ordering of letters indicating most-recent-first order (e.g. 'badc' means b was most-recent)

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.update-rule: updated on every Run-dialog submission (new or reused)

nodes.name: SAM
nodes.exit-attribution-sentence: The SAM is a database file that contains local accounts for the host, typically those found with the `net user` command (MITRE ATT&CK, n.d.).
nodes.integrity-mechanism: password-hash encryption tied to bootkey (SYSTEM hive)

fields.name: rid
fields.note: Relative Identifier — the low 32 bits of the full SID (machine SID + RID = full SID)

fields.name: username
fields.location: V value — parsed from binary structure
fields.note: user account name (sAMAccountName on domain-joined machines)

fields.name: password-hash
fields.location: V value — LM/NTLM hash blobs (encrypted with system key)
fields.note: NOT directly readable — requires bootkey from SYSTEM hive to decrypt. Offline cracking is the common attack path.

fields.name: account-flags
fields.note: 0x0001=disabled, 0x0010=locked, 0x0200=pw-never-expires, 0x8000=domain-trust, etc.

nodes.name: SChannel-TLS-Config
nodes.title-description: SChannel TLS / cipher / protocol registry — attacker-downgrade surface for Windows TLS stack

fields.name: protocol-enabled
fields.note: Enabled: 0xFFFFFFFF / 1 = protocol enabled, 0 = disabled. DisabledByDefault: 1 = negotiation skips unless app explicitly requests, 0 = negotiated normally. MS baseline on Win10/11: TLS 1.2 / 1.3 enabled+default-on; all others disabled+default-off. Attacker re-enabling SSL 2.0 / 3.0 / TLS 1.0 = downgrade surface for passive capture / MITM. Check BOTH Client and Server subkeys.

fields.name: cipher-enabled
fields.note: 0xFFFFFFFF = enabled; 0 = disabled. Cipher-name examples: 'AES 128/128', 'AES 256/256', 'DES 56/56', 'RC2 40/128', 'RC4 40/128', 'RC4 56/128', 'RC4 64/128', 'RC4 128/128', 'Triple DES 168', 'NULL'. MS baseline disables: NULL, DES, RC2, RC4 variants. Attacker re-enabling RC4 / 3DES / DES = cryptographic-weakness reintroduction.

fields.name: hash-enabled
fields.note: Values per hash: MD5, SHA, SHA256, SHA384, SHA512. Enabled: 0xFFFFFFFF = on, 0 = off. MS baseline disables MD5. Attacker re-enable of MD5 = weak-signature acceptance for cert chains.

fields.name: key-exchange-enabled
fields.note: Values: Diffie-Hellman, ECDH, PKCS, RSA, etc. Attacker manipulation of these keyexchange settings enables downgrade to weaker key agreement.

fields.name: fips-algorithm-policy
fields.note: Cross-key sibling: 1 = FIPS mode (restricts SChannel to FIPS-approved algorithms), 0 = non-FIPS. Attacker clearing FIPS mode weakens the entire cryptographic posture. MS baseline on regulated-environment hosts: 1.

fields.name: event-logging
fields.note: Controls SChannel event-log verbosity. 1 = errors, 2 = warnings, 4 = informational, 7 = all. Attacker setting 0 (or very low) suppresses SChannel errors from System-channel / Microsoft-Windows-Schannel/Operational EVTX, hiding cert-validation failures / downgrade-negotiation warnings.

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.note: LastWrite on protocol / cipher / hash subkeys updates when the Enabled or DisabledByDefault value changes. Diff against enterprise hardening-baseline snapshot: any subkey with LastWrite post-dating the baseline deploy = drift candidate.

nodes.name: Scheduled-Tasks
nodes.exit-attribution-sentence: Adversaries may abuse the Windows Task Scheduler to perform task scheduling for initial or recurring execution of malicious code (MITRE ATT&CK, n.d.).
nodes.integrity-mechanism: task XML hash validated against registry Hash value

fields.name: task-path
fields.note: namespace path like '\Microsoft\Windows\UpdateOrchestrator\Schedule Scan'

fields.name: action-executable
fields.location: Actions value (binary blob) — parsed action command

fields.name: principal-sid
fields.note: task runs under this account; may be SYSTEM, LOCAL SERVICE, NETWORK SERVICE, or a real user

fields.name: hash
fields.encoding: SHA256 of the task definition XML (Win8+)
fields.note: integrity check — tasks whose XML was modified out-of-band fail to load

fields.name: dynamic-info
fields.note: last-run + last-result + next-run time blob; requires version-specific parsing

fields.name: last-result
fields.note: Win32 error code of last task run; 0 = success

fields.name: xml-file-reference
fields.note: full task XML lives here — triggers, settings, actions in human-readable XML

nodes.name: Screensaver-Hijack
nodes.title-description: Screensaver SCRNSAVE.EXE registry — executable launched by Desktop Window Manager at inactivity threshold

fields.name: screensaver-path
fields.note: Path to the screensaver executable. Legitimate .scr files live in %SystemRoot%\System32\ (bubbles.scr, ribbons.scr, photoScreensaver.scr, etc.). Technically ANY executable works — Windows does not validate that the file is a true screensaver .scr. A path outside System32 or a non-.scr extension = hijack. Common attacker pattern is to point this at a renamed .exe for user-scope persistence that fires automatically after N minutes of idle.

fields.name: screensaver-active
fields.note: '1' = screensaver enabled; '0' = disabled. Set to '1' for the hijack to fire. Legitimate state varies per user preference.

fields.name: screensaver-timeout
fields.note: Idle-time threshold in seconds before the screensaver launches. Default 900 (15 minutes). Attackers sometimes lower this (60 or 120) for faster trigger — anomalously low timeout value = signal.

fields.name: screensaver-is-secure
fields.note: '1' = exiting screensaver requires user password (lock); '0' = no lock. Attacker-set SCRNSAVE.EXE usually does not set IsSecure=1 because the goal is silent execution, not session lockout. Legitimate enterprise policy often sets IsSecure=1 via GPO.

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.note: LastWrite updates on SCRNSAVE.EXE / ScreenSaveActive / timeout changes. Correlate with the user's logon session (Security-4624) and with any process-creation events writing these values.

fields.name: gpo-policy-scrnsave
fields.note: Group-Policy-pushed screensaver path. When present, GPO wins over the user's Control Panel\Desktop value — attackers who control GPO can enforce a screensaver hijack across all affected users. In domain environments, verify GPO-pushed SCRNSAVE.EXE alongside the user-scope value.

nodes.name: Services
nodes.exit-attribution-sentence: Adversaries may create or modify Windows services to repeatedly execute malicious payloads as part of persistence (MITRE ATT&CK, n.d.).

fields.name: service-name
fields.note: short internal name; e.g., 'Spooler', 'lsass', 'Schedule'

fields.name: display-name
fields.note: human-readable name shown in services.msc

fields.name: image-path
fields.note: path + arguments to the service executable; may reference environment variables

fields.name: service-dll
fields.note: DLL to load into svchost.exe; common injection target

fields.name: start-type
fields.note: 0=boot-driver, 1=system-driver, 2=auto-start, 3=manual, 4=disabled

fields.name: service-type
fields.note: bitfield: 0x01=kernel driver, 0x02=file-sys-driver, 0x10=own-process user, 0x20=shared-process user, 0x100=interactive

fields.name: object-name
fields.note: account the service runs as — 'LocalSystem', 'NT AUTHORITY\LocalService', 'NT AUTHORITY\NetworkService', or a user account name

fields.name: description
fields.note: free-form description shown in services.msc; attackers typically match Microsoft descriptions for camouflage

fields.name: failure-command
fields.note: command to execute on service failure — known persistence/abuse vector

nodes.name: SessionManager-Persistence
nodes.title-description: Session Manager persistence values (BootExecute / Execute / SubSystems / SetupExecute / S0InitialCommand / WOWCommandLine)

fields.name: boot-execute
fields.note: native-mode executables run by smss.exe at very early boot — before Win32 subsystem loads. Default on clean systems is just 'autocheck autochk *'. Any additional entries are persistence; commonly abused because they run before AV / EDR user-mode agents start.

fields.name: execute
fields.note: run by smss.exe slightly after BootExecute. Normally empty on clean systems. Non-empty = likely persistence.

fields.name: s0-initial-command
fields.note: the FIRST Win32 program smss.exe runs during Session 0 startup. Default wininit.exe on Vista+, csrss.exe on XP. Tampering here replaces the first Session 0 program — very high-confidence persistence signal.

fields.name: setup-execute
fields.note: runs on the first boot after Windows Setup completes. Should be EMPTY on any post-setup system. Populated = either setup didn't finish cleanly or persistence injection.

fields.name: sub-systems
fields.note: environment subsystems loaded at boot. Default: csrss.exe for Windows, OS2/POSIX historically. Non-default additions = exotic subsystem persistence; rare but powerful because it runs in Session 0 privileged context.

fields.name: wow-command-line
fields.note: command line used to launch 16-bit Windows (WoW) processes. Rarely relevant on modern 64-bit Windows but leaves an historical persistence surface.

nodes.name: Shell-COM-Hooks
nodes.title-description: Shell CLSID-list persistence (ShellExecuteHooks / ShellServiceObjects / ShellIconOverlayIdentifiers / SharedTaskScheduler)

fields.name: shell-execute-hooks
fields.note: Each value name is a CLSID of a COM object implementing IShellExecuteHook. Explorer invokes the hook on every ShellExecute call (every file/URL launch through the shell). On stock Windows 10/11 this key contains one well-known CLSID ({AEB6717E-7E19-11d0-97EE-00C04FD91972} — URL and shortcut handling). Any additional CLSID = potential hijack; resolve the CLSID under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Classes\CLSID to find the backing DLL.

fields.name: shell-service-objects
fields.note: Each value name (arbitrary string) maps to a CLSID (value data). COM objects listed here are loaded into explorer.exe on logon. Stock Windows has entries like 'WebCheck', 'PostBootReminder', 'SysTray'. Extra entries pointing to non-Microsoft CLSIDs = persistence plant.

fields.name: shell-icon-overlays
fields.note: Each subkey's Default value is a CLSID of an IShellIconOverlayIdentifier COM object. Loaded into explorer.exe to render overlay icons (OneDrive/Dropbox sync-status icons, TortoiseGit state badges). Stock Windows has a handful; cloud-sync apps add more. Subkeys prefixed with leading spaces are a known abuse pattern to win the load-order race (Windows loads only the first ~15 overlay providers).

fields.name: shared-task-scheduler
fields.note: Each value name is a CLSID. COM objects listed here are loaded by a shared-scheduler service early in session startup. Stock Win10/11 usually has only one (Browseui preloader, {438755C2-A8BA-11D1-B96B-00A0C90312E1}). Extra entries = persistence.

fields.name: resolved-dll
fields.note: Each CLSID referenced above resolves to a DLL via the standard COM registration path. THIS is the backing file that actually loads into explorer.exe. Validate that the DLL path is under %SystemRoot%\System32 (or a signed vendor directory for legitimate cloud/shell integrations). Paths in %APPDATA% / %PROGRAMDATA% / user-writable locations = hijack.

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.note: LastWrite on any of these four keys post-dating the OS install AND falling outside a known legit-software install window = candidate persistence write. Correlate with System-7045 / Security-4697 service events and software install logs.

fields.name: bag-tree-hierarchy
fields.location: BagMRU subkey tree — each subkey = one folder level, numbered-value children = that folder's subfolders ordered by first-browse
fields.note: reconstructing the full browse path requires walking BagMRU from root and decoding each shell-item

fields.name: shell-item-binary
fields.note: one shell item per value (numbered 0, 1, 2...); type byte at offset 2 determines which further fields are present

fields.name: shell-item-type
fields.note: 0x1F=root, 0x20-2F=drive/volume, 0x30-3F=folder/file, 0x40-4F=network, 0x70=control panel

fields.name: folder-short-name
fields.encoding: ASCII or UTF-16LE per item flags
fields.note: legacy 8.3 name; see folder-long-name for full filename from ExtensionBlock

fields.name: folder-mft-reference
fields.encoding: entry:sequence (6B entry + 2B sequence from shell-item body)
fields.note: NTFS only; FAT-formatted volumes produce shell items without this field

fields.name: folder-created-time
fields.note: frozen snapshot of folder's creation time at the moment the user first browsed into it

fields.name: volume-label
fields.encoding: ASCII or UTF-16LE per item flags

fields.name: volume-guid
fields.note: present for volume-scoped shell items; distinguishes removable/network volumes

fields.name: network-unc-path
fields.location: shell-item (type 0x40-0x4F) — UNC path to network resource

fields.name: node-slot
fields.note: index into Bags\<N> key that holds this folder's view-state preferences

fields.name: mru-list-ex
fields.encoding: array of uint32-le — children sorted most-recent-first
fields.note: defines the order children were most recently accessed; first entry = most recent subfolder browsed

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.note: approximates 'time user last explored a new subfolder here' — not 'time user opened this folder'
fields.update-rule: updated when a new child is added under this BagMRU key (user browsed a new subfolder)

fields.name: file-path
fields.note: may be drive-letter form OR \Device\HarddiskVolume<N>\... form depending on version

fields.name: last-modified-time
fields.location: per-entry — $FN (NTFS FileName attribute) Modified time captured at cache
fields.note: this is NOT 'when the exe ran' — it's the target's $FN-modified time at the moment ShimCache observed it

fields.name: file-size
fields.location: per-entry (Win XP/7/8 only — dropped in Win10+)

fields.name: execution-flag-win10
fields.location: per-entry — LAST 4 bytes of the Data field (Win10 1507+ / Win11)
fields.note: NOT the InsertFlags DWORD — that field was reorganized on Win10. Zimmerman's AppCompatCacheParser gained support in commit c995e82 (March 2023). Interpretation per Nullsec 23H2 testing: 'Yes' = high confidence executed; 'No' = INCONCLUSIVE (lsass.exe, cmd.exe, explorer.exe frequently show 0 despite clearly running).

fields.name: header-magic
fields.note: version discriminator: 0xdeadbeef=XP-32 (96 entries), 0xbadc0ffe=2003/Vista/7 (512–1024), 0x80000000 with header-size 0x80=Win8/8.1, header-size 0x30=Win10 1507–1607, header-size 0x34=Win10 1703+ / Win11. Entry magic '10ts' signals Win8.1+ entry layout.

fields.name: cache-ordinal
fields.note: entries are kept in MRU order; first entry = most recent

nodes.name: ShutdownTime
nodes.title-description: Last-clean-shutdown FILETIME under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Windows → ShutdownTime

fields.name: last-shutdown
fields.note: Timestamp of last clean shutdown. Directly readable as a Windows FILETIME. Canonical source for answering 'when was the machine last cleanly shut down?' — used in incident timelines, 'was the host on at time X?' queries, and cross-reference with System-1074 / System-6006 shutdown events.

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.note: LastWrite on the Windows key updates whenever any child value changes. In normal operation the LastWrite mirrors the ShutdownTime value. Delta between the two is anomalous — suggests a write other than the canonical shutdown updater has touched this key.

nodes.name: SilentProcessExit-Monitor
nodes.title-description: SilentProcessExit IFEO sibling — attach a monitor process / dump on target process exit

fields.name: monitor-process
fields.note: Full command line of the monitor to launch when target exits. Attacker-typical: 'C:\\Temp\\procdump.exe -ma lsass.exe C:\\Temp\\lsass.dmp' — LSASS credential-dump auto-trigger. Any MonitorProcess value pointing to procdump / comsvcs / custom-binary with lsass target = high-severity alert.

fields.name: reporting-mode
fields.note: Bitmask. 0x1 = enable SilentProcessExit handling. 0x2 = enable WER local dump (separate from Monitor). For attacker persistence, 0x1 is required.

fields.name: local-dump-folder
fields.note: Optional output directory for built-in WER-style dump (bypasses MonitorProcess). Setting this + ReportingMode=0x2 causes Windows to dump the target's memory to disk on exit without running an attacker binary — stealth variant.

fields.name: target-ifeo-globalflag
fields.note: REQUIRED companion — must be 0x200 (FLG_MONITOR_SILENT_PROCESS_EXIT) on the IFEO entry for the SilentProcessExit trigger to fire. Absence of this flag = SilentProcessExit subkey is inert.

nodes.name: Start-TrackProgs
nodes.title-description: Explorer Advanced Start_TrackProgs flag — switches off UserAssist / RunMRU / frequent-apps tracking

fields.name: tracking-enabled
fields.note: 0 = tracking off (anti-forensics state). 1 = tracking on (default on a fresh install). Default value is 1 — absence of the value also means 'on'. Presence AND value=0 is the forensic signal.

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.note: LastWrite on the Advanced key reflects the moment Start_TrackProgs was toggled (or any sibling value changed). Compare with the expected timeline — toggling off immediately before the incident window is a textbook evasion sequence.

fields.name: companion-usertile-off
fields.note: Group-policy counterparts that disable MFU program list / Windows-Tracking instrumentation at the policy layer. When both Start_TrackProgs=0 AND NoInstrumentation=1 are set by the same user, it's deliberate blanket tracking suppression, not a UI slip.

fields.name: favorites-list
fields.note: pinned apps as shell-items; parsers must decode shell-item chains to extract AppIDs

fields.name: favorites-resolve
fields.note: resolution cache; parsers often skip, but contains target paths

fields.name: MRU-server-slot
fields.location: values named 'MRU0', 'MRU1', ..., 'MRU9' at the Default key
fields.note: hostname or IP of a server the user connected to via mstsc.exe

fields.name: UsernameHint
fields.note: last username entered for THAT specific server — attacker credentials left behind

fields.name: CertHash
fields.note: trusted-certificate fingerprint for the server — user accepted this certificate for subsequent connections

fields.name: key-last-write-default
fields.update-rule: on MRU update (new connection or reconnect)

fields.name: key-last-write-per-server
fields.update-rule: on each connection to THAT specific server

nodes.name: Time-Providers
nodes.title-description: W32Time service Time Provider DLLs (NTP client / server plugins loaded into svchost.exe)
nodes.integrity-mechanism: none — DllName is not signature-validated by W32Time

fields.name: dll-name
fields.note: DLL loaded into W32Time's svchost.exe host. Stock Microsoft providers point to %SystemRoot%\system32\w32time.dll (NtpClient / NtpServer both use the same DLL with different provider classes). Third-party / hypervisor providers (VMware, Hyper-V VMICTimeProvider) point to vendor-signed DLLs. Any DllName outside these baselines = candidate persistence plant.

fields.name: provider-enabled
fields.note: 0 = disabled (registered but not active), 1 = enabled (loaded at service start). Attacker-registered disabled providers are less noisy but still visible; enabled=1 on a provider with non-stock DllName = active persistence.

fields.name: provider-input-provider
fields.note: 1 = this provider supplies time samples to W32Time (client-style); 0 = this provider consumes / publishes time only (server-style). Attacker providers typically set InputProvider=0 to avoid interfering with actual time sync — the DLL is loaded for persistence, not time-service functionality.

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.note: LastWrite reflects provider-registration time. Correlate with System-7036 (W32Time service status change — restart needed to load new provider) and Security-4697.

fields.name: event-log-hints
fields.note: Event ID 35 = time-service started. Event ID 257 / 258 = provider loaded. Event ID 134 / 37 = provider errors. New provider registration shows up as a provider-loaded event at next service start.

fields.name: time-zone-key-name
fields.note: e.g., 'Pacific Standard Time', 'UTC', 'Central European Standard Time'

fields.name: standard-name
fields.note: user-facing name for the standard (non-DST) time-zone

fields.name: daylight-name
fields.note: user-facing name during DST

fields.name: active-time-bias
fields.note: minutes OFFSET from UTC (positive = west of UTC). Negative for east, positive for west. Doesn't distinguish standard vs. DST — that's inferred from the effective bias.

fields.name: bias
fields.note: standard-time bias

fields.name: daylight-bias
fields.note: usually -60 during DST season

fields.name: daylight-start
fields.note: SYSTEMTIME-ish struct defining when DST starts

fields.name: recent-target
fields.note: each MRU<N> value holds one recently-connected target (hostname or IP)

fields.name: username-hint
fields.note: the username the user typed for this target — indicates WHAT credential they used

fields.name: cert-hash
fields.note: accepted RDP certificate thumbprint — if user accepted a 'new' cert warning, this records what they trusted

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.location: Default key or per-server subkey metadata
fields.update-rule: updated on MRU change

fields.name: url-slot
fields.note: each slot holds one user-typed Explorer address-bar entry (local path, UNC, file:// URL, or shell:: path)

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.update-rule: on each address-bar navigation (new or reused)

fields.name: url-slot
fields.note: one user-typed URL per slot; slot 1 is the most recent

fields.name: url-time
fields.location: TypedURLsTime subkey → value of same name as in TypedURLs
fields.note: per-URL timestamp — when the URL was most-recently typed. Unlike most MRU artifacts where only the key-last-write gives a timestamp, TypedURLsTime gives per-entry timing.

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.update-rule: on any url-slot add or reuse

nodes.name: Uninstall-Keys
nodes.title-description: Installed-programs Uninstall registry keys — MSI / third-party installer inventory with install timestamps
nodes.integrity-mechanism: MSI package signing (optional) — enforces nothing about the Uninstall registry itself

fields.name: display-name
fields.note: Human-readable application name. Usually the same as the vendor's advertised product name. Attacker-installed 'Apps' (deployed via MSI sometimes) show up here — masquerading as 'Microsoft Edge Update' or 'System Monitoring Tool' or similar.

fields.name: display-version
fields.note: Version string. Legitimate apps match vendor-published versions; attacker apps often have obvious placeholders ('1.0.0.0', '0.0.0.1') or version fields that contain suspicious content.

fields.name: publisher
fields.note: Vendor name (e.g., 'Microsoft Corporation', 'Google LLC', 'Adobe Inc.'). Attacker MSIs sometimes leave Publisher blank, use 'System' / 'Default Manufacturer', or copy a legitimate vendor name to blend. Baseline-compare against known-good publishers.

fields.name: install-location
fields.note: Directory where the app is installed. Legitimate apps install to %ProgramFiles% / %ProgramFiles(x86)% / %LocalAppData%\Programs. Attacker MSIs may install to %TEMP%, %APPDATA%, or user-writable paths — a path outside Program Files is a signal.

fields.name: install-source
fields.note: Directory from which the installer was run. For enterprise-managed installs this is often a UNC path (SCCM content share, DFS share). For attacker installs run from USB or from a downloaded .msi in Downloads, InstallSource reveals the drop-location at install time — potentially BEFORE the attacker cleaned up Downloads.

fields.name: install-date
fields.encoding: yyyyMMdd (8-digit date string; no time component)
fields.note: Install date as a string. Only day-level resolution. For sub-second install-timing use the key LastWrite + UsnJrnl entries. BUT: this is the most directly-readable install date available and shows up in reports unchanged.

fields.name: uninstall-string
fields.note: Command run to uninstall the app. Legitimate apps have 'msiexec /x{GUID}' or a path to vendor-provided uninstaller.exe. Attacker MSIs may have blank / garbage UninstallString to frustrate removal attempts by IR teams.

fields.name: estimated-size
fields.note: Estimated footprint in KB. Not forensically critical; occasionally useful for sanity-checking that the app size matches expected vendor product size.

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.note: Precise (100ns-resolution) install timestamp. Superior to InstallDate's day-granularity string. This is the pivot timestamp to use for timeline work.

fields.name: vid-pid
fields.note: vendor + product identifier from the USB descriptor; common across all instances of the same device model

fields.name: instance-id
fields.note: device-instance path; for devices that report a serial, this IS the serial — for others it's an OS-synthesized instance id

fields.name: container-id-property
fields.note: SAME ContainerID GUID that USBSTOR, WindowsPortableDevices, and Partition/Diagnostic events carry — primary forensic join across independent enumeration paths

fields.name: FriendlyName
fields.note: human-readable device name (e.g., 'USB Composite Device', 'Logitech USB Receiver')

fields.name: DeviceDesc
fields.note: typically an INF-provided descriptor with ampersand-prefix

fields.name: HardwareID
fields.note: ordered list of hardware identifiers used by driver match (USB\VID_xxxx&PID_yyyy&REV_zzzz, etc.)

fields.name: Service
fields.note: bound driver service name (HidUsb, usbccgp, usbaudio, ...)

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.update-rule: driver install, reconfigure, or device first-enumeration

fields.name: serial-number
fields.note: char-pos 2 == '&' means OS-synthesized serial — not device-reported, not unique across same-model devices

fields.name: container-id
fields.note: stable device identity; survives reformat; does NOT survive controller replacement

fields.name: first-install-time
fields.encoding: Win7: FILETIME at <prop-key>\00000000\Data. Win8+: FILETIME as unnamed default value of the 00xx subkey.
fields.note: Hedley 2024 Win10 22H2 caveat: 0064 and 0065 frequently carry identical values. Treat as 'install observed at time X' rather than two independent observations.

fields.name: last-removal-time
fields.note: Hedley 2024 caveat: 0067 updates on DRIVER-UNINSTALL events, not only physical unplug. Do not equate 0067 with 'last physical disconnection' without corroborating Partition/Diagnostic 1006 and UserPnp events.

fields.name: disk-id
fields.encoding: GUID string. Partmgr-assigned disk identifier — per-device GUID the Partition Manager driver binds when the disk is first enumerated. NOT the filesystem VolumeGUID; those live in MountedDevices.
fields.note: Join path to MountedDevices is via USBSTOR <instance-id> substring in MountedDevices binding-data (Format 1), NOT via this DiskId field directly.

fields.name: parent-id-prefix
fields.note: Device-tree position token assigned by PnP. Used by the USB controller to thread this device to its bus-relations chain. Forensically rare — useful when reconstructing hub-topology at time of connection.

fields.name: driver
fields.note: `{4d36e967-e325-11ce-bfc1-08002be10318}\<N>` — DiskDrive class GUID + driver instance number. Cross-reference to `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Class\{4d36e967-...}\<N>` for driver binding details.

fields.name: class-guid
fields.note: Always `{4d36e967-e325-11ce-bfc1-08002be10318}` (GUID_DEVCLASS_DISKDRIVE) under USBSTOR. If different, not a USB-disk — USBSTOR only enumerates mass-storage disks; USB NICs/HIDs live under other Enum subkeys.

fields.name: service
fields.note: Always `disk` or `cdrom` on USBSTOR. `disk` = USB mass storage; `cdrom` = USB optical drive.

fields.name: mfg
fields.note: Manufacturer as reported in INF (e.g., `(Standard disk drives)`). Generic — not device-reported. Don't confuse with `friendly-name`.

fields.name: location-information
fields.note: Physical port location description, e.g., `Port_#0004.Hub_#0001`. Useful for distinguishing otherwise-identical devices connected to different USB ports.

nodes.name: UserAssist
nodes.integrity-mechanism: none, beyond ROT13 'obfuscation' that does not impede tampering

fields.name: category-guid
fields.note: identifies what class of launch this Count subkey tracks

fields.name: rot13-value-name
fields.encoding: ROT13-encoded ASCII path or known constant (UEME_CTLSESSION, UEME_CTLCUACount:ctor, UEME_RUNPATH, UEME_RUNPIDL, etc.)
fields.note: ROT13 is trivially reversible — parsers do it automatically; path references an exe or .lnk target depending on category

fields.name: run-count
fields.note: number of times the launch was observed — on Win7+ starts from 0 and increments; some value-name families never increment

fields.name: focus-count
fields.note: number of distinct focus sessions with the launched window

fields.name: focus-time-ms
fields.note: cumulative milliseconds the window had focus — an approximate 'dwell time' signal

fields.name: last-run-time
fields.update-rule: updated on each launch observed in this user's session

fields.name: user-scope-sid
fields.location: derived from NTUSER.DAT owner via ProfileList

fields.name: value-data-version
fields.location: value data size distinguishes format versions
fields.note: parsers must branch on the Windows version to decode correctly

nodes.name: Windows-Firewall-Profiles
nodes.title-description: Windows Firewall profile enable state + logging config (Domain / Private / Public profiles)

fields.name: enable-firewall
fields.note: 1 = firewall profile active; 0 = DISABLED. DomainProfile / StandardProfile / PublicProfile checked independently. Attacker-set 0 on any profile silently disables enforcement on networks matching that profile — e.g., Private=0 disables firewall on home-network-classified connections. Microsoft baseline + CIS / DISA STIG: all three profiles = 1.

fields.name: default-inbound-action
fields.note: 0 = Allow; 1 = Block. Microsoft baseline: 1 (Block). Attacker changing DomainProfile DefaultInboundAction to 0 allows all inbound traffic on domain networks without explicit rule — broad exposure.

fields.name: default-outbound-action
fields.note: 0 = Allow; 1 = Block. Windows default: 0 (allow outbound). Rare to change — defense-hardened environments sometimes force 1 (block outbound) with explicit allow-rules. Attacker rarely touches this because legitimate posture is already Allow.

fields.name: disable-notifications
fields.note: 1 = suppress firewall block notifications. Attackers may set this to hide the popup toast a user would see when firewall blocks the attacker's inbound / outbound attempt.

fields.name: log-dropped-packets
fields.note: 1 = log drops to pfirewall.log; 0 = don't log. Attacker clearing this hides evidence of blocked attacker traffic. Microsoft baseline: 1.

fields.name: log-successful-connections
fields.note: 1 = log successful connections; 0 = don't log. Microsoft baseline: 1 for at least one profile (usually all).

fields.name: log-file-size
fields.note: Max log size in KB. Default 4096 (4 MB). Attacker reducing to very small value = force rapid rollover that loses evidence window.

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.note: LastWrite on each profile subkey advances when EnableFirewall / DefaultAction / Logging values change. Pair with System EVTX events for tamper timeline.

fields.name: mpssvc-state
fields.note: MpsSvc (Windows Firewall service) startup type. 2 = Automatic (default baseline). 4 = Disabled. Attacker setting Start=4 + reboot disables the entire firewall service — profile-level EnableFirewall becomes moot because the service doesn't start. Broader tamper than profile-level disable.

fields.name: wpd-device-id
fields.note:
Long composite subkey name. Two common patterns: MASS-STORAGE (USB flash, external HDDs — the forensically common case): `SWD#WPDBUSENUMROOT#UMB#2&&0&STORAGE#VOLUME#_??_USBSTOR#Disk&Ven_&Prod_

&Rev_##{}` — contains USBSTOR InstanceID verbatim (the `` segment) MTP/PTP (phones, cameras, media players): `WPDBUSENUMROOT#UMB#2&&0&_##_?{MTP/PTP-device-id}` — no USBSTOR serial; MTP devices bypass mass-storage enumeration Parser: split on `#` and search for `USBSTOR` segment; if present, the segment immediately after `#Disk&Ven_...&Prod_...&Rev_...#` IS the USBSTOR InstanceID. If `USBSTOR` absent, device is MTP — use alternate device-id decoder (Windows Portable Devices API), different identity guarantees.

fields.name: friendly-name
fields.note: volume label — survives most cleanups that hit USBSTOR

fields.name: container-id-property
fields.note: same ContainerID GUID recorded in USBSTOR Properties and Microsoft-Windows-Partition/Diagnostic event 1006 — primary cross-artifact join for physical-device identity

fields.name: instance-id-property
fields.note: Device InstanceID as Property (mirror of the value embedded in the subkey name). Parser convenience — read this rather than parsing the long subkey name path when available.

fields.name: device-desc-property
fields.note: Device description, typically 'USB Mass Storage Device' or vendor-supplied string. Cross-references USBSTOR's DeviceDesc value.

fields.name: class-guid-property
fields.note: Device class: WPD-wrapper GUID (`{eec5ad98-8080-425f-922a-dabf3de3f69a}` for WPDBUSENUM) vs. DiskDrive (`{4d36e967-...}`). Distinguishes MTP from mass-storage devices.

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.note: Not 'last connected' — only updates when Windows Portable Devices re-enumerates OR when FriendlyName changes (e.g., user renames the volume). First write corresponds to first WPD enumeration of the device.
fields.update-rule: set-on-enumeration-or-FriendlyName-change

nodes.name: Winlogon-Extended
nodes.title-description: Extended Winlogon persistence values (AppSetup / GPExtensions / Notify / System / Taskman / UiHost / VMApplet / AvailableShells)

fields.name: app-setup
fields.note: command line run by Winlogon during one-time post-install setup. Should be empty on any normal post-setup system. Populated = persistence that runs once on next boot with SYSTEM privilege.

fields.name: gp-extensions
fields.note: Group Policy Client-Side Extensions. Each sub-GUID registers a DLL that runs during policy processing. Attackers add a GUID with an attacker DLL to get SYSTEM execution whenever policy refreshes (every 90 min by default).

fields.name: gina-dll
fields.note: Pre-Vista GINA (Graphical Identification and Authentication) DLL. Replaced by Credential Providers on Vista+ but the value still honored on legacy/XP systems. Trojan GINA = complete credential interception.

fields.name: notify-packages
fields.note: Legacy Winlogon notification packages. Each sub-key registers a DLL that receives callbacks for logon/logoff/lock/unlock events. Classic NT4–XP persistence; still recognized on modern Windows if present.

fields.name: system
fields.note: apps launched by Winlogon in SYSTEM context at user logon. Default: LsaIso.exe on systems with Credential Guard; empty otherwise. Any unexpected entry runs as SYSTEM during interactive-session setup.

fields.name: taskman
fields.note: replacement for taskmgr.exe (fires when user presses Ctrl+Shift+Esc). Normally unset = default taskmgr. Non-empty value = persistence triggered by user's Task Manager shortcut.

fields.name: ui-host
fields.note: Winlogon UI screen application (Win10+). Default LogonUI.exe. Replacement = intercept login UI.

fields.name: vm-applet
fields.note: Virtual memory applet (rundll32 + sysdm.cpl historically). Deprecated but still honored. Replacement = execution on VM applet launch.

fields.name: available-shells
fields.note: alternative shell candidates. Winlogon consults this list if the primary Shell value is unavailable. Adding an entry here is a secondary-path persistence for shell hijack — fires only when explorer.exe can't launch.

fields.name: userinit
fields.note: comma-separated list of executables run AFTER logon; default is 'C:\Windows\system32\userinit.exe,'

fields.name: shell
fields.note: the shell process launched for a user; default is 'explorer.exe'

nodes.name: WinSock2-LSP
nodes.title-description: Winsock 2 Layered Service Provider catalog — DLLs injected into every network-connected process

fields.name: provider-path
fields.location: Protocol_Catalog9\Catalog_Entries\<NNNNNNNN>\PackedCatalogItem (binary blob) — contains UTF-16LE DLL path at offset
fields.note: Each catalog entry's PackedCatalogItem binary blob embeds the full DLL path of the service provider. Legitimate Windows LSPs point to %SystemRoot%\System32 (rsvpsp.dll, mswsock.dll). Third-party LSPs used to be common for AV / firewall / parental-controls products — modern equivalents use WFP. A novel LSP DLL path outside System32 and not matching a known security product = network-traffic-intercept persistence.

fields.name: provider-name
fields.note: Human-readable name of the provider ('MSAFD Tcpip [TCP/IP]'). Stock names are well-known; an unfamiliar ProtocolName is a trivially-visible anomaly.

fields.name: namespace-provider
fields.note: Name-service provider DLL (hooks name resolution APIs). Stock Windows has a couple of namespace providers (NLA, NTDS, PNRP). Extra entries = injected into every gethostbyname / GetAddrInfoEx call system-wide.

fields.name: chain-length
fields.note: LSP chain depth. 0 = base provider; 1 = 'layered' provider that sits above a base. An LSP with chain length >0 wraps an existing provider — the common hijack pattern is to insert a layered LSP above a core Winsock provider so that ALL traffic flows through the attacker's DLL.

fields.name: catalog-count
fields.note: Total number of entries. Baseline Windows 10/11 has ~30 entries. Substantially higher number = additional providers registered; compare against known-good.

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.note: LastWrite on the Parameters key updates whenever an LSP is installed / removed. An unexpected LastWrite timestamp = install window for the injected LSP.

nodes.name: WMI-Subscriptions
nodes.location-path: CurrentControlSet\Services\ESENT\Parameters\OBJECTS.DATA (binary database — parse via WMI namespace or Autoruns)

fields.name: filter-query
fields.note: WQL query that defines when the consumer fires — e.g., 'SELECT * FROM __InstanceModificationEvent WITHIN 60 WHERE TargetInstance ISA Win32_PerfFormattedData_PerfOS_System AND TargetInstance.SystemUpTime >= 200'

fields.name: consumer-type
fields.note: CommandLineEventConsumer runs a process; ActiveScriptEventConsumer runs VBScript/JScript inline

fields.name: consumer-script
fields.note: inline VBScript/JScript payload for script consumers — common malware carrier

fields.name: query-slot
fields.note: each slot holds one search string the user typed into Explorer's search box or Start menu search

fields.name: MRUListEx
fields.encoding: array of uint32-le (each = a slot number, most-recent-first)
fields.note: ordering of the numeric value names; same pattern as RecentDocs MRUListEx

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.update-rule: on each new search submission

nodes.name: WSL-Lxss
nodes.title-description: Windows Subsystem for Linux distro registry (HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Lxss)

fields.name: distribution-name
fields.note: Human-readable distro name as registered — 'Ubuntu-22.04', 'kali-linux', 'Debian', or an attacker-renamed value. Unusual or pentesting-oriented distro (kali, parrot) on a business endpoint is a strong signal.

fields.name: base-path
fields.note: Windows filesystem path holding the distro's rootfs (ext4.vhdx for WSL2; chroot dir for WSL1). Acquiring this path gives you the entire Linux environment including bash history, /tmp contents, and installed tools.

fields.name: packagefamily-name
fields.note: AppX package family — joins with Store install telemetry and StateRepository. Empty/missing PackageFamilyName with a populated BasePath = sideloaded distro (installed via .appx file or wsl --import, bypassing Store provenance).

fields.name: default-uid
fields.note: Default Linux UID the distro launches as. 0 (root) on an interactive distro is unusual — typical Store distros default to a newly-created non-root user.

fields.name: flags
fields.note: Bitmask — 0x1 WSL_DISTRIBUTION_FLAGS_ENABLE_INTEROP (Windows exe invocation from inside), 0x2 APPEND_NT_PATH, 0x4 ENABLE_DRIVE_MOUNTING, 0x8 (reserved). 0x4 cleared would block /mnt/c access (rare); 0x1 set is the default and enables the 'WSL → Windows binary' bypass vector.

fields.name: version
fields.note: 1 = WSL1 (pico-process translation), 2 = WSL2 (managed VM with ext4.vhdx). WSL2 is stealthier for host-EDR because Linux syscalls happen inside a utility VM the host agent can't inspect.

fields.name: default-distro
fields.note: GUID of the distro `wsl.exe` launches by default when invoked with no argument. Joins back to a {GUID} subkey.

fields.name: key-last-write
fields.note: LastWrite on each distro's GUID subkey reflects install / first-run / version-upgrade time. New LastWrite on a managed workstation without a corresponding IT ticket = user self-installed WSL.

fields.name: AppId
fields.note: contains one entry per platform (Windows, Web, etc.) with the executable path or PWA ID

fields.name: PackageIdHash
fields.note: hashed Windows Store package ID when applicable

fields.name: Payload
fields.note: application-specific content — for Edge, includes page title, URL, tab-group; for Office, the document path

fields.name: ActivityType
fields.note: 5 = user-engagement / 6 = Cortana-task / 10 = foreground-notification; 5 is the primary forensic type

fields.name: ActivityStatus
fields.note: 1 = active / 2 = ended / 3 = ignored

fields.name: OriginalLastModifiedOnClient
fields.note: timestamp on the originating device when the activity is synced from another device via MSA

fields.name: ClipboardPayload
fields.note: cross-device clipboard content when Clipboard History + cloud clipboard are enabled; the CSIDL data-type code precedes

fields.name: host_key
fields.note: cookie domain scope; leading dot means 'and subdomains'

fields.name: value
fields.encoding: DPAPI (Chrome v80+) — key encrypted in Local State → os_crypt.encrypted_key
fields.note: plaintext 'value' column is empty in v80+; all tokens in encrypted_value

fields.name: expires_utc
fields.note: 0 = session cookie (deleted at browser close)

fields.name: samesite
fields.note: -1 unspecified / 0 None / 1 Lax / 2 Strict

fields.name: target_path
fields.note: final disk path of downloaded file

fields.name: current_path
fields.note: path during active download (.crdownload suffix); equals target_path when complete

fields.name: tab_url
fields.note: URL of the page that INITIATED the download (not necessarily the download URL itself)

fields.name: original_mime_type
fields.note: server-declared MIME — diverges from mime_type when Chrome reclassified; forensic anomaly signal

fields.name: state
fields.note: 0 in-progress / 1 complete / 2 cancelled / 3 interrupted / 4 cancelled-by-tab-close

fields.name: danger_type
fields.note: SafeBrowsing verdict — 0 not-dangerous / 1 dangerous-file / 2 dangerous-url / others

fields.name: hash
fields.note: content SHA256 Chrome computed post-download — pivot against threat-intel directly

fields.name: chain-url
fields.note: redirect chain from initial URL to final bytes; multiple rows per download

nodes.name: Chrome-History
nodes.integrity-mechanism: SQLite WAL — recent transactions may not be in main DB

fields.name: url
fields.note: full URL including scheme, host, path, query

fields.name: title
fields.note: HTML <title> at time of visit

fields.name: typed-count
fields.note: how many times the user typed this URL (vs following a link) — high typed_count = bookmarked-equivalent

fields.name: last-visit-time
fields.encoding: Chrome-epoch microseconds (μs since 1601-01-01 UTC — same as FILETIME but in μs)
fields.note: convert: unix_ts = (chrome_ts / 1000000) - 11644473600

fields.name: visit-time
fields.note: each row in visits = one visit event; urls.last_visit_time is the max across visits rows

fields.name: visit-transition
fields.note: bit 0-7 = core type (LINK, TYPED, BOOKMARK, RELOAD, etc.); bits 8+ = qualifier flags

fields.name: from-visit
fields.note: references parent visit row — reconstruct browse trail via this FK chain

fields.name: download-target-path
fields.note: filesystem path where a downloaded file was saved

fields.name: password_value
fields.encoding: Chrome v80+ — AES-GCM with DPAPI-wrapped key in Local State (same scheme as Chrome-Cookies); v79 and earlier — raw DPAPI blob

fields.name: url_rank
fields.note: rank among most-visited; survives browser open/close

fields.name: autofill-entry
fields.note: form-field autofill history — captures names, addresses, phone numbers, search terms typed into forms

fields.name: profile-sid
fields.location: derived from path segment `%APPDATA%\Dropbox\` — the owning user's SID resolves via ProfileList ProfileImagePath
fields.note: Not a column in filecache.dbx — derived from the filesystem path's owning-user-profile. Required to attribute sync-cache evidence to a specific user account.

fields.name: file_journal-entries
fields.note: server-synced file metadata: local_path, server_path, size, rev, modified-time, namespace

fields.name: server_path
fields.note: Dropbox cloud path where the file lives

fields.name: encrypted_value
fields.encoding: DPAPI-wrapped AES-GCM (identical scheme to Chrome-Cookies) — key in Local State → os_crypt.encrypted_key

fields.name: typed_count
fields.note: times URL was manually typed (vs. clicked) — user-intent discriminator

fields.name: visit_time
fields.note: per-visit row; urls.last_visit_time is the max over this set

fields.name: from_visit
fields.note: chain-predecessor visit id for navigation reconstruction (back/forward, redirects)

fields.name: transition
fields.note: PAGE_TRANSITION_* core type + qualifier bits — typed (0), link (1), auto-bookmark (2), auto-subframe (3), reload (8), etc.

fields.name: value
fields.note: Firefox cookies are stored PLAINTEXT in moz_cookies.value — no per-cookie encryption (unlike Chromium)

fields.name: annotation-content
fields.location: moz_annos → content (for rows with anno_attribute_id mapping to 'downloads/destinationFileURI' or 'downloads/metaData')
fields.note: downloads/destinationFileURI holds the target file URI; downloads/metaData holds a JSON blob with source URL, mime, size, referrer

fields.name: profile-sid
fields.location: derived from path segment `%APPDATA%\Mozilla\Firefox\Profiles\<profile>\` — the owning user's SID resolves via ProfileList's ProfileImagePath match against the Users\<username> parent
fields.note: Not a column in moz_formhistory — derived from the filesystem path's owning-user-profile. Required to attribute form-submission evidence to a specific user account.

fields.name: fieldname
fields.note: name of the form field the value was submitted under (e.g. 'email', 'query', 'username')

fields.name: value
fields.note: literal value the user typed into the field — search terms, email addresses, usernames, plaintext form data

fields.name: host
fields.location: moz_places — rev_host column (reversed-domain for fast suffix search)
fields.note: stored in REVERSED form ('moc.elgoog' for 'google.com') — parsers must un-reverse

fields.name: last-visit-date
fields.encoding: Firefox-epoch microseconds (μs since 1970-01-01 UTC — Unix epoch)
fields.note: Firefox uses Unix epoch in μs, unlike Chrome's 1601-epoch — parsers MUST branch on browser family

fields.name: visit-type
fields.note: 1=link, 2=typed, 3=bookmark, 4=embed, 5=redirect-permanent, 6=redirect-temporary, 7=download, 8=framed-link

fields.name: from-visit
fields.note: referrer-visit id; chain for trail reconstruction like Chrome's from_visit

fields.name: place-id
fields.note: FK to moz_places.id — join to get URL

fields.name: handler-primary-id
fields.note: PackageFamilyName or AppUserModelID — joins to AppID concept

fields.name: notification-payload
fields.note: XML or JSON content of the toast — title, body, image URLs. Persisted UNTIL the user dismisses, then deleted.

nodes.name: OneDrive-SafeDelete
nodes.title-description: OneDrive SafeDelete.db — per-user SQLite journal of files sent to Recycle Bin via OneDrive
nodes.integrity-mechanism: SQLite page-level integrity only; no signing

fields.name: local-path
fields.note: Full local path of the OneDrive-synced file that was deleted. For a user hiding exfil traces, this is the 'what did they try to hide' evidence.

fields.name: resource-id
fields.note: GraphDriveItemId — joins against OneDrive server-side audit logs and Graph API. Enables cross-checking what the cloud thinks happened vs. what the local client logged.

fields.name: deletion-time
fields.note: When the OneDrive client moved the item to Recycle Bin. More reliable than the Recycle Bin $I file's deletion-time field when the user has emptied the Recycle Bin before the investigation.

fields.name: size
fields.note: Byte count of the deleted file. Large deletions (documents > 10 MB, zip archives) stand out and pair naturally with 'file was exfiltrated then removed' narrative.

fields.name: parent-folder
fields.note: Graph driveItem ID of the containing folder. Joins back to OneDrive-SyncEngine state to reconstruct the folder hierarchy of deletions.

fields.name: db-mtime
fields.note: NTFS mtime — updated on every new row. Pairs with row timestamps as an independent clock.

fields.name: file-id
fields.note: GUID identifying the cloud item — joins with OneDrive server logs

nodes.name: AnyDesk-Logs
nodes.title-description: AnyDesk remote-access logs (ad.trace, ad_svc.trace, connection_trace.txt, service.conf)

fields.name: ad-id
fields.location: connection_trace.txt — each line is 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS UTC <AD-ID>'
fields.note: The AnyDesk ID (9-10 digit number assigned to this AnyDesk instance) of the remote peer that connected or was connected to. For an attacker using AnyDesk as C2, this ID is their endpoint's AnyDesk ID — an IOC that joins to threat intel (many ransomware crews reuse AnyDesk IDs across intrusions). CONNECTION_TRACE IS ONE OF THE MOST VALUABLE SINGLE FILES IN A RANSOMWARE INVESTIGATION.

fields.name: connection-timestamp
fields.note: Exact moment of each inbound/outbound connection. Build per-session timeline directly from this file.

fields.name: peer-ip-address
fields.location: ad.trace or ad_svc.trace — 'connecting to <IP>' / 'accepted from <IP>' log lines
fields.note: Source or destination IP of remote AnyDesk peer. May be the attacker's VPN egress, a residential proxy, or a compromised host. For ransomware cases, cross-reference against threat-intel feeds of known attacker infrastructure.

fields.name: trace-event
fields.note: Per-operation log lines: session-start, session-end, file-transfer events, clipboard syncs, input events. Session-file-transfer lines identify data exfil (attacker uploads a file to the compromised host; attacker downloads a file off the host).

fields.name: file-transfer-events
fields.note: ALSO HIGH-VALUE: AnyDesk's built-in file-transfer tool is the attacker's exfil / tool-delivery channel. Every file transfer is logged with filename, direction, and timestamp. Correlates with payload-drop and data-exfil indicators elsewhere (Prefetch of tool binaries, UsnJrnl of exfil staging directories).

fields.name: service-conf-id
fields.note: THIS host's AnyDesk ID. In a case where the victim was reached via an attacker's AnyDesk session, this ID is the victim's own — useful for validating which host of many is 'the one the attacker connected to.'

fields.name: service-conf-password
fields.note: AnyDesk unattended-access password hash. Attackers who set an unattended password to guarantee re-entry leave this artifact; the hash is a detectable forensic footprint even if the attacker deletes the live service.

fields.name: file-mtimes
fields.note: NTFS mtime = most recent log write time. Brackets the most recent AnyDesk session.

fields.name: log-line
fields.encoding: YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS, LEVEL CBS COMPONENT: message

fields.name: package-identifier
fields.note: package manifest identifier — matches entries under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Component Based Servicing\Packages

fields.name: sfc-results
fields.note: System File Checker (sfc /scannow) output — verified integrity violations appear here exclusively

nodes.name: CMD-History-Doskey
nodes.location-path: NOT PERSISTED — lives in memory of the cmd.exe process until its session ends

fields.name: command-history-buffer
fields.location: cmd.exe process memory, F7 recall / doskey /h
fields.note: visible on a live system via `doskey /history` inside an active cmd.exe. Lost when the process exits. Memory-forensics tools can recover from a captured RAM image via Volatility's cmdscan / consoles plugins.

nodes.name: CrashDump-MEMDMP
nodes.exit-attribution-sentence: The Complete Memory Dump is the largest and contains the most information, including some User-Mode memory (Microsoft, 2025).

fields.name: dump-type
fields.note: 1 = full, 2 = kernel, 3 = small/minidump, 5 = triage

fields.name: bsod-bugcheck-code
fields.note: 0x7E = system thread exception, 0x1E = unhandled kernel exception, 0x3B = system service exception, etc.

fields.name: kernel-memory-body
fields.note: kernel memory (or subset) at crash time — analyzable with WinDbg or Volatility. Contains live credentials, registry in memory, process list, loaded modules.

fields.name: crashing-process-path
fields.location: derived from kernel-memory-body — !process command in WinDbg or Volatility psscan walks the EPROCESS list to recover the crashing-context image path
fields.note: Not a DUMP_HEADER field — derived by walking the captured EPROCESS / PSACTIVE_PROCESS_LINKS during post-mortem analysis. Names the process that was current on the CPU when the crash happened (relevant when analyzing driver-triggered BSODs like 0x7E or 0x3B).

fields.name: crashing-process-pid
fields.location: derived from kernel-memory-body — the UniqueProcessId of the current-thread's EPROCESS
fields.note: Not stored in the DUMP_HEADER — derived the same way as crashing-process-path. Correlates with concurrent Security-4688 / Sysmon-1 events to bracket the crash into a session window.

nodes.name: CryptnetUrlCache
nodes.title-description: Cryptnet URL Cache (content + metadata files — every URL wininet fetched for signature verification)

fields.name: content-file
fields.note: The actual downloaded content. For Authenticode revocation checks (the common case) this is a small CRL or certificate file. For .application / ClickOnce / msi signature checks it may be the signed artifact itself.

fields.name: metadata-file
fields.note: Binary metadata file. Header contains FILETIMEs (LastModified, Expiry, LastSync), HTTP status code, content length, and — critically — the full URL that was fetched. Parsers (EZ Tools' CryptnetURLCacheParser, KAPE cryptnet module) extract the URL from offset ~0x20 onwards.

fields.name: url
fields.encoding: utf-16le or utf-8 depending on Windows build
fields.note: The source URL. THIS is the forensic gold — reveals what the system attempted to download in the background for signature / revocation checks. Includes both signed-binary CRL lookups AND any URL passed to CryptQueryObject / WinVerifyTrust.

fields.name: last-modified
