fields.note: From the HTTP response's Last-Modified header. Indicates when the remote content was last updated.

fields.name: expiry
fields.note: From HTTP Cache-Control / Expires. Controls when the local cache entry is considered stale.

fields.name: last-sync
fields.note: When the local cache entry was last refreshed from the remote URL — i.e., when the system actually went to the network to fetch or re-validate. For forensic purposes THIS is 'when Windows reached out to this URL.'

fields.name: response-size
fields.note: Size in bytes of the cached Content. Cross-reference with Content\<hash> file size for integrity.

fields.name: http-status
fields.note: HTTP status of the last fetch (200 / 304 / 404). 404 or non-200 on a URL that is NOT a Microsoft revocation endpoint is a hunt signal.

fields.name: content-file-mtime
fields.note: NTFS mtime of the Content file. Pairs with MetaData LastSync as an independent timestamp.

fields.name: scanned-path
fields.note: files scanned during real-time protection — captures every on-access scan

fields.name: threat-name
fields.note: e.g., 'Trojan:Win32/Emotet', 'Behavior:Win32/SuspPowerShellExec'

fields.name: action-taken
fields.note: Allow / Block / Clean / Quarantine / Delete

fields.name: user-context
fields.note: the user context under which the scan occurred

fields.name: engine-version
fields.note: MsMpEng version; useful for timeline of which detections were available

nodes.name: DHCP-Client-log
nodes.location-path: Primary: Microsoft-Windows-Dhcp-Client/Operational (evtx). Legacy text log: %WINDIR%\System32\LogFiles\DHCPSRVLOG* on servers

fields.name: lease-event
fields.location: evtx event ID 50066 / 50067 (DHCPv4) or text-log line on servers
fields.note: modern Windows records lease assign/renew/release in the evtx channel; the text-log form primarily applies to Windows DHCP SERVER role

fields.name: mac-address
fields.note: client MAC — complements NetworkList-profiles GatewayMac for same-network attribution

fields.name: log-line
fields.encoding: YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS, LEVEL DISM COMPONENT: message

fields.name: command
fields.note: every dism.exe or DISM-cmdlet invocation logged with full argv

nodes.name: Hosts-File
nodes.title-description: Windows hosts and lmhosts files (local name-to-IP resolution override)

fields.name: mapping
fields.location: each non-comment line in hosts — format: '<ip-address>  <hostname> [alias ...]'
fields.note: The DNS client resolves each hostname against hosts FIRST, before any real DNS query. Attacker-authored mappings redirect name resolution — e.g., pointing security-products' update hosts to 127.0.0.1 blocks AV updates; pointing legitimate domains to attacker IPs hijacks traffic.

fields.name: lmhosts-mapping
fields.location: each non-comment line in lmhosts — format: '<ip-address>  <NetBIOS-name> [#PRE | #DOM:<domain> ...]'
fields.note: NetBIOS-name to IP pre-loading. Largely obsolete on modern Windows with DNS-only resolution, but still honored when NetBIOS-over-TCP/IP is enabled. #PRE entries are pre-cached. An attacker-populated lmhosts can intercept SMB connections by name.

fields.name: file-mtime
fields.note: NTFS modified time. Default stock hosts file (just comments plus the localhost entries on older Windows) shows an mtime matching the OS install or a Windows Update that touched it. Any mtime outside those windows = edit.

fields.name: file-size
fields.note: Default stock hosts on Win10/11 is ~824 bytes (comments + empty mapping section). Size > 1 KB on a stock install = entries were added. Security products sometimes legitimately add blocklist entries here (making it massive); cross-reference against the product installed.

fields.name: comment-text
fields.note: Stock comments describe the file format and attribution. Attackers sometimes add comments to mask their entries as legitimate. Comment-only diff vs stock = mild signal.

fields.name: log-line
fields.encoding: YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS srcip srcport dstip dstport protocol verb url statuscode siteId reason queuename

fields.name: reason
fields.note: Timer_ConnectionIdle, Connection_Dropped_List_Full, Forbidden, BadRequest, URL_ProcessingFailed, ...

fields.name: log-line
fields.encoding: configurable field list; default includes date/time, s-ip, cs-method, cs-uri-stem, cs-uri-query, s-port, cs-username, c-ip, cs(User-Agent), cs(Referer), sc-status, sc-substatus, sc-win32-status, time-taken

fields.name: cs-username
fields.note: authenticated-only; '-' for anonymous

fields.name: cs(User-Agent)
fields.note: signature for scanner identification — sqlmap, Nikto, curl, ...

nodes.name: PCA-Win11
nodes.title-description: Program Compatibility Assistant (Win11) — plaintext launch-tracking (PcaAppLaunchDic.txt / PcaGeneralDb*.txt)

fields.name: executable-path
fields.note: Full path of the launched executable. One of the few DFIR artifacts on Win11 that preserves INTERACTIVE user launches with a UTF-16LE plaintext timestamp AND survives standard cleanup. ShimCache's timestamps are confusing (last-modified of the binary, not launch time); Amcache has launch data but is ESE-format binary; PCA provides a PLAINTEXT, PARSEABLE, TIMESTAMPED launch log.

fields.name: launch-time
fields.encoding: yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss (UTC, no timezone offset — verify per system)
fields.note: The actual process-launch time. UNLIKE ShimCache's last-modified-of-binary timestamp, this is WHEN THE USER RAN THE BINARY. Plaintext, trivially parseable, directly usable on a timeline — a rarity among Windows execution-evidence artifacts.

fields.name: compat-issue-flag
fields.note: PCA tracks whether the OS detected a compatibility issue for each launch. For forensic purposes this is secondary — the value of PCA is the launch record itself, not the compatibility-assistance outcome.

fields.name: record-version
fields.location: PcaGeneralDb*.txt — file header / per-line format version
fields.note: Format has evolved across Win11 feature updates (21H2 → 22H2 → 23H2). Parse with tools aware of the evolution (community parsers include a version-detection step).

fields.name: file-mtime
fields.note: NTFS mtime — updates on every PCA write. The most-recent launch recorded inside the file matches the file-level mtime within a few seconds. Serves as independent check against embedded timestamps.

fields.name: db-rotation
fields.location: PcaGeneralDb0.txt vs PcaGeneralDb1.txt — which file is 'active'
fields.note: Two-file rotation. The active file grows until a size cap, at which point writes swap to the other. Acquire BOTH files — older launches live in whichever file is currently inactive.

nodes.name: Power-Efficiency-Diagnostics
nodes.title-description: powercfg /energy / /sleepstudy reports — process + USB + driver snapshot at report-generation

fields.name: report-generation-time
fields.location: embedded in HTML: 'Scan Time' / 'Computer Name' / 'System Report' header
fields.note: The moment powercfg ran. Anchors every other field to this instant. Pair with file mtime — matching = legitimate, mismatch = tamper.

fields.name: running-processes
fields.location: HTML section 'Platform Power Management Capabilities' / process tables
fields.note: Process snapshot at scan time — process name, PID, context. For an investigation where Security-4688 has rolled, a preserved energy-report.html captures the process list at report-generation. Attacker binaries running at that moment surface in the table.

fields.name: usb-device-list
fields.location: HTML section 'USB Suspend' / 'USB Device not entering Suspend'
fields.note: Connected USB devices with hardware-ID, friendly name, and suspend-state info. Snapshot of USB devices plugged in at scan time — adds a timestamp point to the USB-device-history timeline built from USBSTOR / setupapi logs.

fields.name: loaded-drivers
fields.location: HTML section 'System Availability Requests:Driver Requests' + kernel-mode driver list
fields.note: Drivers loaded at scan time with path + version. Attacker rootkit / malicious driver manifests here — cross-reference against known Microsoft + enterprise-deployed drivers. Useful when CodeIntegrity-3077 events / Sysmon-6 (DriverLoaded) have rolled.

fields.name: device-config
fields.location: HTML section 'Device Power Requests' + 'Battery' + device tables
fields.note: Power-managed device inventory — monitors, keyboards, HIDs, network adapters, storage controllers. Forensic use case: detecting unusual attached devices (unfamiliar HID / USB storage) at report time.

fields.name: network-interfaces
fields.location: HTML section 'Processor utilization' with network counter breakdown
fields.note: Active network interfaces + recent I/O statistics. Useful for confirming active egress during report-generation window.

fields.name: file-mtime
fields.note: NTFS mtime — matches report-generation-time within seconds. Offset = tamper signal.

nodes.name: PowerShell-Profile
nodes.title-description: PowerShell $PROFILE script — auto-executed on every PowerShell session start

fields.name: profile-script
fields.note: Arbitrary PowerShell code. Executes in the current user's security context every time a PowerShell session launches. Attacker-authored profile scripts commonly: (1) import a malicious module from disk or HTTP, (2) set aliases that replace common commands (gci, ls, dir) with attacker-wrapped versions, (3) run an in-memory payload directly via Invoke-Expression + base64-decoded string, (4) register a PSReadLine key-handler that logs user keystrokes.

fields.name: profile-mtime
fields.note: NTFS mtime. For a legitimate user profile, the file rarely changes after initial setup. A recent mtime outside documented script-editing windows = plant.

fields.name: profile-size
fields.note: Unexpectedly large profile size (> few KB) = lots of content that may include embedded encoded payloads. Unexpectedly tiny size on an otherwise-active user = legitimate minimal profile OR a recently-wiped previously-populated plant.

fields.name: ise-profile-present
fields.note: Windows PowerShell ISE has its own profile file. On modern Windows (PowerShell 5.1 era) ISE is increasingly deprecated. An attacker targeting ISE specifically is unusual and may indicate targeting of admin-user workflow (ISE is a power-user tool).

fields.name: dot-sourced-inclusions
fields.location: . C:\path\to\other.ps1 lines inside the profile script
fields.note: Dot-source inclusions (`. C:\some\other\file.ps1`) chain other scripts. Attacker plants may use this to keep the profile file itself small (one line) while the actual payload lives elsewhere — inspect every dot-sourced path referenced by the profile.

nodes.name: proxy-log
nodes.location-path: deployment-specific — Squid: /var/log/squid/access.log ; Zscaler: cloud; ISA/TMG: W3C-formatted in custom path
nodes.integrity-mechanism: depends on deployment — many proxies forward to SIEM for immutable storage

fields.name: timestamp
fields.encoding: epoch-seconds OR ISO-8601 depending on proxy

fields.name: bytes-transferred
fields.note: large PUT/POST bytes = potential exfiltration signal

fields.name: authenticated-user
fields.note: when available, ties the request directly to a Windows user

fields.name: command-line
fields.note: the verbatim PowerShell command line the user typed

fields.name: line-ordinal
fields.note: approximates chronological order; file grows append-only until 4096-line rollover

fields.name: file-mtime
fields.note: individual commands lack timestamps — file mtime is the ONLY temporal anchor (last command's approximate time)

nodes.name: ScheduledTask-XML
nodes.title-description: Scheduled Task XML definitions (System32\Tasks\) — richer view than registry Scheduled-Tasks
nodes.integrity-mechanism: XML is plain text; no signing

fields.name: actions-exec-command
fields.note: Executable path the task runs. For attacker-authored tasks, commonly points to cmd.exe / powershell.exe with an encoded-command argument in the sibling <Arguments> element. Also common: rundll32.exe invocations and direct script-interpreter calls (wscript, cscript, mshta).

fields.name: actions-exec-arguments
fields.note: Command-line arguments. Attacker tasks routinely encode the payload here (PowerShell -enc <base64>, mshta javascript: URL, etc.). The registry TaskCache does NOT preserve full Arguments in a single accessible value — the XML is where the complete command-line lives.

fields.name: principals-user-id
fields.note: Account the task runs as. Tasks running as SYSTEM, LOCAL SERVICE, NETWORK SERVICE, or a specific domain-admin account are higher-privilege execution — always check Principal for each task. Attacker persistence tasks frequently specify SYSTEM for maximum impact.

fields.name: principals-runlevel
fields.note: HighestAvailable = elevated (run with full admin token if the UserId supports it). LeastPrivilege = restricted. Attacker tasks almost always use HighestAvailable.

fields.name: triggers
fields.location: XML //Triggers element (LogonTrigger, TimeTrigger, CalendarTrigger, BootTrigger, EventTrigger, IdleTrigger, RegistrationTrigger, SessionStateChangeTrigger)
fields.note: The conditions that fire the task. BootTrigger + SYSTEM UserId = boot-persistence equivalent of a service. LogonTrigger + specific UserId = per-user-logon persistence. EventTrigger referencing a specific EVTX channel + event ID = rare but powerful (fires on arbitrary system events — e.g., on every Security-4624 of a target user).

fields.name: registration-info-author
fields.note: Author field recorded at task creation. Legitimate Microsoft-shipped tasks have 'Microsoft' or 'Microsoft Corporation'. Attacker-authored tasks frequently leave this blank, copy the Microsoft value to blend, or set it to a computer-name + account tuple (e.g., DESKTOP-ABC123\user) — which directly reveals the creating account.

fields.name: registration-info-date
fields.note: Task creation timestamp embedded in the XML. Joins with the XML file's NTFS mtime for cross-verification. Discrepancy between embedded Date and file mtime = potential tampering (Date is typically set at creation and not updated).

fields.name: security-descriptor
fields.note: Task's security descriptor lives in the sibling registry, NOT in the XML. Governs who can read/modify/delete the task. Attacker tasks sometimes have overly permissive or contradictory SDs — included here as a cross-reference note.

fields.name: file-mtime
fields.note: NTFS mtime — updated on task modification (including UpdateExistingTask API). Comparison against the embedded RegistrationInfo/Date gives create-vs-modify visibility that registry TaskCache alone does not offer.

nodes.name: Search-Gather-Logs
nodes.title-description: Windows Search gather logs — per-indexer-run records of what content was indexed when (.gthr / .Crwl)

fields.name: crawl-path
fields.note: Path of each item visited in the crawl. Includes local filesystem paths, mail-store item IDs (Outlook), and any other Search-enabled content source. Reveals filesystem paths the indexer reached — and thus files that existed at crawl time even if they've since been deleted. Attacker-dropped files the indexer had time to scan before deletion appear here.

fields.name: crawl-result
fields.note: Status of each crawl attempt: SUCCESS, FAILED, ACCESS_DENIED, IN_USE, etc. Access-denied on a path the indexer normally reaches = signal of ACL tampering. Large-scale FAILED entries during attacker-window = possible deliberate indexer disruption.

fields.name: batch-start-time
fields.note: When the crawl batch began. Gives the temporal anchor: 'indexer was scanning filesystem at this moment.'

fields.name: batch-end-time
fields.note: When the batch finished. Short batches with many items = fast-changing directory; long batches = large / slow content source.

fields.name: items-processed
fields.note: Number of items processed in this batch. Correlates crawl intensity with user activity periods. Abnormal spikes / drops can indicate malware activity (many new files created) or tampering (indexer disabled / errors).

fields.name: file-mtime
fields.note: NTFS mtime = when the file was written. For recent logs this is very close to the embedded batch-end-time. For archived logs it's the same — these files are not modified after creation.

fields.name: log-rotation
fields.note: Sequence number. Sorted chronologically gives an ordered history of crawl batches. Default retention keeps dozens of batches on an active system — weeks of indexer timeline recoverable.

fields.name: entry-timestamp
fields.note: local-time in log; use the trailing '+/-<offset>' marker (when present) for UTC normalization

fields.name: device-instance-id
fields.note: USBSTOR\Disk&Ven_X&Prod_Y&Rev_Z\<SERIAL> form — matches USBSTOR registry instance id

fields.name: driver-inf
fields.note: 'oem*.inf' or 'Windows\INF\<class>.inf'

fields.name: install-status
fields.note: 0x00000000 = success; non-zero = install error

nodes.name: Setupapi-Upgrade-Log
nodes.title-description: setupapi.upgrade.log — device / driver events recorded during Windows in-place upgrades (Win10+)

fields.name: device-install-records
fields.location: log lines matching 'dvi:' or '[Device Install (DiInstallDriver)]' sections
fields.note: Per-device install blocks recording hardware-ID, driver-INF path, install outcome, and timestamp. Preserves device-enumeration events that occurred specifically during the upgrade window.

fields.name: device-hwid
fields.note: Hardware ID of enumerated devices (VID/PID for USB; bus-specific format for internal). USB hardware IDs appearing here (USB\VID_xxxx&PID_xxxx format) indicate the device was plugged in during the upgrade — a device-history breadcrumb independent of USBSTOR's steady-state record.

fields.name: driver-inf-path
fields.note: Driver INF package path as selected for install. For attacker-signed driver delivery during an upgrade window, this field names the specific INF — combines with driver-signing investigations.

fields.name: event-timestamp
fields.encoding: yyyy/MM/dd HH:mm:ss.SSS (local time on host; check LogTimestampSource)
fields.note: Per-event timestamp — brackets the upgrade operation window. File-level mtime gives the last log-write time; individual event timestamps give the fine-grained per-device moments within the upgrade.

fields.name: upgrade-start-end
fields.location: log lines — '>>>  [Setup Identifier (DiInstallDevice)]' / upgrade phase markers
fields.note: Phase markers delineating upgrade stages. Useful for correlating which devices were enumerated DURING the upgrade vs before/after.

fields.name: file-mtime
fields.note: NTFS mtime = last write. Matches the upgrade completion timestamp. File size is a rough proxy for how many devices / drivers the upgrade touched.

nodes.name: WER-Report
nodes.title-description: Windows Error Reporting Report.wer files — crash metadata fingerprinting every faulting binary

fields.name: event-name
fields.note: WER event classification — 'APPCRASH', 'BEX', 'BlueScreen', 'LiveKernelEvent 141/177/etc.', 'CbsPackageServicingFailure2'. For malware analysis, APPCRASH (native app crash) and BEX (buffer overflow exception) reports are the highest-yield — they fingerprint an execution that failed.

fields.name: app-name
fields.note: Executable name (without path) that crashed. For malware that caused legitimate apps to crash (DLL injection gone wrong, shellcode overrun), AppName points at the VICTIM — the injected host process. The ATTACKER binary typically surfaces in ModulePath.

fields.name: app-path
fields.location: Report.wer INI — [AppPath] key or Parameter1 (AppVer) + path-reconstructable context
fields.note: Full path of the crashing executable. Joins with Amcache / Prefetch / Security-4688. CRITICAL: WER records path EVEN for binaries that were later deleted or renamed — so Report.wer is often the only on-disk evidence that a now-missing attacker binary ran.

fields.name: module-path
fields.note: Module (DLL) that caused the fault. For injection-failure crashes, ModulePath IS the attacker DLL. For exploit attempts that crashed the target, ModulePath may point at the vulnerable legitimate DLL that was called incorrectly.

fields.name: app-version
fields.note: File version and PE timestamp of the crashing binary. Useful for correlating against known-malicious IOCs (file-version strings, specific builds) and for cross-referencing the binary's compile time against intrusion timeline.

fields.name: exception-code
fields.note: 0xC0000005 = access violation (most common). 0xE0434352 / 0xE0434f4D = .NET exception. 0xC00000FD = stack overflow. Specific codes distinguish native crashes vs. managed-runtime crashes vs. OS-lib errors, narrowing analysis focus.

fields.name: report-time
fields.location: Report.wer INI — EventTime (FILETIME integer) or file $SI mtime
fields.note: Embedded EventTime is the moment the fault occurred. File mtime is moments later (when WER finished writing). Both should align. A significant delta or a rewritten Report.wer mtime = tampering.

fields.name: user-sid
fields.note: For per-user reports (LocalAppData path), the SID is implicit from the containing directory. For ProgramData reports, the user-SID of the faulting process may be recorded in session metadata. Pair with Security-4624 to attribute the crash to a specific logon session.

fields.name: companion-dmp
fields.note: Minidump of the crash. When present, this is the FULL process-memory snapshot at fault time — carveable for strings, credentials, decrypted payloads. Much more than Report.wer metadata alone. Acquire the whole <Kind>_<HashName> directory, not just Report.wer.

fields.name: log-line
fields.note: format — "YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS.mmm PID TID Component MESSAGE"

fields.name: update-id
fields.location: lines mentioning GUID update IDs (often in the MESSAGE body)

fields.name: package-name
fields.location: lines mentioning KB number or package name (KB5001649, etc.)

fields.name: timestamp
fields.update-rule: append-on-event

nodes.name: YARA-hits
nodes.location-path: deployment-specific — typically SIEM-forwarded or a scanner-specific output dir
nodes.integrity-mechanism: none unless forwarded to immutable SIEM

fields.name: file-hash
fields.note: typically SHA256 in modern YARA outputs

fields.name: tags
fields.note: e.g., 'malware,trojan,APT29' — rule author's categorization

fields.name: profile-sid
fields.location: derived from path segment `%LOCALAPPDATA%\` — the owning user's SID resolves via ProfileList ProfileImagePath
fields.note: Not stored in the cache file itself — derived from the per-user %LOCALAPPDATA% path. Required to attribute icon-cache evidence to a specific user's Explorer interactions.

fields.name: entry-hash
fields.note: same Windows path-hash as thumbcache; matches thumbcache entries for the same source file

fields.name: identifier-string
fields.note: Win10+ stores source file path; earlier OSes abbreviate

fields.name: profile-sid
fields.location: derived from path segment `%LOCALAPPDATA%\` — the owning user's SID resolves via ProfileList ProfileImagePath
fields.note: Not stored in the cache file itself — derived from the per-user %LOCALAPPDATA% path. Required to attribute thumbnail-cache evidence to a specific user's Explorer activity. Matches the pattern used on sibling IconCache.

fields.name: entry-hash
fields.note: computed from the source file path via a Windows-specific path-hash; used as primary key. NOT a cryptographic content hash — do not confuse with SHA-1/SHA-256 file hashes. Matching entries across thumbcache_<size>.db files share this hash.

fields.name: identifier-string
fields.note: most commonly the source file path (Win10+); may be abbreviated or hash-encoded on older builds

fields.name: data-blob
fields.encoding: JPEG / PNG / BMP (extension-free, detect by magic)
fields.note: the actual thumbnail image — extractable to disk as normal image

fields.name: cache-file-mac
fields.note: DB-level last-write captures most-recent thumbnail addition

nodes.name: AppID
nodes.description:
16-character uppercase hex representation of a 64-bit CRC64 hash of a
Windows Application User Model ID (AppUserModelID). Used as the filename
prefix for jump-list artifacts and as a key name in several taskbar- and
shell-related registry locations. Lets an examiner correlate evidence
belonging to the same application across multiple artifacts.

roles.description: AppID used as filename prefix of an .automaticDestinations-ms / .customDestinations-ms file
AppID referenced in TaskbarLayout as a user-pinned application
AppID referenced in MUICache per-app display-name entries

nodes.name: ContainerID
nodes.description:
Windows-assigned GUID identifying a physical device across all of its
logical views (disk, volume, partition, portable-device interfaces).
Stable across reformatting and repartitioning of the same device. Does
NOT survive controller replacement or cryptographic erase.

roles.description: Stable Windows-assigned GUID uniquely identifying a physical device across its logical views

nodes.name: DeviceSerial
nodes.description:
USB device serial number. Advertised by the device in its USB descriptor,
or synthesized by the OS when the device declines to report one. On Windows,
OS-synthesized serials are flagged by an '&' at character position 2 of the
USBSTOR <instance-id>.

roles.description: USB device serial — the primary identity of a USB storage device as advertised or OS-synthesized

nodes.name: DomainName
nodes.description:
Fully-qualified or partial DNS domain name. Captured by any artifact that
records name resolution, network communication, or domain-associated
content. Primary pivot for "where did this host go?" questions.

roles.description: Domain portion of an email address (from/to/cc or message-header source)
Authority host of an HTTP/HTTPS request or browsed URL
Hostname that was resolved (DNS cache entry or DNS-query event)
DNS suffix configured for a known network profile
Domain of an authenticated account — AutoLogon DefaultDomainName, 4624 TargetDomainName, cached-credential domain

nodes.name: EmailAddress
nodes.description:
RFC-5321 email address — local-part + '@' + domain-part. Captured by email
clients, anti-phishing telemetry, identity artifacts, and some malware
that hardcodes C2 email channels.
nodes.canonical-format: local-part@domain (case-insensitive by convention; domain IS case-insensitive per DNS)

roles.description: Sender address on a message
Recipient address (to/cc/bcc) on a message
Email-form identity cached for authentication or user-mapping purposes

nodes.name: ExecutableHash
nodes.description:
Cryptographic hash of a PE file's contents (SHA1, SHA256, MD5, or IMPHASH).
The canonical way to identify the same executable across artifacts
regardless of path or filename. Primary pivot against threat-intel feeds.

roles.description: Hash of an executable observed at the moment of execution
Hash of a file flagged by security tooling
Hash observed during a scan without necessarily being flagged
Hash captured as content of the artifact itself — certificate thumbprint, signed-binary catalog hash, file-hash embedded in a log line — not tied to an execution / detection / scan event

nodes.name: ExecutablePath
nodes.description:
Full filesystem path to a program executable. Captured by many execution-
evidence artifacts — Prefetch, Amcache, ShimCache, BAM/DAM, UserAssist,
Security-4688, Sysmon-1, SRUM. Combining the path with a run timestamp
is the cornerstone of "what executable ran and from where" questions.
nodes.canonical-format: absolute filesystem path with drive letter or device path (e.g., 'C:\Windows\System32\powershell.exe')

roles.description: Executable that actually ran — captured by execution-evidence artifacts
Executable configured to run via a persistence mechanism (Run keys, Services, Tasks, etc.)
DLL loaded into a process (AppInit_DLLs, module-load events)
Process that is the actor of a specific telemetry event (file create, registry set, DNS query, etc.)
Executable path scanned or matched by security tooling (AV, YARA)
Executable path referenced by shell-level caches without necessarily being executed

nodes.name: FilesystemVolumeSerial
nodes.description:
32-bit volume serial number written into the filesystem's boot sector at
format time. Distinct from the 128-bit Mount Manager VolumeGUID. Appears
in shell artifacts that preserve volume identity at the moment of access
(LNK files, shellbags, jump lists, prefetch).

roles.description: Filesystem volume serial captured at a moment of per-user access (shell artifacts)
Filesystem volume serial captured at runtime (Prefetch volumes section)

nodes.name: FILETIME100ns
nodes.description:
Microsoft Windows FILETIME — a 64-bit unsigned integer counting
100-nanosecond intervals since midnight UTC on January 1, 1601
(Gregorian). The canonical timestamp encoding across the Windows
kernel, filesystem ($MFT / $FILE_NAME timestamps), registry
(key-last-write), event log ($TimeCreated), and most DFIR-relevant
binary structures.

roles.description: Point-in-time value recorded by a Windows subsystem (kernel, filesystem, event log, registry)
Difference between two FILETIMEs — used for age / interval computations
Low-order 100-ns ticks as a forensic precision-vs-tool tell (see Galhuber 2022 timestomp research)

nodes.name: FirewallRuleName
nodes.description:
Windows Firewall rule identifier — a string name or GUID that uniquely
identifies a firewall rule. Appears as a subkey value in the FirewallRules
registry path and as the RuleId field in Firewall/Firewall evtx events.
nodes.canonical-format: string (friendly name) OR GUID in registered-rule format

roles.description: Rule's canonical identifier — registry value name and evtx RuleId match
Rule name on add events (2004)
Rule name on modify events (2006)
Rule name on delete events (2033)

nodes.name: GPTPartitionGUID
nodes.description:
16-byte GUID identifying a partition on a GPT-formatted disk. Analogous
to MBRDiskSignature + partition-offset for MBR disks, but scoped to the
partition rather than the whole disk. Written to the GPT Partition Entry
Array at disk initialization; Windows preserves it across remounts.
Distinct from GPT-Disk-GUID (whole-disk identity, different field).
nodes.canonical-format: 16-byte GUID; mixed-endian per the GUID wire format

roles.description: GPT partition GUID as embedded in MountedDevices binding-data Format 3 — a 24-byte value whose first 8 bytes are 'DMIO:ID:' and trailing 16 bytes are the partition GUID
GPT partition GUID as recorded by the Partmgr kernel driver in Partition/Diagnostic-1006 PartitionTableBytes (parsed out of the raw GPT Partition Entry Array)

nodes.name: HandleId
nodes.description:
Windows kernel object handle identifier — a numeric value the kernel
assigns when a process opens a handle to an object (file, registry
key, process, token, etc.). Correlates events within a single
open-access-close lifecycle: 4656 (handle requested) and 4663 (object
access) and 4658 (handle closed) — or 4657 (registry value set) and
Sysmon-13 — share the same HandleId when they describe operations on
the same opened handle. Intra-process + intra-logon scope ONLY.

roles.description: The handle identifier during a single access event (4663, 4657) — ties this access to the 4656 that opened the handle and the 4658 that will close it
The handle identifier emitted when a handle is first requested (4656) — starts the lifecycle
The handle identifier on a close event (4658) — ends the lifecycle; HandleId no longer references a valid handle after this point

nodes.name: IPAddress
nodes.description:
IPv4 or IPv6 address. Captured by any artifact involving network
communication — resolver caches, connection logs, firewall events,
proxy records, TCP/UDP session artifacts.
nodes.canonical-format: IPv4 dotted-quad (1.2.3.4) or IPv6 colon-hex (::1, fe80::...)

roles.description: Originator of a network flow or audited action
Target of a network connection
IP produced by DNS resolution
Source IP recorded on an authentication event
Intermediate SMTP relay captured in a Received-by header chain
Client IP in a web-proxy access log

nodes.name: Location
nodes.description:
Geographic location — lat/lon coordinate, geofence boundary, place name,
or cell-tower fix. Captured by Windows location providers (GPS, Wi-Fi
positioning, cell triangulation) and surfaced in Cortana / Timeline /
Maps / virtual-assistant artifacts.
nodes.canonical-format: latitude/longitude decimal degrees (WGS84); optional radius (meters) for geofences

roles.description: Location value recorded by a subsystem at a point in time
Location value defining the edge of a geofence trigger
Location attached to a user-created reminder (arrive/leave trigger)

nodes.name: LogonSessionId
nodes.description:
Windows logon session identifier (LUID). Assigned by the Local Security
Authority when a user session is created (Security-4624) and referenced
by every subsequent audit event that occurs within that session until
the matching 4634 or 4647 logoff. Format: 0xHEXHEX - a pair of DWORDs.

roles.description: The logon event that ASSIGNS this LUID — Security-4624 TargetLogonId, Security-4648 TargetLogonId
The LUID recorded on in-session events (4688 SubjectLogonId, 4663, 4672, 5140, 4647, 4634)

nodes.name: MachineNetBIOS
nodes.description:
NetBIOS name of the machine whose Distributed Link Tracking service stamped
an object (LNK file, jump list entry, etc.). Preserved even when the object
is copied to another machine — making it one of the rare cross-host
attribution artifacts on Windows.
nodes.canonical-format: ASCII string, up to 15 characters, uppercase conventional

roles.description: NetBIOS machine name from a LNK-format TrackerDataBlock — cross-host provenance

nodes.name: MBRDiskSignature
nodes.description:
32-bit disk signature written to the MBR at offset 0x1B8 by Windows at
disk initialization. Identifies the PHYSICAL DISK, not any volume or
filesystem on it — persists across partition reformats and filesystem
changes within the disk, changes only when the MBR itself is rewritten.
Distinct from FilesystemVolumeSerial (volume-level, regenerates per format)
and from DeviceSerial (hardware-firmware, device-level identity).
nodes.canonical-format: uint32 LE; often shown as 8 hex chars, e.g., 'A1B2C3D4'

roles.description: MBR disk signature as carried in the MBR sector itself — the authoritative source
MBR disk signature as embedded in MountedDevices binding-data Format 2 — the first 4 bytes of a 12-byte MBR-case binding
MBR disk signature as recorded by the Partmgr kernel driver in Partition/Diagnostic-1006 EventData\Mbr\Signature

nodes.name: MFTEntryReference
nodes.description:
The NTFS Master File Table identifier — a 64-bit value composed of a 48-bit
entry (record) number and a 16-bit sequence number. Uniquely identifies a
specific MFT record for the life of the filesystem, and can pivot to the
$MFT artifact for complete lifecycle history of a file or folder.

roles.description: This MFT entry IS the record's own reference (MFT artifact primary identity)
MFT reference to the parent directory of the entity (path reconstruction)
MFT reference captured by a shell artifact pointing at a specific file/folder
MFT reference captured as the target / object of a surrounding operation — clipboard drop, search crawl, copy-move destination

nodes.name: PIDL
nodes.description:
Shell Item ID List — a serialized pointer-to-item-ID-list structure
(ITEMIDLIST) that encodes a location in the Windows shell namespace
as a chain of variable-length shell items. The canonical "what folder
or file was referenced" payload shared across Explorer browsing state,
file-dialog state, shortcut targets, and jump list destinations. Also
known as a Shell Item in the LNK specification context.
nodes.canonical-format: binary blob of concatenated ITEMIDLIST entries; null-terminated; interior items carry FAT/DOSDate MAC timestamps and MFT entry+sequence references on NTFS

roles.description: PIDL captured as a folder the user browsed via the Explorer shell (ShellBags BagMRU numbered values)
PIDL captured as a file/folder referenced through a standard Open/Save file dialog (OpenSavePidlMRU, LastVisitedPidlMRU)
PIDL captured as the target Shell Item chain embedded in a .lnk file (LNK Shell Item IDList) or in a jump list DestList entry

nodes.name: ProcessId
nodes.description:
Windows process identifier — an OS-assigned 32-bit integer that uniquely
identifies a running process within a single boot session. Appears in
security audit events as NewProcessId (4688, the process just created),
ProcessId (4663/4657, the actor process accessing an object), and
SubjectProcessId (various). The numeric join key that threads 4688
process-creation events to the 4663/4657 object-access events the
resulting process generates.
nodes.canonical-format: hex uint32 (Windows native); also shown as decimal in some tools

roles.description: The process identifier assigned to a newly-created process (4688.NewProcessId — the process that just came into existence)
The identifier of the process that spawned a child (4688.ProcessId — the parent field in process-creation events)
The process identifier of the process performing an action (4663.ProcessId, 4657.ProcessId, Firewall events' modifying-process) — the runtime actor

The process identifier of the object being accessed by cross-process events (Sysmon-8.TargetProcessId on CreateRemoteThread, Sysmon-10.TargetProcessId on ProcessAccess, Security-4688.TargetProcessId on CreateProcessAsUser)
PID captured as context of a broader session / memory snapshot — e.g., process-list embedded in a hibernation file, crash-dump process list — not as the actor of a specific event

nodes.name: RegistryKeyPath
nodes.description:
Registry key path string (e.g., "HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows\\
CurrentVersion\\Run"). Value-type concept — two registry artifacts
that reference the same key-path are corroborating observations of
that key, not proof of identity. The on-disk registry hive + key
name together IDENTIFY a key absolutely; the string representation
is the portable form analysts use for reporting and cross-referencing.

roles.description: The registry key this artifact primarily describes (e.g., Run-Keys records HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run as its subject).

nodes.name: ServiceName
nodes.description:
Windows service name — the SCM-level short identifier for a service
(e.g., "PSEXESVC", "Spooler", "W32Time"). Distinct from DisplayName
(human-readable) and from ServiceDll/ImagePath (the binary).
nodes.canonical-format: ASCII string, conventionally < 256 chars; case-preserved but case-insensitive match

roles.description: Service's canonical short name — Services registry subkey name and System-7045 ServiceName field both carry this
Service name recorded on state-change events (start / stop / fail) — System-7036
Service name recorded at install-time — Security-4697, System-7045

Service name captured via its persistence footprint (ETW autologger provider subkey, Windows Firewall profile service state, other registry-resident service references) — not from a live state-change or install event

nodes.name: TaskName
nodes.description:
Windows Scheduled Task name/path — the forward-slash-delimited path
that uniquely identifies a task (e.g., "\Microsoft\Windows\Defrag\ScheduledDefrag",
"\MyEvilTask"). Both the Task Registry TaskCache\Tree\<path> key and the
TaskScheduler/Operational evtx events carry this value.
nodes.canonical-format: string path beginning with backslash, forward-slash-separated folders

roles.description: Task's canonical path under \Root — registry subkey path and evtx TaskName field match
Task name on run events (TaskScheduler-200, 201)
Task name on register/update events (TaskScheduler-106, 140)

Task name mentioned as a scheduled-execution object (pre-execution, config-only) — task-XML config reference, GPP scheduled-task preference, Security-4699 deletion — distinct from execution / registration events

nodes.name: URL
nodes.description:
Full URL — scheme + host + path + query. Captured by browser histories,
cache artifacts, file-download provenance markers (Zone.Identifier),
and web-proxy logs. Contains a `DomainName` as a substring.

roles.description: URL the user browsed to
URL that served a downloaded file
The page that referenced a subsequent navigation or download
URL recorded in a web-proxy access log entry
URL embedded in another artifact's content (email body, file metadata)

nodes.name: UserSID
nodes.description:
Windows Security Identifier for a user principal. Persistent across
rename and password-change; the canonical cross-artifact key for user
attribution.

roles.description: SID is the definition of a user identity (ProfileList entry, SAM account)
SID of the account being authenticated in a logon event
SID of the user subject performing an audited action
SID of the account that is the target of an administrative action
SID of the user whose NTUSER profile owns this per-user artifact

nodes.name: VolumeGUID
nodes.description:
Globally unique volume identifier assigned by Windows Mount Manager on
volume mount. Uniquely identifies a specific volume (partition-plus-
filesystem) across sessions. Does not necessarily survive reformatting.

roles.description: Volume GUID as captured at the system mount level (MountedDevices, Partition/Diagnostic)
Volume GUID as captured in per-user access artifacts (MountPoints2, shellbags, LNK target chain)

nodes.name: VolumeLabel
nodes.description:
Filesystem volume label — the user-visible string written at format time
or by label.exe. Frequently survives cleaner tooling that targets device-
history registry keys (particularly Windows Portable Devices, which most
cleaners miss).
nodes.canonical-format: utf-16le string, up to 32 chars for NTFS, 11 for FAT

roles.description: Volume label captured at a moment of per-user access (shell artifacts)
Volume label as known to device-enumeration layer (WPD, legacy ReadyBoost)

links.note: The shim database itself — a compiled collection of shim rules (pattern matches against executables plus actions to apply). Attacker shim databases typically contain a single entry: 'when <target>.exe runs, apply InjectDll / RedirectEXE / RedirectShortcut action pointing to attacker DLL/EXE'. sdb2xml (Python) parses the format; SDBExplorer (Willi Ballenthin) provides GUI inspection.

XML rule collection. Each rule allows or denies execution of Exe / Msi / Script / Dll / AppX based on: Publisher (cert signer), Product Name, File Version range, Hash, or File Path. Attacker-modified AppLocker rule adding an Allow-Path for an attacker-controlled directory = effective AV-bypass on AppLocker-enforced hosts. Check for Allow rules targeting %TEMP% / %APPDATA% / user-writable paths.

The URL BITS fetched from (download job) or posted to (upload job). An attacker-configured BITS job pointing to a C2 URL is one of the most durable exfil / download persistence mechanisms on Windows — BITS runs as SYSTEM, survives reboot, throttles gracefully to avoid detection, and resumes automatically across network outages.
Local destination path (download) or source path (upload). Download jobs writing to %TEMP% / user-writable paths that later match Amcache execution entries = payload delivery completed.

Text content of indexed queries / tiles. Surfaces recent Cortana search queries, suggestion content, and cached web-result snippets the UWP assistant displayed. Similar in flavor to browser search history but specifically Cortana-sourced.

Direct audio recording of user voice command. Can be played back to hear exact user speech at query time. Voice-biometric material for user-attribution cases (speaker recognition) in addition to the semantic content of the query.

Target of the credential (what it authenticates to). For RDP mstsc /savecred: 'Domain:target=<hostname>'. For Outlook: 'LegacyGeneric:target=<outlook-service-id>'. For Git: 'git:https://<host>'. Reveals which services the user / attacker authenticated to. Attacker-added credentials (for persistence / lateral movement staging) reveal attacker infrastructure.

The encrypted master-key file. Structure: header + MASTERKEY_BLOB (PBKDF2-salt + encrypted AES-256 key) + LOCAL_BACKUP_KEY + DOMAIN_BACKUP_KEY (when domain-joined). Decrypted with user password hash OR domain DPAPI backup key. Resulting plaintext AES key is the master key used to decrypt all DPAPI blobs of that user.

GPP-deployed scheduled tasks. Every task definition includes: target executable, arguments, trigger, run-as context, creation timestamp. Attacker-modified ScheduledTasks.xml in SYSVOL deploys ransomware-execution tasks to every client — classic T1053.005 mass-execution vector. ALSO subject to cpassword in some legacy versions (run-as passwords for tasks that need to run with a specific credential).

Target HKLM or HKCU path that the policy writes. Enumeration of all target-paths = complete enforced-registry-setting baseline. Attacker-authored policies targeting Defender exclusion paths, LSA Run Level, Credential Delegation settings, or audit-policy-disable flags = persistence-enforcement plant.

Every process that was running at hibernate time — with PID, parent, command-line, and in-memory artifacts. Attacker processes that existed only in memory (fileless malware, injected payloads) appear here. Compare against surviving on-disk evidence — discrepancies ('this process ran but nothing on disk') = fileless.

Locally-unique identifier for the Windows logon session that holds this ticket. Joins to Security-4624 LogonID field. Multiple tickets under the same LogonID = tickets acquired during the same user-session.
User principal name (user@REALM) or NT-format username. The authenticated identity this ticket represents.

If the tab was opened from a saved file, this field records that path. Absence of a path = pure untitled buffer (most common forensic case: the user was drafting something they never intended to persist).

Raw image as delivered by the notifying app. For messaging apps (Teams, Slack, WhatsApp, Signal for Windows), this may include received-message preview images, attachment thumbnails, and profile pictures of senders. Surfaces visual content the user saw without having to recover the full app state.

UNC server name whose shares are cached. Reveals which file servers the user's CSC has mirrored content from. Enterprise environments typically have one or two primary file servers — additional unexpected server names = investigate. Hostnames of decommissioned servers preserve in CSC even after the server is gone.

Browser and application URL fetches leave full URL strings in pagefile pages. Useful when browser history is cleared or the browser uses a DNS-over-HTTPS stack that bypasses host DNS telemetry — the URL string still lands in the browser's paged-out working set.
Username of the account that submitted the print job. Joins with logon session data (Security-4624) to tie the job to a specific session. For domain printers, DOMAIN\user format.
Not stored in the cache file itself — derived from the per-user cache directory path. Required to attribute RDP-session bitmap evidence to a specific user account that ran mstsc.exe.

Full path of an executable the Application Compatibility engine observed being launched. Cache is populated by the AELookup service as part of Windows' shim-selection logic. For Win7-era execution-evidence investigations, this is the direct predecessor of Amcache's InventoryApplicationFile data.

Command the task runs. For surviving-from-legacy .job files, the path may reference long-gone executables (software uninstalled years ago) — but occasionally surfaces as attacker-placed-long-ago-and-forgotten persistence. Double-check any .job file referencing an extant executable against current scheduled-task enumeration tools: if a .job file references a running binary that DOESN'T appear in schtasks / Get-ScheduledTask output, the legacy task may be dormant OR (rare) actively used.
User-chosen filename when 'Save As' was used. Filename semantics (e.g., 'customer_list.png', 'password_prompt.png') frequently reveal intent directly.

Each suspended region contains the UWP app's identity markers. Cross-reference against the StateRepository (AppRepository\StateRepository-Machine.srd) to attribute a recovered region to a specific Store app. Unique to swapfile — pagefile has process-context-free pages.

FileDrop format — when the user copied FILES (not file contents) to clipboard (e.g., Ctrl+C on selected files in Explorer). List of full paths. Reveals which files the user staged in clipboard for subsequent paste.

Root directory for one user's Windows Hello enrollment. Contents: 1.dat / 2.dat / 3.dat (encrypted blobs for PIN protector, TPM-wrapped key, metadata), Policies subkey (container policies), linking / signing metadata. The entire directory must be acquired for offline analysis.

Action invoked when a Filter fires. CommandLineEventConsumer with an ExecutablePath field pointing outside System32 / Program Files = classic WMI-persistence attacker consumer. ActiveScriptEventConsumer with ScriptingEngine='VBScript' holding a base64 / obfuscated script body = scripted payload.

SHA-256 of the boot manager. Known-good hashes per Windows build are published by Microsoft. Unknown hash = replaced / tampered boot manager (bootkit). Compare against Microsoft's published baseline AND against the file's Authenticode signature (must chain to Microsoft UEFI CA).
Boot Configuration Data hive. Separate artifact (BCD-Store). Its location on the ESP (vs. legacy-BIOS C:\Boot\BCD) matters for acquisition — forgetting to acquire from ESP misses the UEFI BCD.

THE MBR disk signature. Written by Windows at disk initialization (diskmgmt, diskpart, Windows Setup). Read by Windows on every boot to correlate the disk against Boot Configuration Data. Referenced by MountedDevices binding-data (Format 2 — MBR case) and by PartitionDiagnostic-1006 EventData\Mbr\Signature.
Unique ID per snapshot. Exposed by `vssadmin list shadows` output. Used to reference the snapshot for mounting (`vssadmin mount` / mklink junction to \?\GLOBALROOT\Device\HarddiskVolumeShadowCopyN).
Text of each reminder. User-authored content — often reveals personal / professional context (appointments, people, errands, project names). Similar value to journal / notes recovery.
full executable path of the process that transferred bytes
SID that owned the process
Source IP address of the client contacting a UAL-instrumented server role (AD FS, IIS, RDP, DHCP, DNS, File Services, etc.). Per-row granularity keyed on (role, client IP, username, date).

KB identifier (e.g., KB5021233) or title text. Joins to MSRC advisories for what CVE(s) the update addresses. For incident-timeline work: 'was this host patched against CVE-XXX when the attack happened?' — answer by finding the KB corresponding to the CVE and checking its install status in store.db.
set of email-specific columns — ToAddress, FromAddress, Subject, Body, Attachments; index covers Outlook PST/OST if search scope includes them
filesystem path OR behavior-context identifier; format varies by detection type (file:_<path>, containerfile:_<archive>, behavior:_<ctx>, amsi:_<content>, webcontent:_<url>)
process that triggered the detection (Explorer during download scan, the malicious process itself for behavior hits)
SID under which the detection fired
Matches the USBSTOR / USB / WPD instance path — the same string used as registry subkey names.
Selected INF filename for the install (e.g. usbstor.inf_amd64_<hash>). The actual INF used, not the device-class default.
INF filename driving the service install (e.g. usbstor.inf).
Target device node for which the service is being created.
'USB\VID_xxxx&PID_xxxx\<SERIAL>' or 'USBSTOR\...\<SERIAL>' form
SID of the user context under which the rule-add occurred. Added in Win10 1903 — earlier 2004 events omit this field. When present, joins to Security-4624 (TargetUserSid) for session correlation.

Full path to the executable that added the rule (typically svchost.exe for Group Policy, powershell.exe for script-based, wf.msc wrapper for GUI). Join to Security-4688 where NewProcessName matches this path and TimeCreated window overlaps — yields the exact 4688 process-creation event that preceded this rule-add.
SID of the user context under which the rule-delete occurred. Added in Win10 1903. Combined with rule-delete-time proximity to a specific 4624 session, places the deletion within a user session.

Full path to the executable that deleted the rule. Join to Security-4688 NewProcessName + time-window overlap to identify the deleting process. Forensic IOC: ModifyingApplication of cmd.exe/powershell.exe/wscript.exe during an incident window suggests scripted cleanup.
PnP device instance path, e.g. USB\VID_0781&PID_5567\4C530001... — the canonical device-identity string used across USBSTOR, Enum\USB, Security-6416, and setupapi.dev.log.
PnP device instance path — same canonical identifier as 400, USBSTOR, setupapi.dev.log, Security-6416.

present when partition-style == MBR; 4-byte disk signature matching MountedDevices binding-data MBR-case encoding; Full MBR dump — allows independent recomputation of disk signature and partition layout even if the signature field is stripped.

Vasilaras / Dragonas / Katsoulis 2021 (DFIR Review, doi 10.21428/b0ac9c28.de3816b0): Vbr0 carries the VSN of the device's FIRST partition AT CONNECTION TIME. Persists even after user reformats — provides historical VSN attribution.; Second VSN if device has 2+ partitions. Only present on multi-partition MBR devices.; Third VSN. Vbr3+ fields are NOT populated even on 4+ partition devices — the event schema is capped at three VBRs.
GPT partition GUIDs; for GPT-formatted removables, one of these matches the VolumeGUID used by MountPoints2 and the 16-byte tail of MountedDevices' Format-3 binding-data (after the 'DMIO:ID:' prefix)
GPT partition GUIDs; for GPT-formatted removables, one of these matches the VolumeGUID used by MountPoints2 and the 16-byte tail of MountedDevices' Format-3 binding-data (after the 'DMIO:ID:' prefix)
user context at mount time — this is the single biggest reason this event is more forensically powerful than USBSTOR alone
SID of the account that cleared the Security log — almost always an admin
typically SYSTEM (S-1-5-18) for interactive logons; represents the authority that requested the logon

logon ID of the session that REPORTED the logon (usually 0x3e7 for SYSTEM); distinct from TargetLogonId which is the session being created; LUID of the paired logon session for UAC split-token scenarios (standard + elevated). Same interactive user produces two 4624s — their TargetLogonIds reference each other via TargetLinkedLogonId. v2 / Win10+.
the account being logged on — the forensic target

THE LUID — unique session identifier assigned by LSASS at logon. Appears as SubjectLogonId on every in-session 4688/4663/4657/4672/5140, and as TargetLogonId on the matching 4634/4647 logoff. The single most important session-scope join key in Security.evtx.
logon ID of the REPORTING session (0x3e7 for SYSTEM)
'S-1-0-0' (null SID) when the account doesn't exist — distinguishes typo from valid-user-wrong-password
same LogonId value that appeared in the corresponding 4624 logon event — use as session pairing key
matches the LUID assigned in the session-opening 4624 — closes the session window for any 4688/4663/etc. sharing this LUID
the user account that initiated the runas/explicit-credential action
logon ID of the CALLER's session (the session that supplied alternate credentials)
PID of the process that issued the explicit-credential call (consent.exe, runas.exe, mstsc.exe, ...). Joins to Sysmon-1 / Security-4688 for full process context.
the process that called the explicit-credential API — e.g., consent.exe (UAC), runas.exe, mstsc.exe
The handle value the kernel just assigned. This HandleId threads all subsequent 4663/4657 events on this open; the matching 4658 will close it.

Ties this write to the preceding 4656 that opened the key handle. ProcessId + HandleId + same-session yields the full context: which process, which session, what key was opened with what rights, what value was written.

The handle being released. After this event, this HandleId value is DEAD — the kernel may reuse it for a future 4656 in the same process, so any subsequent event carrying this HandleId in the same ProcessId is a NEW handle lifecycle, not a continuation.
PID of the process performing the access. Joins backward to the 4688 with NewProcessId==this value to identify when/how this process was created, its parent, its command line, and its SubjectLogonId.

Kernel handle identifier. Joins to the 4656 that opened this handle (same HandleId + ProcessId) and the 4658 that will close it. Same ObjectName accessed via two different HandleIds = two distinct open-access-close lifecycles on the same logical object.
SID being granted special privileges
ties this privilege assignment to a specific 4624 logon session; critical pivot
the account under which the new process was created

Session identifier — correlates with 4624 TargetLogonId for session context. Every process this session spawns carries this LUID as its SubjectLogonId, allowing backward chain 4688→4624 for full user-attribution.; LUID of the session the NEW process runs in (as opposed to SubjectLogonId which is the session that CREATED the process). Differs from SubjectLogonId only when runas / CreateProcessAsUser shifts identity. v2 / Win10+.
PID assigned to the newly-created process. Appears in subsequent 4663/4657/4658 events as ProcessId — the primary join key for reconstructing what this process did after creation.
PID of the process that spawned this one. Walk parent-child recursively by joining each parent-process-id to a preceding 4688's new-process-id to reconstruct the full process tree.
populated for runas / CreateProcessAsUser scenarios where the creator and target identity differ. v2 / Win10+.
logon ID of the installing session — joins to the originating 4624
the ImagePath configured for the new service — raw string, often includes arguments
PID of the process that RPC-called schtasks. Win10 1903+. Hunt-gold field — reveals the actual invoker when schtasks is abused remotely.
Parent PID of the invoking client process. Win10 1903+. Joins to 4688 to reconstruct the parent-child chain behind task creation.
SID of the user that deleted the task. For cleanup-trail investigations this identifies the removing actor.
Logon session that made the change. Joins back to Security-4624.
Full task path (e.g., \Microsoft\Windows\CustomTask\<task>). Joins to Security-4698 of the SAME TaskName in the earlier lifecycle event pair.

Account receiving the new right. Attacker-useful grants: SeDebugPrivilege (debug processes — credential dump), SeTakeOwnershipPrivilege (overwrite ACLs), SeBackupPrivilege (bypass NTFS ACLs — read any file), SeLoadDriverPrivilege (load unsigned driver).
Account that changed the policy. SYSTEM = automatic policy refresh from GPO. User SID = direct tamper.
the account that CREATED the new user (admin/SYSTEM)
session in which the creation was performed — joins to the 4624 that opened the admin session
SID of the NEW user account
Account whose password was reset. Classic attacker pattern: reset a dormant admin account's password to 'take it over' without knowing the original password.
Account that performed the reset. For attacker-initiated reset this is the compromised admin account.
Session LUID of the acting admin.
Account that was disabled. Pair with 4722 (enabled) for lifecycle. Attacker post-ops: disable legitimate admin accounts to force reactivation via their chosen channel.
SID of the account added. NTDS-dit lookup resolves to current account attributes + cross-reference against Security-4738 for any simultaneous attribute changes.
Account that made the add. Privileged-group-add actor tracking.
Session LUID of the acting admin. Threads to Security-4624 for full session context.
SID of the account added to the group. Joins to NTDS-dit / SAM for account context.
Account that performed the group-add. For mover / privilege-abuse investigations this is the person who added the member.
Session LUID of the acting admin. Threads to Security-4624 for context.
SID of the account that was changed. Joins to NTDS-dit / SAM for account-context and to subsequent auth events.
Account that MADE the change. For auditing: attacker-initiated changes (password reset, UAC flags, SID history addition) reveal who performed the modification.
Session LUID of the acting user — threads to Security-4624 for context.

NetBIOS name of the workstation attempting authentication — the CLIENT. Crucial for pass-the-hash detection: a workstation name that does NOT exist in the domain is a tell that an attacker forged the name. Also the same-workstation-many-accounts pattern reveals credential spraying.

Account being validated. Multiple 4776 events for the same account from the same workstation in close time = normal. Many DIFFERENT accounts from ONE workstation = spray / enumeration. One account from MANY workstations = credential reuse / lateral movement.
SID of the renamed account — PERSISTENT across the rename (SID doesn't change). Cross-reference with both old and new SAM name.
Account whose password hash was extracted by ADMT. In legitimate use: source-domain account whose credential material is being migrated to the target forest.

Account that invoked the ADMT PasswordMigrationFilter. For legitimate use: the administrator running ADMT. Any 4782 with a non-admin, non-migration-service SubjectUserSid is suspicious but should be triaged as unauthorized ADMT / misuse-of-migration-tooling rather than DCSync.
Session LUID of the acting account on the DC.

Path of the image whose hash didn't match. Attacker tamper with signed system binaries (e.g., overwriting a System32 DLL with a patched version) surfaces here. Cross-reference against Amcache SHA-1 to see WHICH hash was observed.
join key back to the 4624 session
source IP of the connecting host
PID of the process whose connection was permitted. Joins to Security-4688 NewProcessId / Sysmon-1 ProcessId for process attribution. Key join-field for the authentication-to-network-effect chain.

Kernel-format path of the executable initiating / receiving the connection. Requires drive-letter translation (via GetFinalPathNameByHandle or mapping against MountedDevices). Prefetch / Amcache lookup by filename provides execution-evidence context.
Source IP of the connection. For outbound (direction=0) from this host, equals this host's IP. For inbound (direction=1), equals the remote peer's IP.
Destination IP. For outbound = remote peer; for inbound = this host.
Logon session the connecting process belongs to. Joins back to Security-4624 TargetLogonId — completes the user→process→connection chain.
Credential target identifier — 'TERMSRV/<host>' for saved RDP, 'git:https://<host>' for Git credentials, 'LegacyGeneric:target=<service>' for saved Outlook. Reveals WHICH credential was read.
Account whose Credential Manager was read (is the process running under this user's context).
always SYSTEM / S-1-5-18 — PnP runs in kernel context; real attribution comes from correlating timestamp with user-context events
always 0x3e7 (SYSTEM logon) — same caveat
PnP instance path — casing trap: DeviceId (not DeviceInstanceId as used by Kernel-PnP)
PID assigned to this newly-created process. Joins forward to all subsequent Sysmon events for this process via ProcessGuid (more durable) or PID (faster, reuse-risky).
correlates with Security 4624 TargetLogonId — joins Sysmon-1 to the Security.evtx session window
which algorithms are included depends on Sysmon config (HashAlgorithms directive)
PID of the process that spawned this one. Recursively join each parent-process-id to a prior Sysmon-1's process-id to reconstruct the process tree.
typically lsass.exe in credential-access cases
PID of the process being OpenProcess'd. Join to Sysmon-1.process-id for the target's image+parent+command-line lineage.
the process that created the file
Process connecting TO the pipe. Pairs with Sysmon-17 (creation) to capture the full pipe-client-server handshake — detecting both the framework's server side and the lateral-movement client side.
PID of the process that issued the DNS query. Join to Sysmon-1.process-id for the resolver caller's image+command-line.
process that made the DNS query — unique visibility Sysmon provides that the OS resolver doesn't
reverse-PTR lookup of destination-ip — NOT the name the process resolved. For the actually-queried name, pair with Sysmon-22 (DNS) by matching process-guid + nearby timestamp.

Full path of the loaded driver. Microsoft drivers live under %SystemRoot%\System32\drivers\. Third-party drivers in vendor-specific paths (typically Program Files or dedicated driver directories). Attacker-loaded drivers in unusual paths (%TEMP%, user-writable locations) = candidate rootkit. BYOVD (Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver) attacks load legitimately-signed BUT KNOWN-VULNERABLE drivers to abuse their kernel-mode capabilities — check loaded drivers against Microsoft's known-vulnerable-driver list.

Cryptographic hashes of the driver binary. Cross-reference against VirusTotal / MSRC / internal threat intel for attribution. Microsoft's LOLDrivers project maintains a public list of known-vulnerable drivers by hash — Sysmon-6 hashes matching LOLDrivers = BYOVD indicator.

PID of the process that triggered the driver load (typically the Service Control Manager or System process). For user-initiated driver loads (e.g., via sc.exe or net start), PID identifies the initiator — joins to Security-4688 / Sysmon-1 process-creation record.
PID of the process into which the module was loaded.
the process into which the module was loaded
the DLL/EXE module that was loaded — this is the forensic target
hash of the LOADED module (not the process)
process creating the remote thread; process receiving the remote thread — commonly lsass.exe, explorer.exe, or a legitimate binary being hollowed
SID of the account that cleared the log — requires membership in Administrators / Backup Operators / Event Log Readers group OR equivalent 'Manage auditing and security log' right.
LUID of the session that initiated the clear. Joins to Security-4624 / 4634 around the event to identify how the clearing session was established (interactive / remote / scheduled / etc).
Host that recorded the event. Useful in multi-host investigations.
Host that recorded the event. Useful in multi-host investigations where evtx bundles land together and need sorting.
raw binary-plus-args as configured; may include service-host host PID or DLL path for svchost-style services
account that registered the task (domain\user or SID)
full path of the action binary — the ImagePath TaskScheduler will launch
'LOCAL' for console logons, IP for RDP
THE VSN. Offset differs from FAT32 (0x43) and NTFS (0x48). Same shell-artifact representation: 4-byte LE integer, often shown XXXX-XXXX in Explorer.
THE VSN. Shell artifacts (LNK, ShellBags, jump lists) record this 4-byte value at access time. Regenerated on every format.
DLT volume identifier at the target's creation — partially decodable as FilesystemVolumeSerial
full Shell Link structure per entry — shell-item list with volume-GUID / volume-label / MFT refs
full Shell Link structure per entry — shell-item list with volume-GUID / volume-label / MFT refs
full Shell Link structure per entry — shell-item list with volume-GUID / volume-label / MFT refs
full Shell Link structure per entry — shell-item list with volume-GUID / volume-label / MFT refs
full Shell Link per entry — MAC times, target path, tracker-block, volume info all present
full Shell Link per entry — MAC times, target path, tracker-block, volume info all present
full Shell Link per entry — MAC times, target path, tracker-block, volume info all present
full Shell Link per entry — MAC times, target path, tracker-block, volume info all present
full Shell Link per entry — MAC times, target path, tracker-block, volume info all present
full ShellLNK structure — every field of the LNK substrate applies: target path, volume IDs, TrackerDataBlock, etc.
inherited from the embedded ShellLNK structure — attributes the LNK to a specific volume VSN at the moment of link creation
inherited from the embedded ShellLNK structure — attributes the LNK to a specific volume VSN at the moment of link creation
MachineID of the HOST that CREATED the LNK, not the target server. Reveals cross-host LNK movement.
inherited from the embedded ShellLNK structure — attributes the LNK to a specific volume VSN at the moment of link creation
document path opened through Office apps
inherited from the embedded ShellLNK structure — attributes the LNK to a specific volume VSN at the moment of link creation
NetBIOS name of the host that created the LNK — survives copy, shows cross-host provenance
full shell-namespace chain to target; embeds volume-GUID when target is on a removable volume; embeds MFT entry+sequence when target is a file or folder on NTFS (from shell-item extension blocks)
full shell-namespace chain to target; embeds volume-GUID when target is on a removable volume; embeds MFT entry+sequence when target is a file or folder on NTFS (from shell-item extension blocks)
full shell-namespace chain to target; embeds volume-GUID when target is on a removable volume; embeds MFT entry+sequence when target is a file or folder on NTFS (from shell-item extension blocks)
32-bit FS serial; regenerated on reformat — see FilesystemVolumeSerial concept
NetBIOS name of the host whose DLT service stamped this LNK — travels with the file
executable (or script) launched at logon
if non-local, indicates the LNK was created on another host and copied in (supply-chain or lateral drop)
inherited from the embedded ShellLNK structure — attributes the LNK to a specific volume VSN at the moment of link creation
MFT reference of the file/subfolder this entry points at
the $MFT record this transaction acted upon
references the PARENT DIRECTORY's MFT record — walk these to reconstruct full path

SID of the file owner at SD-write time. Recovering historical owner SIDs from $SDS lets analysts identify which users created / owned specific files (including deleted ones). An attacker SID owning files in an unexpected directory = evidence of their prior activity.
parent directory at time of event — useful for path reconstruction of deleted files
the URL of the downloaded resource itself
short form — the full path is in the filename-strings section; pair them for canonical ExecutablePath
on Win8+, each referenced file carries its MFT entry+sequence — direct pivot to the $MFT at the time the prefetch was written
full RFC-5322 headers — contains Received chain, source IPs, SPF/DKIM results
full RFC-5322 headers — contains Received chain, source IPs, SPF/DKIM results
full RFC-5322 headers — contains Received chain, source IPs, SPF/DKIM results

Full original path of the deleted file (C:\Users\...\document.doc). Recovers the pre-delete filename AND the pre-delete directory. For cold-case investigations this is the direct evidence of what the user had in a location before deletion.
the SID of the account that deleted the file — cryptographically bound via folder ownership

Command line executed by userinit.exe at logon trigger. Stock Microsoft Active Setup components reference signed Microsoft binaries under %SystemRoot% (ie4uinit.exe, themeui.exe). A StubPath pointing to rundll32.exe with an attacker DLL argument, or to a non-Microsoft binary, or to a script-interpreter + inline command = textbook attacker plant.

Per-user 'last executed' stamp. Compare against HKLM.Version — if HKLM is newer (or HKCU entry missing), StubPath fires at next logon. For forensics, the ABSENCE of this value under a given user's NTUSER.DAT means that user has NOT yet received the component's StubPath execution.

Command line executed when WER triggers. Standard format contains %ld placeholders for PID and event handle. Legitimate Visual Studio entry: '"C:\WINDOWS\system32\vsjitdebugger.exe" -p %ld -e %ld -j 0x%p'. Attacker-authored entries replace the binary path with a dropper or injector — the crash of ANY application triggers attacker-code execution in the crashing process's security context.
install location of the application
leading four zeros indicate SHA1 algorithm; the remaining 40 chars are the PE content SHA1
full filesystem path of the .lnk file itself
resolved target of the shortcut — the executable the shortcut points at
the same ContainerID that appears in InventoryDevicePnp, MountedDevices, MountPoints2, and WPD
cross-device pivot — same physical device gets the same ContainerId across its logical interfaces
full path to the driver file (typically .sys)
INF file path (often %WINDIR%\INF\oem<N>.inf) — names the driver-install manifest that caused this driver package to be registered
Every DLL listed here is loaded into ANY process that calls CreateProcess / CreateProcessAsUser / WinExec. Broadest-reach DLL-injection persistence in Windows.
space-separated list of DLLs; EVERY user32-loading process loads them
full path of the executable; registered app launcher. Start → Run or Win+R resolves exe name via App Paths.
Domain scope of the auto-logon account. For local accounts this is the machine NetBIOS name; for domain accounts, the AD DNS or NetBIOS domain.
BAM's per-user scope is encoded directly in the key hierarchy — no ProfileList lookup needed
the value name IS the full executable path — BAM uses the path as its key

Path on the OSDevice to the OS loader (\Windows\system32\winload.efi or \Windows\system32\winload.exe). Redirecting this to an attacker binary = the single most privileged persistence on Windows because winload.efi runs before the OS itself (kernel-mode bootkit territory).

Cipher selection. 1 = AES128 (CBC); 2 = AES256 (CBC); 3 = AES128 XTS (modern default for OS/fixed/removable); 4 = AES256 XTS. The XTS variants are preferred on modern Windows. Enterprise policy may force a specific value — mismatched runtime state vs policy = misconfiguration.

GUIDs of key protectors applied to each BitLocker-protected volume. Key protector TYPES include: TPM only, TPM+PIN, TPM+Startup Key, Startup Key only, Password (non-OS volumes), Recovery Password (numerical 48-digit), Recovery Key (external .bek), AD DS recovery, Azure AD recovery, Auto-unlock. Each protector GUID in registry corresponds to a specific unlock method. Critical for investigations: 'Recovery Password' protector presence indicates a 48-digit numerical recovery key was generated and should be backed up somewhere (print, USB, AD, Azure AD).
TPM ownership state. BitLocker's TPM-bound protectors require TPM ownership — TPM registry gives TPM-clear and TPM-ownership-change events useful for interpreting BitLocker key-protector rotations.

Path to the in-process DLL implementing the COM object. Loaded into whatever process instantiates the CLSID. Hijack = replace with attacker DLL; every caller of CoCreateInstance(<CLSID>) loads the attacker code.; DLL that provides lightweight local handling of a remote COM object. Less common than InprocServer32 but equally weaponizable; same load semantics when the CLSID is instantiated locally.; Full executable path launched as its own process to host the COM object (out-of-process server). Attacker gets a new process spawning whenever the CLSID is activated; process name can be anything, persistence trigger is COM activation rather than boot/logon.

Full command line run at every cmd.exe start. Any command / script / binary invocation here is effectively invoked once per cmd.exe launch. Attackers commonly set this to a PowerShell one-liner ('powershell -w hidden -enc ...') or a path to a dropper .bat.

HKLM AutoRun fires for every user's cmd.exe; HKCU fires only for the current user but can be set without admin. HKCU is more stealthy for an unprivileged attacker who wants persistence in their own session.
current NetBIOS computer name; also mirrored under ActiveComputerName
Master switch for VBS (requires VBS for Credential Guard and HVCI). 1 = enabled; 0 = disabled. Setting 0 disables the foundation of CG + HVCI + other VBS features. Enterprise baseline: 1.

The DLL actually loaded for the CLSID. The GUID → DLL resolution goes via the COM class registration under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Classes\CLSID. A Credential Provider CLSID pointing to a DLL outside %SystemRoot%\System32 is high-suspicion.; DLL implementing the filter. Same resolution path as providers; same suspicion profile for non-system32 paths.
SID of the domain user whose credentials were cached
UPN form (user@domain.com) for modern domain accounts — email-form identity

Enforcement mode for a specific ASR rule: '0' = Disabled (not enforcing), '1' = Block (enforce and block), '2' = Audit (log but don't block), '6' = Warn (user prompt). Microsoft recommends Block mode for all ASR rules on hardened endpoints. Attacker turning '1' → '0' silently disables a specific rule — targeted defense-evasion. Commonly-targeted: LSASS credential-theft rule (9e6c4e1f-7d60-472f-ba1a-a39ef669e4b2), Office-macro-creates-child-process rule (D4F940AB-401B-4EFC-AADC-AD5F3C50688A), untrusted-USB rule (b2b3f03d-6a65-4f7b-a9c7-1c7ef74a9ba4).
Value NAME is a process executable name (e.g., 'mimikatz.exe'). Defender will not scan file activity initiated by that process. Classic evasion: exclude the attacker's dropper's process name.
1 = real-time protection disabled. Complementary tamper indicator — often set alongside exclusion additions. Blocked by Tamper Protection when enabled.

DNS namespace the rule applies to. Formats: '.corp.example.com' (suffix match), 'exact.example.com' (exact), '*.example.com' (wildcard), 'example.com' (exact without dot). Attacker-authored rule targeting a specific sensitive domain = redirect that domain's queries to attacker infrastructure while leaving other DNS normal.

DNS server(s) to route the matching namespace's queries to. Multiple servers allowed. An attacker IP here + sensitive namespace in Name = queries for sensitive domains go to attacker server → attacker sees internal DNS patterns + can serve malicious A records.

For rules using DNS-over-HTTPS: the DoH template URL (e.g., https://attacker.example/dns-query). Attacker-configured DoH target bypasses enterprise DNS telemetry AND enterprise TLS inspection (DoH goes over HTTPS to a single server — looks like normal HTTPS traffic).

Canonical format (Vista–11):
  _??_USBSTOR#Disk&Ven_<Vendor>&Prod_<Product>&Rev_<Revision>#<USBSTOR-Instance-ID>#{53f56307-b6bf-11d0-94f2-00a0c91efb8b}<VolumeLabel>_<VolumeSerialDecimal>
Four extractable identifiers in ONE subkey name:
  (1) USBSTOR instance id — the hardware serial (or OS-synthesized &0 suffix)
  (2) Class GUID {53f56307-...} — DiskDrive class, constant
  (3) VolumeLabel — shell-visible label at evaluation time
  (4) VolumeSerial — DECIMAL uint32 (NOT hex); convert to hex to join other artifacts
Parser: split on '#' then on final two '_' (VolumeLabel may contain embedded _).
The safest parse is to anchor on the trailing digits (VolumeSerial) and the class-GUID.

Canonical format (Vista–11):
  _??_USBSTOR#Disk&Ven_<Vendor>&Prod_<Product>&Rev_<Revision>#<USBSTOR-Instance-ID>#{53f56307-b6bf-11d0-94f2-00a0c91efb8b}<VolumeLabel>_<VolumeSerialDecimal>
Four extractable identifiers in ONE subkey name:
  (1) USBSTOR instance id — the hardware serial (or OS-synthesized &0 suffix)
  (2) Class GUID {53f56307-...} — DiskDrive class, constant
  (3) VolumeLabel — shell-visible label at evaluation time
