GroupPolicy-Registry-Pol: GPO-enforced registry settings are re-applied on every refresh (typically every 90 minutes + 30 min random offset).
WMI-CIM-Repository: Survives reboot; triggered by Winmgmt service start + whatever WQL trigger is embedded in the Filter.
EFI-System-Partition: Bootkit persistence executes before OS — reboot always re-triggers.

Security-4657: When the ObjectName + ObjectValueName combination matches a known persistence mechanism (Run key, Services subkey, AppInit_DLLs, IFEO Debugger, Winlogon Shell/Userinit, etc.), the CONFIGURED proposition directly satisfies PERSISTED's input.
Sysmon-6: Kernel driver loaded into the running kernel; persists until unload OR next reboot.
Active-Setup: Triggers on every user's logon where HKCU\Version < HKLM\Version — broad reach, admin-level plant, user-context execution.
AeDebug: Fires on any process crash — persistent until registry entry is cleared.
BCD-Store: Persists across reboots by definition — every reboot consults BCD.
CommandProcessor-AutoRun: Triggered on every cmd.exe launch — high-frequency.
Credential-Guard-State: CG state persists across reboot (and across UEFI power-cycle when UEFI-locked).
Defender-Exclusions: Exclusions "persist" the attacker's freedom to operate rather than their code itself.
DNS-NRPT: NRPT rules persist across reboot and are consulted on every DNS lookup.
ETW-Autologger: Autologger config persists across reboot — takes effect at next boot.
Intune-PolicyManager: MDM policy re-applies at every refresh cycle; cached value is enforced between refreshes.
LSA-Protection-RunAsPPL: Value is read at boot; persists across reboot until explicitly changed.
Netsh-Helpers: Trigger is every netsh.exe invocation — constant in normal Windows operation.
Port-Monitors: Trigger is Print Spooler service start — automatic on boot.
RegBack-Hives: RegBack preserves prior persistence configurations even after attacker cleanup — offline diff surfaces what was removed.
Registry-Transaction-Logs: Log-replayed hive recovers persistence configurations that attacker cleanup had removed from the primary hive.
RootCertificate-Store: Root certs persist until explicitly removed; no reboot or logon trigger needed (cert is consulted on every TLS / Authenticode validation).
Run-Keys: combined with EXISTS(entity=referenced exe on disk), Run-Keys closes PERSISTED(entity, system, via=run-key)
SChannel-TLS-Config: Schannel reads these values at TLS negotiation time — persistent effect across reboots.
Scheduled-Tasks: task-scheduler is a mature persistence class; all scheduled tasks are PERSISTED unless actively disabled
Screensaver-Hijack: Triggers on every user-idle period meeting the timeout — persistent within the interactive session.
Services: auto-start services (Start=2) are PERSISTED by definition; the CONFIGURED + PERSISTED composite captures both the presence and the activation trigger
Shell-COM-Hooks: Trigger is every interactive logon (explorer.exe start) — effectively a boot-persistent hook per user session.
SilentProcessExit-Monitor: Trigger is the target process's exit. For lsass target, fires on every shutdown.
Start-TrackProgs: Persists until the user toggles the Settings UI back on or registry is edited.
Time-Providers: Trigger is W32Time service start — automatic at boot.
Uninstall-Keys: Installed apps persist across reboot until explicitly uninstalled.
Windows-Firewall-Profiles: Profile state persists across reboot and is re-applied when MpsSvc starts.
WinSock2-LSP: LSP loads into every new process that links ws2_32.dll — persistence is implicit in every network app on the system.
WSL-Lxss: Distro survives reboot; uninstalling via wsl --unregister removes the registry entry + rootfs — look for orphan ext4.vhdx files if the user tried to cover up.
Hosts-File: Survives reboot; persists until manually edited.
PowerShell-Profile: Trigger is every PowerShell session start — high frequency on admin / developer workstations.
ScheduledTask-XML: Task fires on whatever trigger is embedded — boot, logon, time, event — until deleted or disabled.

convergences.name: PROTECTS_VOLUME
convergences.summary: Volume-protection configuration proposition — cryptographic or platform mechanisms in place to protect volume contents. Joins BitLocker configuration (BitLocker-FVE) with boot-integrity + platform-attestation state (BCD-Store) via VolumeGUID + RegistryKeyPath pivots.

join-chain.description:
Volume-identity pivot. BitLocker-FVE stores per-volume
key-protector GUIDs under FVE\<VolumeGUID>\. BCD-Store
binds boot-loader entries to specific volume GUIDs via
device objects. Joining on VolumeGUID resolves "is THIS
specific volume BitLocker-protected AND boot-anchored into
the chain the TPM attests?" — the full protection story:
encryption state + boot-integrity binding + TPM-release
policy. A BitLocker-protected volume with its boot entry
removed from BCD = offline-attack attempt (attacker wants
the encrypted data but can't boot the platform normally).

Configuration-anchor pivot. BitLocker-FVE config lives at
HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\FVE\ (policy overrides) and
HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\FVE (runtime state);
BCD-Store is HKLM\BCD00000000 (mounted) or \EFI\Microsoft\
Boot\BCD (file). Joining on RegistryKeyPath bridges policy
(GPO-pushed FVE settings) to runtime (current BitLocker
state) to boot (secure-boot + testsigning flags). Tamper
detection: policy says "require BitLocker" + runtime says
"not enabled" = policy non-compliance or removed via local
admin.

notes: BitLocker-FVE: FVE = Full Volume Encryption registry + per-volume key-protector GUIDs. Each key-protector GUID maps to a specific unlock path for the associated volume (TPM, TPM+PIN, recovery-key, etc.). Exit-node for volume-encryption-state evidence.

BCD-Store: Boot Configuration Data — boot-entry definitions + secure-boot state + test-signing flag. Pairs with BitLocker for the full trust-chain story (BitLocker requires boot integrity to release the VMK).

convergences.name: RAN_PROCESS
convergences.summary: Extracted from artifact extends-to rules. join-chain and exit-node require authorship.

join-chain.description:
Shared process-identity anchor across memory-state and snapshot
witnesses. Hiberfil's EPROCESS linked-list entries carry ImageFileName
(short) and the executable's FullPath via VAD traversal; Pagefile PE
carvings recover the binary's image-header name; Power-Efficiency-
Diagnostics lists image paths for running processes at report-
generation time; VSS-Shadow-Copies preserves prior Prefetch /
Amcache / UsnJrnl entries keyed on the same path. Path match is
moderate-strength (evadable via rundll32 / legitimate-binary-abuse /
image-name-spoofing) but no stronger pivot exists across these
disjoint subsystems. Temporal + path-agreement across 2+ witnesses
produces C3; a single witness is C2.

notes: Hiberfil: Processes enumerated from the EPROCESS list are direct evidence of execution at hibernate moment.
Pagefile: PE-signature carved payload is direct evidence the process ran in this system's memory.
VSS-Shadow-Copies: Prior Prefetch / Amcache / UsnJrnl entries surviving in snapshots corroborate execution evidence attacker-cleaned from the live state.
Power-Efficiency-Diagnostics: Processes listed in the report were confirmed running at report-generation time.

convergences.name: STAGED
convergences.summary: Content-staging proposition — user set aside content for subsequent movement (paste, sync, delete). Joins clipboard history (Windows-Clipboard) with deletion-staging (Recycle-Bin-INFO2) and cloud-sync pre-commit (OneDrive-SafeDelete) via UserSID + FILETIME pivots.

join-chain.description:
Actor-attribution pivot. Windows-Clipboard History lives
under %LOCALAPPDATA%\Microsoft\Windows\Clipboard — per-user.
Recycle-Bin-INFO2 records the deleting-user SID (each $I
file under $RECYCLE.BIN\<SID>\ is per-SID). OneDrive-
SafeDelete mirrors the per-user OneDrive staging pre-
commit to cloud. Joining on UserSID identifies which
account staged content — attribution grade for insider-
threat exfil-staging pattern detection: same user copies
sensitive data to clipboard + deletes source file + syncs
clipboard content to another channel.

Temporal-bracketing pivot. Windows-Clipboard entries carry
per-entry timestamps. Recycle-Bin-INFO2 $I files store
the deletion timestamp. OneDrive-SafeDelete tracks pre-
commit staging times. Joining on FILETIME reconstructs the
staging SEQUENCE: clipboard-copy at T1 + source delete at
T2 + cloud-sync at T3 within seconds = coordinated exfil
staging pattern. Time ordering distinguishes exfil-prep
staging from routine content rearrangement.

notes: Windows-Clipboard: Windows 10+ clipboard history store (Win+V). Cleartext or ciphertext content depending on app. Exit-node for clipboard-staging evidence — content IS in the store.

Recycle-Bin-INFO2: $I sidecar files under $RECYCLE.BIN\<SID>\. Records original path + deletion timestamp + file size. Exit-node for deletion-as-staging evidence (attacker deletes source after copying elsewhere).
OneDrive-SafeDelete: cloud-sync pre-commit staging. Records files marked-for-delete before cloud-side deletion fires. Useful for recovering briefly-staged-then-deleted evidence.

convergences.name: TRANSACTED_FILE_OPERATION
convergences.summary: Per-operation chronology of NTFS file actions via the MFT + $LogFile + $UsnJrnl triad (Cowen 2013 Triforce methodology).

yields.casey-rationale: Three independent subsystems record each NTFS file operation — the MFT record itself, the $LogFile transaction log, and the $UsnJrnl change journal. When all three agree on an operation (type + time + target record), the claim is C4 anti-tamper-hard because compromising one log does not affect the other two. Disagreement or single-witness observations drop to C3.

join-chain.description:
MFT entry reference ({record-number, sequence-number} pair) is the
globally-unique identifier for an NTFS file across the Triforce. MFT
records each file's current state keyed by this reference; $UsnJrnl
USN_RECORD entries include the FileReferenceNumber for every
operation; $LogFile transaction records name the target MFT entry
being mutated. Without this pivot, the three sources would report
operations on "some file" — with it, they report operations on THE
SAME file, enabling the three-witness agreement that produces C4.

Timestamp is the secondary pivot — each witness timestamps the same
operation independently. MFT $SI/$FN record kernel-written times
for the current file state; $UsnJrnl USN_RECORD carries TimeStamp
per operation; $LogFile records transaction completion times.
Agreement on operation time (within 100ns FILETIME resolution)
strengthens the Triforce claim; disagreement on time but agreement
on MFT reference is a timestomp indicator (MFT times rewritten,
journals not). Moderate strength because precision differs across
witnesses and timestamps are the easiest field to selectively tamper.

notes: MFT: provides the file's current record state + LogFileSequenceNumber (LSN) pointer into the $LogFile transaction chain (header offset 0x08). $SI/$FN timestamps give current time state; the LSN ties this record to its last transaction.

$LogFile ($LogFile metafile, MFT entry 2): transaction log of every filesystem operation at the redo/undo granularity. Records are structured as log records containing before-image + after-image data for the affected MFT record. Retention is typically hours to days on a busy volume; transaction records circle out as newer entries overwrite. When present, authoritative for 'what did this operation look like' per-transaction.

UsnJrnl ($UsnJrnl:\$J stream on NTFS): per-record change journal. Emits a USN_RECORD entry for every create / delete / rename / modify / security-change / ADS-operation. Reason flags (USN_REASON_*) enumerate the operation type. Retention is longer than $LogFile (weeks on typical volumes, months on less-active ones). Authoritative for 'what operations happened in order' per-record.

Triforce correlation (Cowen 2013 HECFBlog): when the MFT record's LSN chain, the $LogFile transaction record for that LSN, and the $UsnJrnl RecordNumber for the same operation all agree on operation-type + timestamp + target, the observation reaches C4. One tampered witness cannot move the conclusion; two must be compromised simultaneously (not a realistic attacker model on a live system).

Degradation: when $LogFile has rolled but $UsnJrnl retains the operation, claim drops to C3 — same witness-count as $MFT + $UsnJrnl agreement (two independent subsystems, but $MFT reflects only the CURRENT state, not the operation sequence). When only $UsnJrnl survives (attacker overwrote MFT timestamps, $LogFile rolled), claim is C2 — single-subsystem recollection.

convergences.name: USED
convergences.summary: Extracted from artifact extends-to rules. join-chain and exit-node require authorship.

join-chain.attribution-sentence: USBSTOR contains an entry for every USB device connected to the system, keyed on the device's instance ID which includes the vendor-assigned serial number — this serial is what threads the device identity across MountedDevices, EMDMgmt, WindowsPortableDevices, and PartitionDiagnostic-1006.

A LinkTargetIDList structure carries a VolumeID shell item that encodes the volume's drive type, serial number, and label — this is the cross-artifact binding the Shell Link (.LNK) format uses to identify the volume from which a referenced object was opened.
join-chain.description:
Volume-level pivot bridging device-identity to filesystem artifacts.
MountedDevices binds VolumeGUID to device-instance; MountPoints2
per-user entries key on VolumeGUID; AutomaticDestinations /
CustomDestinations / ShellBags / ShellLNK embed VolumeGUID (or its
FilesystemVolumeSerial derivative) in the shell-item chain /
LNK header. VolumeGUID is moderate-strength because it's
regenerated on volume reformat, but globally unique otherwise —
the pivot that ties "a user opened file X" (ACCESSED input) to
"device D was connected" (CONNECTED input).

join-chain.attribution-sentence: Each Windows user profile carries its own NTUSER.DAT registry hive whose path embeds the account's SID — artifacts rooted under HKCU (MountPoints2, ShellBags, AutomaticDestinations, CustomDestinations) therefore attribute their contents to a specific UserSID by filesystem location alone, independent of any in-hive field.
join-chain.description:
User-attribution pivot closing the USED claim. MountPoints2 lives
per-user in NTUSER (POSSESSED input proves THIS user recognized
the device); ShellBags / ShellLNK / AutomaticDestinations /
CustomDestinations are per-user artifacts. PartitionDiagnostic-1006
uniquely carries a user-sid field inline — CAN close USED without
MountPoints2 when the per-user registry is cleaned. Full
convergence: DeviceSerial identifies the device, VolumeGUID
identifies the volume presented, UserSID attributes the interaction
to a specific account. Missing any one → degradation path.

notes: PartitionDiagnostic-1006: uniquely, this artifact can close USED without needing MountPoints2 — the user-sid field is captured inline
ShellLNK: when the LNK's volume-label / volume-GUID matches a MountPoints2 entry under the same user, this closes USED(user, removable-device) via the file that was opened from it

EMDMgmt: EMDMgmt doesn't carry user SID itself (HKLM hive, not user-scoped). USED chain: EMDMgmt provides CONNECTED(device=D, time=T) → correlate with MountPoints2 under NTUSER.DAT for user U → derives USED(U, D). When USBSTOR has been cleaned but EMDMgmt survives, EMDMgmt IS the CONNECTED anchor for the chain.

MountPoints2: MountPoints2 supplies the POSSESSED input (device in user scope). CONNECTED comes from USBSTOR (primary) or MountedDevices (VolumeGUID join). When MountPoints2 has been cleaned per-user but SYSTEM-hive artifacts survived, USED collapses to session-window inference.
ShellBags: closing USED(user, removable-device) via ShellBags requires the volume identity in the shell-item chain to match a volume-GUID/serial tracked in MountPoints2

USBSTOR: POSSESSED input is per-user (NTUSER.DAT MountPoints2). When MountPoints2 has been cleaned, fall back to EMDMgmt (SOFTWARE hive — typically survives) for CONNECTED corroboration, then session-window-infer the user.

WindowsPortableDevices: WPD is HKLM-scoped (no user context). USED chain: WPD provides CONNECTED → MountPoints2 provides POSSESSED via NTUSER-profile ownership → USED derived. When USBSTOR is cleaned, WPD frequently becomes the primary CONNECTED anchor.

convergences.name: VIEWED
convergences.summary: Visual-content observation proposition — user saw / displayed / captured specific visual content. Joins thumbnail cache (Thumbcache-Entry), RDP visual tiles (RDP-Bitmap-Cache), and explicit screen captures (Snipping-Tool-Captures) via UserSID + TimeWindow + ExecutableHash pivots.

join-chain.description:
Actor-attribution pivot. RDP-Bitmap-Cache lives under
%LOCALAPPDATA%\Microsoft\Terminal Server Client\Cache —
per-user. Thumbcache-Entry is per-user at
%LOCALAPPDATA%\Microsoft\Windows\Explorer\thumbcache_*.db.
Snipping-Tool-Captures writes under %LOCALAPPDATA%\Packages\
Microsoft.ScreenSketch_8wekyb3d8bbwe\LocalState. Joining on
UserSID (inferred from the containing profile path) resolves
"this user saw / captured this visual content" — attribution-
grade for insider-threat + incident reconstruction involving
screenshotted sensitive content.

Temporal-bracketing pivot. RDP-Bitmap-Cache .bmc file mtimes
advance per-session; Thumbcache-Entry each cached thumbnail
carries a ModifiedTime at index-time (FILETIME). Snipping-
Tool-Captures writes MAC timestamps per PNG / clip file.
Joining on time brackets "what visual content was surfaced
around T" — cross-reference with Security-4624 session window
+ file-access audit events to corroborate user presence.

Content-identity pivot. Thumbcache-Entry keys on a 64-bit
hash of the source-file path; Snipping-Tool-Captures stores
PNG content (hashable); RDP-Bitmap-Cache stores 64x64 tile
content (tile-hash). Joining on content-hash ties a cached
thumbnail to a specific source file, or a snipping-tool PNG
to a specific remote-tile rendering. Useful for "attacker-
captured screenshot of THIS document" claims where content
hashes match known-sensitive material.

notes: RDP-Bitmap-Cache: per-session tile store — definitive record of what the user saw over RDP. Exit-node for remote-session visual evidence.

Thumbcache-Entry: Explorer-generated thumbnail cache — records thumbnails for every file-preview-capable file the user BROWSED (even without opening). Exit-node for "what files existed in the users view" inference; survives file deletion (thumbnail persists after source deleted).
Snipping-Tool-Captures: explicit user-driven screenshots. Exit-node for intentional capture evidence — the PNG IS the content.

convergences.name: VISITED_LOCATION
convergences.summary: Extracted from artifact extends-to rules. join-chain and exit-node require authorship.

join-chain.description:
Two very different location models force a weak pivot. NLA-Signatures-
Unmanaged binds a unique gateway MAC address to a network-profile
entry with DateCreated / DateLastConnected timestamps — the host
was physically close to THAT access point at that window.
Cortana-CoreDb reminder trigger-location entries carry a
geolocation (lat/lon / named-place) for user-designated geofences —
the user deliberately marked a location of interest. Neither system
shares a coordinate or identifier; both represent "place" at
different abstractions (network-local vs. semantic geography). Weak
pivot because matching requires human interpretation (e.g. "home
router MAC" ↔ "home" reminder). Strengthens forensically when
NLA timestamps bracket a Cortana reminder trigger — concurrent
evidence the user was at the named place when the reminder fired.

notes: Cortana-CoreDb: Reminder trigger-location entries are direct evidence the user designated / visited that geolocation.

NLA-Signatures-Unmanaged: A unique gateway MAC is effectively a physical-location anchor. A network-history entry places the host at the router's location at the time window bracketed by DateCreated / DateLastConnected.

matrix-dt020-shellbags-usb.apa: Insider Threat Matrix (n.d.). DT020 - Shellbags, USB Removable Storage. Insider Threat Matrix. https://insiderthreatmatrix.org/detections/DT020
matrix-dt020-shellbags-usb.author: Insider Threat Matrix
matrix-dt020-shellbags-usb.title: DT020 - Shellbags, USB Removable Storage
matrix-dt020-shellbags-usb.note: ITM DT020 dedicated. Documents both hive locations: NTUSER.DAT\Software\Microsoft\Windows\Shell\BagMRU (+ Bags) and UsrClass.dat\Local Settings\Software\Microsoft\Windows\Shell\BagMRU. USB removable-storage lens on ShellBags.

matrix-dt021-usbstor-registry-key.apa: Insider Threat Matrix (2024). DT021 - USBSTOR Registry Key. Insider Threat Matrix. https://insiderthreatmatrix.org/detections/DT021
matrix-dt021-usbstor-registry-key.author: Insider Threat Matrix
matrix-dt021-usbstor-registry-key.title: DT021 - USBSTOR Registry Key
matrix-dt021-usbstor-registry-key.note: Re-verified 2026-04-22. Page created 2024-05-31, updated 2025-10-23. Documents USBSTOR path (HKLM\\SYSTEM\\ControlSet001\\Enum\\USBSTOR) with timestamp / serial number / unique ID / container ID / friendly name / device name / make / model / type fields. Explicit cross- citations to sister detections DT022 USB-Enum, DT023 MountedDevices, DT024 DriverFrameworks-UserMode, DT025 Setupapi.dev.log, DT087 MountPoints2. MITRE ATT&CK DS0024 data-source alignment documented. Sitemap confirms 1327 total URLs; 152 detections + 86 preventions + chains across 5 articles (AR1-AR5). Strong per-artifact attribution source — the DT-number scheme means ITM publishes one page per detection artifact, so each DT is a focal source.

matrix-dt022-usb-registry-key.apa: Insider Threat Matrix (n.d.). DT022 - USB Registry Key. Insider Threat Matrix. https://insiderthreatmatrix.org/detections/DT022
matrix-dt022-usb-registry-key.author: Insider Threat Matrix
matrix-dt022-usb-registry-key.title: DT022 - USB Registry Key
matrix-dt022-usb-registry-key.note: ITM detection page dedicated to Enum\USB registry key. Covers VID/PID/hardware-ID, device-install timestamps (0064/0066/0067 property IDs), cross-refs DT021 (USBSTOR), DT023 (MountedDevices), DT024 (DriverFrameworks-UM), DT025 (setupapi.dev.log), DT087 (MountPoints2), DT020 (Shellbags-USB).

matrix-dt023-mounteddevices-registry-key.apa: Insider Threat Matrix (n.d.). DT023 - MountedDevices Registry Key. Insider Threat Matrix. https://insiderthreatmatrix.org/detections/DT023
matrix-dt023-mounteddevices-registry-key.author: Insider Threat Matrix
matrix-dt023-mounteddevices-registry-key.title: DT023 - MountedDevices Registry Key
matrix-dt023-mounteddevices-registry-key.note: ITM detection page dedicated to MountedDevices registry key. Documents registry path (HKLM\SYSTEM\MountedDevices), binding-data encoding, USB-chain correlation, and cross-references to DT021 (USBSTOR), DT022 (USB-Enum), DT024 (DriverFrameworks-UM), DT025 (setupapi.dev.log), DT087 (MountPoints2).

matrix-dt024-driverframeworks-um.title: DT024 - Windows Event Log, DriverFrameworks-UserMode
matrix-dt024-driverframeworks-um.note: ITM DT024 dedicated page for DriverFrameworks-UserMode/Operational event channel. Documents events 2003/2100/2102 (narrower than ArtifactExplorer's Kernel-PnP 2003/2004/2100/2101/2102/2105 set). Notably does NOT cross-ref DT021/023/025/087 (diverges from the heavy sibling cross-ref pattern of the other USB-family DT pages).

matrix-dt087-usb-mountpoints2.apa: Insider Threat Matrix (n.d.). DT087 - USB MountPoints2. Insider Threat Matrix. https://insiderthreatmatrix.org/detections/DT087
matrix-dt087-usb-mountpoints2.author: Insider Threat Matrix
matrix-dt087-usb-mountpoints2.title: DT087 - USB MountPoints2
matrix-dt087-usb-mountpoints2.note: ITM detection page dedicated to NTUSER MountPoints2. Documents the per-user USB mount-history registry key, subkey namespaces (CPC, \??\Volume{GUID}, drive-letter), BaseClass REG_SZ, _Autorun and Shell\AutoRun nested keys. Cross-refs ITM framework-nodes IF002.001/006, PR002.001/004.003, ME005.001, AF022.003 (distinct from DT021/023 which cross-ref sibling detections).

matrix-dt093-shimcache-mft-timestamp.title: DT093 — ShimCache / MFT timestamp correlation
matrix-dt093-shimcache-mft-timestamp.note: ITM dedicated detection-method page for ShimCache timestamp semantics. Published note contains a factual error (says 'last executed' — actually last-modified per Mandiant Parisi 2015). Registering with semantic caveat here so future references know to read with correction in mind.

ms-event-1102.apa: Microsoft. (2023). Event 1102 (S): The audit log was cleared. Microsoft Learn. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/security/threat-protection/auditing/event-1102
ms-event-1102.author: Microsoft
ms-event-1102.title: Event 1102 (S): The audit log was cleared
ms-event-1102.note: Provider is Microsoft-Windows-Eventlog (GUID fc65ddd8-d6ef-4962-83d5-6e5cfe9ce148), not Security-Auditing. Cannot be disabled by audit policy — writes unconditionally on Security-log clear. Uses UserData/LogFileCleared, not EventData.

ms-event-4624.apa: Microsoft. (2023). Event 4624 (S): An account was successfully logged on. Microsoft Learn. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/security/threat-protection/auditing/event-4624
ms-event-4624.author: Microsoft
ms-event-4624.title: Event 4624 (S): An account was successfully logged on
ms-event-4624.note: Authoritative field reference. Documents v0 (Vista / 2008), v1 (Win8 / 2012) adding ImpersonationLevel, and v2 (Win10) adding RestrictedAdminMode / VirtualAccount / ElevatedToken / TargetLinkedLogonId / TargetOutboundUserName / TargetOutboundDomainName.

ms-event-4625.apa: Microsoft. (2023). Event 4625 (F): An account failed to log on. Microsoft Learn. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/security/threat-protection/auditing/event-4625
ms-event-4625.author: Microsoft
ms-event-4625.title: Event 4625 (F): An account failed to log on
ms-event-4625.note: Authoritative field reference. Subcategories: Audit Account Lockout AND Audit Logon. Fires on the target system (not the DC for network logons unless the DC is itself the target). WorkstationName is client-asserted and spoofable.

ms-event-4634.apa: Microsoft. (2023). Event 4634 (S): An account was logged off. Microsoft Learn. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/security/threat-protection/auditing/event-4634
ms-event-4634.author: Microsoft
ms-event-4634.title: Event 4634 (S): An account was logged off
ms-event-4634.note: Authoritative field reference. Subcategory: Audit Logoff. Fires on all session ends (timeout, force-logoff, RDP disconnect). No Subject block — only Target. Network (type 3) sessions usually emit only 4634, no matching 4647.

ms-event-4647.apa: Microsoft. (2023). Event 4647 (S): User initiated logoff. Microsoft Learn. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/security/threat-protection/auditing/event-4647
ms-event-4647.author: Microsoft
ms-event-4647.title: Event 4647 (S): User initiated logoff
ms-event-4647.note: Authoritative field reference. Subcategory: Audit Logoff. No LogonType field (unlike 4634). Typical for Interactive / RemoteInteractive graceful logoff; a matching 4634 normally follows. Correlate to 4624 via TargetLogonId.

ms-event-4648.apa: Microsoft. (2023). Event 4648 (S): A logon was attempted using explicit credentials. Microsoft Learn. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/security/threat-protection/auditing/event-4648
ms-event-4648.author: Microsoft
ms-event-4648.title: Event 4648 (S): A logon was attempted using explicit credentials
ms-event-4648.note: Authoritative field reference. Subcategory: Audit Logon. Fires on runas, scheduled-task-with-stored-creds, net use /user, CreateProcessWithLogonW, and lateral-movement tooling. LogonGuid and TargetLogonGuid jointly fingerprint the 4648 → 4624 chain across hosts for Kerberos lateral-movement attribution.

ms-event-4656.apa: Microsoft. (2023). Event 4656 (S, F): A handle to an object was requested. Microsoft Learn. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/security/threat-protection/auditing/event-4656
ms-event-4656.author: Microsoft
ms-event-4656.title: Event 4656 (S, F): A handle to an object was requested
ms-event-4656.note: Authoritative field reference. Subcategories: Audit File System, Audit Kernel Object, Audit Registry, Audit Removable Storage. Fires on request (pair with 4663). Failure variant exists. v1 (Server 2012 / Win8) added ResourceAttributes and AccessReason.

ms-event-4657.apa: Microsoft. (2023). Event 4657 (S): A registry value was modified. Microsoft Learn. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/security/threat-protection/auditing/event-4657
ms-event-4657.author: Microsoft
ms-event-4657.title: Event 4657 (S): A registry value was modified
ms-event-4657.note: Authoritative field reference. Subcategory: Audit Registry. Fires on VALUE modification only — not key creation / deletion. Requires 'Set Value' auditing in the key's SACL. Single event version.

ms-event-4658.apa: Microsoft. (2023). Event 4658 (S): The handle to an object was closed. Microsoft Learn. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/security/threat-protection/auditing/event-4658
ms-event-4658.author: Microsoft
ms-event-4658.title: Event 4658 (S): The handle to an object was closed
ms-event-4658.note: Authoritative field reference. Subcategories: Audit File System, Audit Handle Manipulation, Audit Kernel Object, Audit Registry, Audit Removable Storage. No ObjectName — correlate HandleId back to the 4656 that opened it. MS notes 'little to no security relevance' standalone; value is in the lifetime-bound pair with 4656.

ms-event-4663.apa: Microsoft. (2023). Event 4663 (S): An attempt was made to access an object. Microsoft Learn. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/security/threat-protection/auditing/event-4663
ms-event-4663.author: Microsoft
ms-event-4663.title: Event 4663 (S): An attempt was made to access an object
ms-event-4663.note: Authoritative field reference. Subcategories: Audit File System, Audit Kernel Object, Audit Registry, Audit Removable Storage. Success-only — no Failure variant (unlike 4656). Indicates a right was exercised vs merely requested. v1 added ResourceAttributes.

ms-event-4672.apa: Microsoft. (2023). Event 4672 (S): Special privileges assigned to new logon. Microsoft Learn. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/security/threat-protection/auditing/event-4672
ms-event-4672.author: Microsoft
ms-event-4672.title: Event 4672 (S): Special privileges assigned to new logon
ms-event-4672.note: Authoritative field reference. Subcategory: Audit Special Logon. Fires when one of 13 sensitive privileges is in the new token: SeTcb, SeBackup, SeCreateToken, SeDebug, SeEnableDelegation, SeAudit, SeImpersonate, SeLoadDriver, SeSecurity, SeSystemEnvironment, SeAssignPrimaryToken, SeRestore, SeTakeOwnership. Every SYSTEM logon triggers it — expect high volume.

ms-event-4688.apa: Microsoft. (2023). Event 4688 (S): A new process has been created. Microsoft Learn. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/security/threat-protection/auditing/event-4688
ms-event-4688.author: Microsoft
ms-event-4688.title: Event 4688 (S): A new process has been created
ms-event-4688.note: Authoritative field reference. v1 added CommandLine (gated by separate GPO); v2 added Target subject block, ParentProcessName, and MandatoryLabel (integrity level).

ms-event-4697.apa: Microsoft. (2023). Event 4697 (S): A service was installed in the system. Microsoft Learn. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/security/threat-protection/auditing/event-4697
ms-event-4697.author: Microsoft
ms-event-4697.title: Event 4697 (S): A service was installed in the system
ms-event-4697.note: Authoritative field reference. Subcategory: Audit Security System Extension. MINIMUM OS Windows 10 / Server 2016 — unlike most peers which reach back to Vista. ImagePath captured at creation only — later changes are not logged. ServiceAccount only populates for Win32 Own/Share Process types; blank for kernel drivers (implies LocalSystem).

ms-event-4698.apa: Microsoft. (2023). Event 4698 (S): A scheduled task was created. Microsoft Learn. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/security/threat-protection/auditing/event-4698
ms-event-4698.author: Microsoft
ms-event-4698.title: Event 4698 (S): A scheduled task was created
ms-event-4698.note: Authoritative field reference. Subcategory: Audit Other Object Access Events — off by default. v1 (Win10 1903+) added ClientProcessStartKey / ClientProcessId / ParentProcessId / RpcCallClientLocality / FQDN — the invoker-attribution fields critical for remote-schtasks abuse detection. Doc labels 'Event Versions: 0' inconsistently but field additions are real.

ms-event-4699.apa: Microsoft. (2022). Security-4699 — A scheduled task was deleted. Microsoft Learn. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/security/threat-protection/auditing/event-4699
ms-event-4699.author: Microsoft
ms-event-4699.title: Security-4699 — A scheduled task was deleted
ms-event-4699.note: Authoritative Microsoft reference. Documents the full set of EventData fields including TaskContent.

ms-event-4720.apa: Microsoft. (2023). Event 4720 (S): A user account was created. Microsoft Learn. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/security/threat-protection/auditing/event-4720
ms-event-4720.author: Microsoft
ms-event-4720.title: Event 4720 (S): A user account was created
ms-event-4720.note: Authoritative field reference. Subcategory: Audit User Account Management. Fires on DCs, member servers, AND workstations (local accounts). NewUacValue uses SAM [MS-SAMR] USER_ACCOUNT flags, NOT the AD userAccountControl schema — they differ bit-for-bit. SidHistory populated only on cross-domain migration; non-empty at local creation = injection attempt.

ms-event-5140.apa: Microsoft. (2023). Event 5140 (S, F): A network share object was accessed. Microsoft Learn. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/security/threat-protection/auditing/event-5140
ms-event-5140.author: Microsoft
ms-event-5140.title: Event 5140 (S, F): A network share object was accessed
ms-event-5140.note: Authoritative field reference. Subcategory: Audit File Share. Fires ONCE per session on first access, NOT per file operation — use 4663/5145 for per-file. AccessMask is hardcoded to 0x1 and AccessList to ReadData (%%4416) regardless of actual operation. Both Success and Failure variants.

recon-2022-hibernation-recon-convert-hib.note: Primary commercial tool for hiberfil.sys parsing / decompression across Windows versions. Handles the format differences between pre-Win8 and Win8+ images.

regripper-plugins.apa: Carvey, H. (2024). RegRipper plugin catalog — per-key Windows Registry analysis plugins. GitHub (keydet89). https://github.com/keydet89/RegRipper3.0/tree/master/plugins
regripper-plugins.author: Carvey, H.
regripper-plugins.title: RegRipper plugin catalog — per-key Windows Registry analysis plugins
regripper-plugins.note: Re-verified 2026-04-22. Plugin catalog confirmed live. Directly verified usbstor.pl exists at /plugins/usbstor.pl (Author: H. Carvey, Version 20200515, target: ControlSet00X\\Enum\\USBStor, extracts FirstInstallDate / InstallDate / LastArrivalDate / LastRemovalDate, FriendlyName, ParentIdPrefix, serial number). amcache.pl and usbdevices.pl also confirmed via direct URL. ~500 plugins total; each should be considered an individual leaf source. The /plugins/ tree URL cited here is the umbrella — appropriate as an index-catalog entry. Per-plugin citations would be richer attribution but add registry volume.

repnz-etw-providers-docs-kernel-pnp-mani.apa: repnz (GitHub). (n.d.). ETW Providers Docs: Microsoft-Windows-Kernel-PnP manifest. github.com/repnz/etw-providers-docs. https://github.com/repnz/etw-providers-docs/blob/master/Manifests-Win10-17134/Microsoft-Windows-Kernel-PnP.xml
repnz-etw-providers-docs-kernel-pnp-mani.author: repnz (GitHub)
repnz-etw-providers-docs-kernel-pnp-mani.title: ETW Providers Docs: Microsoft-Windows-Kernel-PnP manifest
repnz-etw-providers-docs-kernel-pnp-mani.note: Authoritative ETW manifest dump for Kernel-PnP provider {9c205a39-1250-487d-abd7-e831c6290539}. Documents event 400/410/411/430/441/442 field structures. MS Learn has no equivalent published manifest.

research-2023-blackbasta-lockbit-use-of-.apa: BlackBerry Threat Research. (2023). BlackBasta / LockBit use of AnyDesk — forensic breakdown. blogs.blackberry.com. https://blogs.blackberry.com/en/category/research-and-intelligence
research-2023-blackbasta-lockbit-use-of-.author: BlackBerry Threat Research
research-2023-blackbasta-lockbit-use-of-.title: BlackBasta / LockBit use of AnyDesk — forensic breakdown
research-2023-blackbasta-lockbit-use-of-.note: Practitioner case studies documenting connection_trace.txt content recovery in ransomware IR. Includes AD IDs known to be reused across campaigns.

rfc-1002-netbios.apa: NetBIOS Working Group. (1987). Protocol Standard for a NetBIOS Service on a TCP/UDP Transport: Detailed Specifications (RFC 1002). IETF. https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc1002
rfc-1002-netbios.author: NetBIOS Working Group
rfc-1002-netbios.title: Protocol Standard for a NetBIOS Service on a TCP/UDP Transport: Detailed Specifications, RFC 1002
rfc-1002-netbios.note: IETF Internet Standard defining the NetBIOS naming service over TCP/UDP — the spec underwriting the MachineNetBIOS join key (16-character host identifier persisted in registry, event-log SourceHostName, and SMB session events). Pairs with RFC 1001 (concepts and methods).

rfc-1035-domain-names.apa: Mockapetris, P.. (1987). RFC 1035 — Domain Names: Implementation and Specification. IETF. https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc1035
rfc-1035-domain-names.author: Mockapetris, P.
rfc-1035-domain-names.title: RFC 1035 — Domain Names: Implementation and Specification
rfc-1035-domain-names.note: Core DNS protocol spec. Defines domain-name wire format, label structure, and resource-record types underpinning every Windows DNS-cache artifact, DNS query event (ETW / Sysmon-22), and URL host component.

rfc-3986-uri-generic-syntax.apa: Berners-Lee, T., Fielding, R., & Masinter, L.. (2005). RFC 3986 — Uniform Resource Identifier (URI): Generic Syntax. IETF. https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc3986
rfc-3986-uri-generic-syntax.author: Berners-Lee, T., Fielding, R., & Masinter, L.
rfc-3986-uri-generic-syntax.title: RFC 3986 — Uniform Resource Identifier (URI): Generic Syntax
rfc-3986-uri-generic-syntax.note: Upstream authoritative specification of URI syntax. Underpins URL parsing across every Windows artifact carrying URL content (browser history, BITS, email, registry, prefetch, etc.). Supersedes RFC 2396.

rfc-4291-ipv6-addressing-architecture.apa: Hinden, R. & Deering, S.. (2006). RFC 4291 — IP Version 6 Addressing Architecture. IETF. https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc4291
rfc-4291-ipv6-addressing-architecture.author: Hinden, R. & Deering, S.
rfc-4291-ipv6-addressing-architecture.title: RFC 4291 — IP Version 6 Addressing Architecture
rfc-4291-ipv6-addressing-architecture.note: IPv6 address format + scope rules. Relevant wherever Windows records IPv6 endpoints — Sysmon-3, firewall channel, ETW network providers. Forensic parsers must handle compact (::) notation plus zone-id (fe80::1%eth0).

rfc-5322-internet-message-format.apa: Resnick, P.. (2008). RFC 5322 — Internet Message Format. IETF. https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc5322
rfc-5322-internet-message-format.author: Resnick, P.
rfc-5322-internet-message-format.title: RFC 5322 — Internet Message Format
rfc-5322-internet-message-format.note: Authoritative format for email messages. §3.4 defines addr-spec (local-part '@' domain) — the canonical EmailAddress grammar. Governs From/To/Cc/Bcc header parsing and the Received: chain structure across mail clients and servers.

rfc-5891-idn-in-applications.apa: Klensin, J.. (2010). RFC 5891 — Internationalized Domain Names in Applications (IDNA): Protocol. IETF. https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc5891
rfc-5891-idn-in-applications.author: Klensin, J.
rfc-5891-idn-in-applications.title: RFC 5891 — Internationalized Domain Names in Applications (IDNA): Protocol
rfc-5891-idn-in-applications.note: IDN / Punycode (xn--) encoding for non-ASCII domain labels. Phishing-defense relevance: homoglyph domains encoded as Punycode appear in Windows artifacts with visually-different unicode when rendered. Forensic parsers must reversibly convert xn-- labels.

rfc-6531-smtputf8-internationalized-emai.apa: Yao, J. & Mao, W.. (2012). RFC 6531 — SMTP Extension for Internationalized Email. IETF. https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc6531
rfc-6531-smtputf8-internationalized-emai.author: Yao, J. & Mao, W.
rfc-6531-smtputf8-internationalized-emai.title: RFC 6531 — SMTP Extension for Internationalized Email
rfc-6531-smtputf8-internationalized-emai.note: Extends RFC 5321/5322 for UTF-8 local-parts and domains. Relevant for EmailAddress in internationalized deployments — without it, forensic tooling may fail to decode non-ASCII addresses or treat them as malformed.

rfc-791-ipv4.apa: Postel, J.. (1981). RFC 791 — Internet Protocol (IPv4). IETF. https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc791
rfc-791-ipv4.author: Postel, J.
rfc-791-ipv4.title: RFC 791 — Internet Protocol (IPv4)
rfc-791-ipv4.note: Foundational IPv4 spec. Defines 32-bit address format (dotted-decimal a.b.c.d) that appears in firewall logs, network event captures, ETW TCP/UDP, registry (NetworkList profiles), DHCP cache, etc.

robbins-2022-group-policy-preferences-an.apa: Metcalf, S. (2022). Finding GPP Passwords and Exploiting Group Policy Preferences. SpecterOps. https://adsecurity.org/?p=2288
robbins-2022-group-policy-preferences-an.author: Metcalf, S.
robbins-2022-group-policy-preferences-an.title: Finding GPP Passwords and Exploiting Group Policy Preferences
robbins-2022-group-policy-preferences-an.note: Practitioner research on current-day GPP abuse patterns including ScheduledTasks / Shortcuts / Registry XML for mass-persistence deployment. Post-ransomware-era writeup.

sans-2022-sysmon-event-7-dll-side-loadin.apa: SANS. (2022). Sysmon Event 7 — DLL Side-Loading Hunting. SANS Institute. https://www.sans.org/blog/sysmon-7-dll-sideloading-hunting/
sans-2022-sysmon-event-7-dll-side-loadin.author: SANS
sans-2022-sysmon-event-7-dll-side-loadin.title: Sysmon Event 7 — DLL Side-Loading Hunting
sans-2022-sysmon-event-7-dll-side-loadin.note: SANS deep-dive on using Event 7 OriginalFileName mismatches and path anomalies to surface DLL-side-load campaigns. Covers evasion via reflective loading.

sans-2022-the-importance-of-sysmon-event.apa: SANS. (2022). The Importance of Sysmon Event ID 11. SANS Institute. https://www.sans.org/blog/the-importance-of-sysmon-event-id-11/
sans-2022-the-importance-of-sysmon-event.author: SANS
sans-2022-the-importance-of-sysmon-event.title: The Importance of Sysmon Event ID 11
sans-2022-the-importance-of-sysmon-event.note: Practitioner deep-dive on FileCreate. Discusses pairing with Event 2 (FileCreateTime change) for timestomp detection since Event 11 alone misses in-place ransomware encryption.

sans-lo-2014-shellbag-forensics-in-depth.apa: Lo, V. (2014). Windows ShellBag Forensics in Depth. SANS Institute. https://www.sans.org/white-papers/34545
sans-lo-2014-shellbag-forensics-in-depth.author: Lo, V.
sans-lo-2014-shellbag-forensics-in-depth.title: Windows ShellBag Forensics in Depth
sans-lo-2014-shellbag-forensics-in-depth.note: SANS/GIAC white paper dedicated to ShellBags. Tier-1 academic authority for this artifact (parallel role to Hedley-2024 for USBSTOR).

sans-tilbury-2011-windows-7-shellbags.apa: Tilbury, C. (2011). Computer Forensic Artifacts - Windows 7 Shellbags. SANS DFIR Blog. https://www.sans.org/blog/computer-forensic-artifacts-windows-7-shellbags
sans-tilbury-2011-windows-7-shellbags.author: Tilbury, C.
sans-tilbury-2011-windows-7-shellbags.title: Computer Forensic Artifacts - Windows 7 Shellbags
sans-tilbury-2011-windows-7-shellbags.note: Foundational writeup of Win7 ShellBags structural changes. Authoritative secondary tier.

schroeder-2016-get-gpppassword-powershel.apa: PowerSploit (Will Schroeder). (2016). Get-GPPPassword — PowerShell one-liner for domain-wide SYSVOL cpassword sweep. github.com. https://github.com/PowerShellMafia/PowerSploit
schroeder-2016-get-gpppassword-powershel.author: PowerSploit (Will Schroeder)
schroeder-2016-get-gpppassword-powershel.title: Get-GPPPassword — PowerShell one-liner for domain-wide SYSVOL cpassword sweep
schroeder-2016-get-gpppassword-powershel.note: Canonical practitioner tool for the SYSVOL XML enumeration + cpassword decrypt. Runs as any authenticated domain user. If this tool returns results, the domain has standing credential exposure.

securelist-2024-userassist-ir-value.apa: Kaspersky / Securelist. (2024). UserAssist: IR value and decoding deep-dive. Securelist. https://securelist.com/
securelist-2024-userassist-ir-value.author: Kaspersky / Securelist
securelist-2024-userassist-ir-value.title: UserAssist: IR value and decoding deep-dive
securelist-2024-userassist-ir-value.note: Most complete 72-byte layout documentation. Documents 2-day cumulative-focus-time session-reset rule and 5 execution-method completeness patterns.

singh-2017-cortana-forensics-windows-10.apa: Singh, B. & Singh, U.. (2017). A forensic insight into Windows 10 Cortana search. Computers & Security, 66, 142-154. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cose.2017.01.007
singh-2017-cortana-forensics-windows-10.author: Singh, B. & Singh, U.
singh-2017-cortana-forensics-windows-10.title: A forensic insight into Windows 10 Cortana search
singh-2017-cortana-forensics-windows-10.note: Peer-reviewed DOI:10.1016/j.cose.2017.01.007. Documents CortanaCoreDb.dat tables (Reminders, Geofences, Locations, Triggers), ships CortanaDigger Python parser, reconstructs user location timelines — matches Cortana-CoreDb.md field schema 1:1. Replaces retracted khatri-2019-cortana-forensics-coredb-and-i (ghost).

socinv-event-5379-malicious-zip.apa: SOC Investigation. (n.d.). Security-5379 malicious ZIP detection EQL. SOC Investigation. https://www.socinvestigation.com/
socinv-event-5379-malicious-zip.author: SOC Investigation
socinv-event-5379-malicious-zip.title: Security-5379 malicious ZIP detection EQL
socinv-event-5379-malicious-zip.note: SOC Investigation EQL rule for the ZipFolder TargetName pattern on event 5379 — typical exfil-via-zip-container detection.

specterops-2019-a-deep-dive-into-dpapi-c.apa: Harmj0y (SpecterOps). (2019). A Deep Dive Into DPAPI — comprehensive reverse-engineering. SpecterOps. https://posts.specterops.io/a-deep-dive-into-dpapi-1a48b0d26dec
specterops-2019-a-deep-dive-into-dpapi-c.author: Harmj0y (SpecterOps)
specterops-2019-a-deep-dive-into-dpapi-c.title: A Deep Dive Into DPAPI — comprehensive reverse-engineering
specterops-2019-a-deep-dive-into-dpapi-c.note: Definitive practitioner deep-dive covering master-key structure, domain backup key, and decryption workflows end-to-end. The single most complete DPAPI reference.

specterops-2019-sharpdpapi-c-implementat.apa: Harmj0y (SpecterOps). (2019). SharpDPAPI — C# implementation of credential + vault extraction. github.com. https://github.com/GhostPack/SharpDPAPI
specterops-2019-sharpdpapi-c-implementat.author: Harmj0y (SpecterOps)
specterops-2019-sharpdpapi-c-implementat.title: SharpDPAPI — C# implementation of credential + vault extraction
specterops-2019-sharpdpapi-c-implementat.note: Open-source practitioner tool. Extracts and decrypts credentials / vault / certificates given the required key material. Operational-security-friendly C# implementation.

specterops-2021-understanding-and-defend.apa: SpecterOps. (2021). Understanding and defending against access-token theft. SpecterOps. https://posts.specterops.io/understanding-and-defending-against-access-token-theft-finding-alternatives-to-winlogon-exe-80696c8a73b
specterops-2021-understanding-and-defend.author: SpecterOps
specterops-2021-understanding-and-defend.title: Understanding and defending against access-token theft
specterops-2021-understanding-and-defend.note: Covers the access-mask interpretation behind credential-access detection via Event 10. Particularly useful for distinguishing token-duplication from memory-read attack chains.

splunk-2026-runmru-registry-deletion-det.apa: Splunk ES. (2026). RunMRU Registry Key Deletion. Splunk Enterprise Security. https://research.splunk.com/endpoint/e651795f-xxxx/
splunk-2026-runmru-registry-deletion-det.author: Splunk ES
splunk-2026-runmru-registry-deletion-det.title: RunMRU Registry Key Deletion
splunk-2026-runmru-registry-deletion-det.note: Splunk ES analytic detecting RunMRU key deletion via Sysmon EID 12. Maps to T1112.

splunk-security-4742.apa: Splunk Research. (2024). Security-4742 — 5 detections (T1134.005, T1207, T1210). Splunk Research. https://research.splunk.com/sources/ea830adf-5450-489a-bcdc-fb8d2cbe674c/
splunk-security-4742.author: Splunk Research
splunk-security-4742.title: Security-4742 — 5 detections (T1134.005, T1207, T1210)
splunk-security-4742.note: Splunk ES production detection catalog for Security-4742. Maps to T1134.005 (SID-history injection), T1207 (rogue DC), T1210 (exploitation of remote services).

sqlite-org-fileformat.apa: SQLite Consortium. (2024). SQLite File Format Specification. sqlite.org. https://www.sqlite.org/fileformat.html
sqlite-org-fileformat.author: SQLite Consortium
sqlite-org-fileformat.title: SQLite File Format Specification
sqlite-org-fileformat.note: Authoritative on-disk format spec for SQLite databases. Page structure, B-tree cells, WAL, journal, varint encoding, record format, sqlite_schema. Substrate anchor for windows-sqlite (~15+ artifacts: Chrome-History, Chrome-Cookies, Edge-*, Firefox-*, ActivitiesCache, Notifications-wpndatabase, Cortana-CoreDb, etc). Parallel to how libyal-libregf anchors windows-registry-hive.

stig-2023-windows-10-11-security-technic.apa: CIS / DISA STIG (2023). Windows 10 / 11 Security Technical Implementation Guide — Schannel baseline. public.cyber.mil. https://www.stigviewer.com/stigs/microsoft-windows-11-security-technical-implementation-guide
stig-2023-windows-10-11-security-technic.author: CIS / DISA STIG
stig-2023-windows-10-11-security-technic.title: Windows 10 / 11 Security Technical Implementation Guide — Schannel baseline
stig-2023-windows-10-11-security-technic.note: Authoritative hardening baselines defining the expected Schannel configuration. DFIR reference point for detecting drift from enterprise security posture.

suhanov-2019-windows-registry-forensics-.apa: Maxim Suhanov. (2019). Windows registry forensics — parsing transaction logs. github.com. https://github.com/msuhanov/regf
suhanov-2019-windows-registry-forensics-.author: Maxim Suhanov
suhanov-2019-windows-registry-forensics-.title: Windows registry forensics — parsing transaction logs
suhanov-2019-windows-registry-forensics-.note: Canonical open-source implementation of hive + log replay. Python libregf provides read/replay APIs. Maxim's research is the DFIR reference for the transaction-log format internals.

suhanov-2020-dfir-ru-bam-internals.apa: Suhanov, M.. (2020). BAM internals. dfir.ru. https://dfir.ru/2020/04/08/bam-internals/
suhanov-2020-dfir-ru-bam-internals.author: Suhanov, M.
suhanov-2020-dfir-ru-bam-internals.title: BAM internals
suhanov-2020-dfir-ru-bam-internals.note: Authoritative analyst-writeup on BAM value structure. Documents byte-layout including moderation-state DWORD at offset 16-19 (0-2 throttling state).

synacktiv-2023-ntdissector.apa: Synacktiv. (2023). ntdissector — post-2016 NTDS.dit parser with AES support. Synacktiv. https://www.synacktiv.com/publications/ntdissector
synacktiv-2023-ntdissector.author: Synacktiv
synacktiv-2023-ntdissector.title: ntdissector — post-2016 NTDS.dit parser with AES support
synacktiv-2023-ntdissector.note: Synacktiv's NTDS.dit parser covering modern AES-encrypted Windows Server 2016+ ntds.dit format. Backs the 'AES-mode' claim in NTDS-dit encryption notes.

synacktiv-2023-pca-parsing-and-cross-com.apa: Blanche Lagny / Synacktiv. (2023). PCA — parsing and cross-comparison against ShimCache / Amcache. synacktiv.com. https://www.synacktiv.com/publications
synacktiv-2023-pca-parsing-and-cross-com.author: Blanche Lagny / Synacktiv
synacktiv-2023-pca-parsing-and-cross-com.title: PCA — parsing and cross-comparison against ShimCache / Amcache
synacktiv-2023-pca-parsing-and-cross-com.note: Complementary research with deeper format breakdown and parser implementation. Addresses edge cases the format-dev evolved across Win11 feature updates.

thedfirreport.apa: The DFIR Report (2024). The DFIR Report — intrusion case studies and TTPs. The DFIR Report. https://thedfirreport.com/
thedfirreport.author: The DFIR Report
thedfirreport.title: The DFIR Report — intrusion case studies and TTPs
thedfirreport.note: 85+ intrusion case studies with detailed reconstructions. Each post covers one real-world incident end-to-end: initial access, execution, persistence, lateral movement, exfiltration, impact. Tagged by ransomware family / attack vector / malware. Canonical methodology-level DFIR source for scenario attribution.

thedfirspot-nd-lateral-movement-rdp-arti.apa: The DFIR Spot. (n.d.). Lateral Movement: Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) Artifacts. thedfirspot.com. https://www.thedfirspot.com/post/lateral-movement-remote-desktop-protocol-rdp-artifacts
thedfirspot-nd-lateral-movement-rdp-arti.author: The DFIR Spot
thedfirspot-nd-lateral-movement-rdp-arti.title: Lateral Movement: Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) Artifacts
thedfirspot-nd-lateral-movement-rdp-arti.note: Anchors RELAY verdict — 'existence of these keys does not indicate the connection was successful.' User-intent artifact, not connection-fact.

thehacker-recipes-mimikatz-lsadump-cache.apa: The Hacker Recipes. (n.d.). mimikatz lsadump::cache — MSCASH v1/v2 extraction. thehacker.recipes. https://www.thehacker.recipes/ad/movement/credentials/dumping/sam-and-lsa-secrets
thehacker-recipes-mimikatz-lsadump-cache.author: The Hacker Recipes
thehacker-recipes-mimikatz-lsadump-cache.title: mimikatz lsadump::cache — MSCASH v1/v2 extraction
thehacker-recipes-mimikatz-lsadump-cache.note: Documents NL$KM syskey unwrap flow → MSCASH v1/v2 hash recovery. Fills gap left by gentilkiwi wiki stub

trustedsec-2021-sysmon-event-22-dns-quer.apa: TrustedSec. (2021). Sysmon Event 22 DNS Query Analysis. trustedsec.com. https://www.trustedsec.com/blog/sysmon-event-22-dns-query-analysis/
trustedsec-2021-sysmon-event-22-dns-quer.author: TrustedSec
trustedsec-2021-sysmon-event-22-dns-quer.title: Sysmon Event 22 DNS Query Analysis
trustedsec-2021-sysmon-event-22-dns-quer.note: Practitioner deep-dive. Covers QueryResults field parsing, CNAME-chain reconstruction, and how to correlate Event 22 with Event 3 to distinguish 'DNS-queried but never connected' (reconnaissance) from 'queried and connected' (active C2).

trustedsec-2022-sysinternals-sysmon-a-sw.apa: TrustedSec. (2022). Sysinternals Sysmon — a Swiss army knife for detection and IR. trustedsec.com. https://www.trustedsec.com/blog/sysinternals-sysmon-a-swiss-army-knife-for-detection-and-incident-response/
trustedsec-2022-sysinternals-sysmon-a-sw.author: TrustedSec
trustedsec-2022-sysinternals-sysmon-a-sw.title: Sysinternals Sysmon — a Swiss army knife for detection and IR
trustedsec-2022-sysinternals-sysmon-a-sw.note: Practitioner walkthrough of Sysmon's network-connection visibility including its blind spots (processes that terminate before sampling, loopback exclusion patterns).

trustedsec-sysmon-community-guide.author: TrustedSec
trustedsec-sysmon-community-guide.title: Sysmon Community Guide
trustedsec-sysmon-community-guide.note: Community-maintained Sysmon configuration + detection guide. Covers Sysmon-1/3/11/13/22 detection patterns at depth. Tier-3 tooling reference.

ultimatewindowssec-interactive-logon-cac.author: Ultimate Windows Security
ultimatewindowssec-interactive-logon-cac.title: Interactive Logon — cached logons count policy
ultimatewindowssec-interactive-logon-cac.note: Confirms CachedLogonsCount default=10, range=0-50. Replacement for the ms-cached-credentials-cachedlogonscoun 404'd MS Learn URL.

Documents 3 subkey namespaces (CPC/Volume-GUID/drive-letter), _Autorun + Shell\AutoRun nested keys, BaseClass REG_SZ. Artifact-scope peer to substrate-scope libyal-libregf (same pattern as winreg-kb-mounted-devices for MountedDevices).
